issues.claim: Lucene (or Elasticsearch in AEMaaCS) indexes support full-text and property filtering
issues.problem: AEMaaCS uses Oak's native Lucene-based indexes — not Elasticsearch. Adobe never replaced Oak's search layer with Elasticsearch for AEMaaCS.
issues.fix: Lucene indexes support full-text and property filtering, while Property indexes are optimal for narrow equality lookups — the same index types apply in both AEM 6.5 and AEMaaCS.

issues.claim: Lucene indexes cover full-text and property filtering while Property indexes are best for narrow equality lookups
issues.problem: The fs incorrectly implies Elasticsearch replaces Lucene in AEMaaCS (inherited from the card body). While the fs itself does not repeat 'Elasticsearch', the card body it belongs to does, making the overall card instruction wrong for AEMaaCS context.

issues.claim: A Dispatcher flush agent is a replication agent configured on the Publish instance (not Author) that sends an HTTP FLUSH request to the Dispatcher's /invalidate endpoint when content is replicated.
issues.problem: In standard AEM 6.5 on-prem topology the Dispatcher flush agent is configured on the Author instance (at /etc/replication/agents.author). The Author activates pages and then sends the cache-invalidation signal to the Dispatcher. A Publish-side flush agent is an atypical secondary pattern, not the canonical configuration.
issues.fix: A Dispatcher flush agent is a replication agent configured on the Author instance (at /etc/replication/agents.author) that sends an HTTP FLUSH request to the Dispatcher's /invalidate endpoint immediately after content is activated.

issues.claim: The flush agent belongs on Publish, not Author
issues.problem: Inverts the standard topology. Adobe documentation and the default AEM 6.5 out-of-the-box configuration place the flush agent on Author, not Publish. The race condition described in the card (flush fires before content is live on Publish) also only makes logical sense for an Author-side flush agent.
issues.fix: The flush agent belongs on Author, not Publish

issues.claim: The flush agent goes on the Author instance, and the Dispatcher deletes cached files when it receives a flush.
issues.problem: The first half of the fj ('flush agent goes on the Author instance') is actually correct standard practice. Presenting it as the weak/wrong answer is a polarity error. Only the second half ('Dispatcher deletes cached files') is genuinely wrong (it touches a .stat file, not deletes).
issues.fix: Split the fj: the Dispatcher-deletes claim is the genuinely wrong part. The Author-placement claim should be moved to the correct answer.

issues.claim: For custom cache invalidation rules (e.g., invalidating related pages), you use ACS Commons Dispatcher Flush Rules as OSGi configurations deployed via code, which listen for replication events and send HTTP purge requests to configured CDN endpoints.
issues.problem: ACS Commons is a community open-source library not certified or recommended by Adobe for AEMaaCS CDN purge. AEMaaCS has its own built-in CDN purge mechanism through the Sling Content Distribution pipeline and native cache invalidation APIs. Recommending ACS Commons as the mechanism for AEMaaCS presents a community workaround as the canonical solution.
issues.fix: For custom cache invalidation rules in AEMaaCS, implement an OSGi ReplicationEventListener or use the AEMaaCS CDN Purge API (via an HTTP call to the CDN configuration endpoint) to send targeted purge requests — AEMaaCS does not require ACS Commons for this pattern.

issues.claim: Configure org.apache.sling.security.impl.CORSPolicyImpl with alloworigins=[https://myangularapp.com]
issues.problem: The correct OSGi PID for AEM's CORS configuration is com.adobe.granite.cors.impl.CORSPolicyImpl (from the Granite CORS bundle), not org.apache.sling.security.impl.CORSPolicyImpl. The org.apache.sling.security package handles the Sling Referrer Filter, not CORS.
issues.fix: Configure com.adobe.granite.cors.impl.CORSPolicyImpl with alloworigin=[https://myangularapp.com]

issues.claim: AEM 6.5 enters end-of-extended-support in 2025, meaning no security patches
issues.problem: Adobe's published End of Extended Support date for AEM 6.5 is November 2026, not 2025. Using 2025 overstates urgency and could cause incorrect migration timelines in a business case.
issues.fix: AEM 6.5 reaches end-of-extended-support in November 2026, meaning no further security patches after that date.

aem.note: Mostly solid content with good depth, but has a significant structural error: the Dispatcher flush agent placement (card 7) is inverted throughout — the fj states the correct answer as wrong, corrupting the polarity of the most-tested AEM ops question. Also contains a wrong OSGi PID for CORS, an incorrect Elasticsearch claim for AEMaaCS, and a wrong EOS year for AEM 6.5.

issues.claim: uses 'conversations' for state rather than the Assistants API's 'threads and messages'
issues.problem: The Foundry Agent Service REST API still uses threads, messages, and runs as its primary primitives — the same object model as the Assistants API. 'Conversations' is portal/narrative language, not an API primitive that replaced threads.
issues.fix: Remove the 'conversations' claim; the Agent Service preserves the thread/run model. The key architectural change is that the underlying inference calls use the Responses API, not that state objects were renamed.

issues.claim: threads become conversations, runs become responses, and run-steps become items
issues.problem: The Foundry Agent Service API retains threads and runs as first-class API objects; they are not renamed to 'conversations' and 'responses'. This migration mapping would send a developer to look for non-existent API endpoints.
issues.fix: The correct migration mapping is: assistants → agents (same concept, new service endpoint), threads → threads, runs → runs. The Responses API is the new inference backbone but the Agent Service surface still exposes threads/runs.

issues.claim: It unifies the lineage of Semantic Kernel (enterprise integration, plugins) and AutoGen (multi-agent orchestration) into one supported framework.
issues.problem: Semantic Kernel and AutoGen (AutoGen v0.4 / AG2) remain separate, independently versioned SDKs. Microsoft has not released a single 'Microsoft Agent Framework' binary that formally merged both codebases. They share design influence but are not one unified framework.

issues.claim: quantizedFlat (memory-resident, good for up to roughly 1 M vectors, exact recall by default)
issues.problem: quantizedFlat uses scalar quantization (INT8 compression) which is lossy; it does NOT provide exact recall over the original float32 vectors. The `flat` index type (no quantization) provides exact recall. quantizedFlat trades accuracy for memory efficiency and is approximate.
issues.fix: Replace 'exact recall by default' with 'approximate recall (SQ8 quantization reduces accuracy slightly vs. the non-quantized flat index)'.

issues.claim: quantizedFlat = memory-resident exact ANN up to ~1M vectors
issues.problem: Same error as in the answer: quantizedFlat is not exact; it is approximate due to scalar quantization. The fs summary repeats the inaccuracy.
issues.fix: quantizedFlat = memory-resident approximate (SQ8-quantized) index up to ~1M vectors

issues.claim: from azureml.core.model import Model  # available in scoring env
issues.problem: This is an SDK v1 import that is never used in the code (AZUREML_MODEL_DIR is used directly instead). Including an unused v1 import with a 'available in scoring env' comment in a v2 managed online endpoint example misleads readers into thinking the import is needed or idiomatic.
issues.fix: Remove the `from azureml.core.model import Model` import line entirely; it is dead code in this SDK v2 example.

issues.claim: Senior teams use Prompt Flow for deterministic, auditable pipelines (e.g., grounded Q&A with citable sources)
issues.problem: Card 11 in this same file states that Microsoft ended Prompt Flow feature development on April 20, 2026 and plans full retirement on April 20, 2027. Recommending 'senior teams use Prompt Flow' for new production builds without that caveat is directly contradicted by the file's own retirement card.
issues.fix: Add caveat: 'Prompt Flow is being retired (dev ended Apr 2026, EOL Apr 2027); for new deterministic pipelines target the Agent Framework or Azure Functions-based orchestration instead.'

issues.claim: A production RAG pipeline in Foundry's prompt flow typically looks like: (1) query rewriting node...
issues.problem: This card presents Prompt Flow as the current recommended way to build production RAG pipelines, but as of June 2026 Prompt Flow feature development has ended (April 2026) and retirement is April 2027. The answer should reflect that this is a legacy pattern, not the recommended new-build path.
issues.fix: Add a note that Prompt Flow is in deprecation (dev ended Apr 2026) and new production RAG pipelines should target the Agent Framework or Agent Service instead.

issues.claim: Azure OpenAI fine-tuning (GA for GPT-4o-mini, preview for GPT-4o as of early 2026)
issues.problem: GPT-4o fine-tuning was announced as generally available in October 2024, not still in preview as of early 2026. This claim is roughly 15 months out of date.
issues.fix: Update to: 'GA for both GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o (GPT-4o fine-tuning reached GA in October 2024)'.

azure-ai.note: Mostly solid coverage of Azure AI platform as of 2026 with accurate deprecation dates; main issues are the Foundry Agent Service threads-vs-conversations API mismatch, the Agent Framework lineage claim, quantizedFlat described as exact (it is approximate), a dead SDK v1 import in a code sample, and two cards that recommend Prompt Flow for new production builds without the retirement caveat established in card 11.

issues.claim: Nova Multimodal Embeddings handles text, images, video, and audio in one model with 256/384/1,024/3,072 options via Matryoshka representation learning
issues.problem: Amazon Nova Multimodal Embeddings supports 256, 512, and 1024 dimensions per AWS documentation — 384 and 3,072 are not documented dimension options for this model. The internal followup answer only lists 256/384/1024 (dropping 3,072), showing inconsistency that points to fabricated values.

issues.claim: meaning you can truncate a single embedding to 256/384/1024 and still get a usable vector
issues.problem: The followup lists 256/384/1024 while the main answer lists 256/384/1024/3072 — the two are inconsistent, which itself signals fabricated dimension values. If 512 is the correct second tier (not 384), both answers are wrong.

issues.claim: current Claude models like Sonnet 4.5/Opus require about 4,096 tokens per checkpoint (older 3.7 Sonnet needed 1,024)
issues.problem: No AWS or Anthropic documentation establishes a 4,096-token minimum for Claude 4.x prompt caching checkpoints on Bedrock. Anthropic's documented minimum for Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet has been 1,024 tokens. The claim inverts the expected direction (newer model requiring 4x more tokens to qualify) with no published basis.

issues.claim: The 1-hour TTL is only supported on newer Claude models (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus 4.5)
issues.problem: 'Opus 4.5' is not a real Anthropic model name — Claude 4 family uses Opus 4 and Sonnet 4, not a '4.5' variant. The 1-hour cache TTL on the Anthropic API was introduced with Claude 3.7 Sonnet (not restricted to a '4.5' tier), so the model list is both mis-named and likely inaccurate.

issues.claim: u.get('cacheReadInputTokens')
issues.problem: The Bedrock Converse API usage object uses 'cacheReadInputTokenCount' (not 'cacheReadInputTokens'). Using the wrong key silently returns None rather than the cached-token count, making the logging code a no-op for the cache metric.
issues.fix: u.get('cacheReadInputTokenCount')

bedrock.note: Overall factually solid — the main conceptual content (RAG, guardrails, agents, PT vs on-demand, cross-region profiles) is accurate; the five issues cluster around Nova Multimodal Embeddings dimension values, Claude 4.x prompt-caching minimums, and one boto3 field name in sample code.

issues.claim: SELECT AI_COMPLETE('claude-4-sonnet',
issues.problem: claude-4-sonnet is not a valid Snowflake Cortex model ID; Anthropic/Snowflake naming for this model is claude-sonnet-4 (adjective-noun-version order), not noun-version-adjective.
issues.fix: SELECT AI_COMPLETE('claude-sonnet-4',

issues.claim: st.experimental_rerun or a manual refresh button to poll
issues.problem: st.experimental_rerun was deprecated and removed; the GA replacement is st.rerun() (Streamlit ≥ 1.28). The same card's followup correctly identifies this, making the main answer self-contradictory.
issues.fix: st.rerun() or a manual refresh button to poll

issues.claim: resp = session.sql(f"SELECT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.ANALYST_CHAT({window!r})").collect()[0][0]
issues.problem: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.ANALYST_CHAT does not exist as a SQL function. Cortex Analyst is invoked exclusively via the REST API endpoint /api/v2/cortex/analyst/message; it cannot be called with a SQL SELECT statement.
issues.fix: Use the snowflake.core.cortex.analyst REST client or a direct HTTP call to the Analyst API endpoint instead of a SQL SELECT.

issues.claim: import boto3, uuid\n    boto3.client('s3').put_object(Bucket='my-audio-bucket',Key=key_path,Body=audio)
issues.problem: The UDF calls boto3.client('s3') without any AWS credentials configured, and the External Access Integration only allows api.cartesia.ai:443 — S3 endpoints are not in the allowlist. Both issues would cause the UDF to fail at runtime.

issues.claim: Wrap with `<say-as interpret-as='ordinal'>` or `<say-as interpret-as='unit'>` for the percentage
issues.problem: The SSML 'ordinal' interpret-as produces ordinal readings (first, second, ninety-ninth), not cardinal numbers. Applied to 99.7, it would yield 'ninety-ninth', not 'ninety-nine point seven'. The correct interpret-as for a numeric percentage is 'cardinal', not 'ordinal'.
issues.fix: Use `<say-as interpret-as='cardinal'>99.7</say-as> percent` or simply pre-format the text as prose ('ninety-nine point seven percent') before passing to ElevenLabs.

cortex.note: Mostly sound Snowflake Cortex and TTS content; five substantive errors span a fabricated SQL function (ANALYST_CHAT), a wrong model ID format (claude-4-sonnet), a deprecated Streamlit API promoted in the main answer, a broken UDF code sample (boto3 without credentials or S3 network rule), and incorrect SSML ordinal vs cardinal usage for percentages.

issues.claim: ai_classify, ai_extract, ai_analyze_sentiment, and ai_translate wrap common tasks with tuned prompts
issues.problem: ai_analyze_sentiment is not a real Databricks built-in AI function. The actual task-specific functions are ai_classify, ai_extract, ai_fix_grammar, ai_gen, ai_mask, ai_similarity, ai_summarize, and ai_translate.
issues.fix: ai_classify, ai_extract, ai_summarize, and ai_translate wrap common tasks with tuned prompts

issues.claim: On Databricks the keyword index is learned over your data and hybrid is available with no extra setup.
issues.problem: BM25-style keyword (full-text) search is a statistical inverted-index algorithm, not a learned/trained model. Saying the keyword index is 'learned' implies it uses ML training, which is incorrect.
issues.fix: On Databricks the keyword index is built from your data (an inverted index) and hybrid is available with no extra setup.

issues.claim: Row filters are defined as Delta table properties via `ALTER TABLE ... SET ROW FILTER`
issues.problem: Row filters are Unity Catalog security constructs, not Delta table properties. Delta table properties are set via TBLPROPERTIES. Calling them 'Delta table properties' conflates two distinct concepts and would mislead a candidate who knows the difference.
issues.fix: Row filters are defined as Unity Catalog security policies via `ALTER TABLE ... SET ROW FILTER`, pointing to a SQL function that takes table column values and returns a boolean.

issues.claim: Use both: dashboards for the 20% long-tail, Genie for the rest.
issues.problem: This is the opposite of what the answer body says. The answer correctly states 'a dashboard for the 20% of repeated questions and a Genie space for the long tail.' Dashboards cover the common/repeated 20%, and Genie handles the long-tail ad-hoc questions — not the reverse.
issues.fix: Use both: dashboards for the 20% repeated common questions, Genie for the long tail.

issues.claim: AVG(g.latency_ms)   AS p50_latency
issues.problem: AVG computes the arithmetic mean, not the 50th percentile (median). p50 = median. Using AVG as a proxy for p50 is incorrect and would be flagged immediately in a real cost/latency review.
issues.fix: PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY g.latency_ms) AS p50_latency

mosaic.note: Mostly solid Mosaic AI content; 5 confirmed errors: one invented AI function name, one statistical-vs-learned confusion, one wrong categorical label for row filters, one inverted Pareto claim in the fs summary, and one mean-vs-median mislabel in SQL code.

issues.claim: pair it with `require_incremental=True`, which forces the run to fail rather than silently snapshot when incrementality is unavailable
issues.problem: require_incremental is not a documented parameter of the @incremental decorator; the documented parameters are semantic_version, snapshot_inputs, strict_append, and v2_semantics — this appears to be a hallucinated API name.

issues.claim: Functions on Objects are TypeScript (or Python) functions that operate over Ontology objects and links at query time
issues.problem: Foundry Functions on Objects (Ontology Functions) are TypeScript-only; Python is the language for batch transforms, not for Ontology-level Functions. The parenthetical '(or Python)' is incorrect and conflates transform authoring with Function authoring.
issues.fix: Functions on Objects are TypeScript functions that operate over Ontology objects and links at query time

issues.claim: Ontology functions are TypeScript or Python that execute at query time over live objects and links
issues.problem: Same error as the main answer: Foundry Ontology Functions are TypeScript-only; repeating 'or Python' in the strong-answer framing teaches the wrong language.
issues.fix: Ontology functions are TypeScript that executes at query time over live objects and links

issues.claim: The TypeScript and Python OSDK clients expose a semanticSearch method on object type handles.
issues.problem: The primary and fully-documented OSDK is TypeScript; the Python OSDK (palantir-sdk) has a different and more limited surface, and semanticSearch support on the Python client is not publicly documented as of 2025/2026. The code example in the card only shows TypeScript, contradicting the 'and Python' text.

palantir.note: Mostly accurate Foundry/AIP content; core transaction-type, incremental, marking, Workshop, and governance cards are solid. Main risks are a hallucinated @incremental parameter name (require_incremental=True) and an incorrect language claim for Ontology Functions (TypeScript-only, not Python).

issues.claim: On the TargetEndpoint Response flow, add a ResponseCache policy for idempotent GET-style lookups to avoid redundant Ultra Task invocations.
issues.problem: A ResponseCache policy placed only on the TargetEndpoint Response flow populates the cache but never reads it — the cache lookup must also be wired into the ProxyEndpoint Request flow (or TargetEndpoint Request PreFlow) with the same policy name; without the request-flow instance, every request still hits the SnapLogic Ultra Task, making the cache useless.
issues.fix: Add the ResponseCache policy to two flows: the ProxyEndpoint Request flow (cache lookup — if hit, skip SnapLogic entirely) and the TargetEndpoint Response flow (cache populate). Both references must use the same policy name so Apigee treats them as one logical cache entry.

issues.claim: Apigee Message Processors are memory-intensive (1–2 GB heap per pod)
issues.problem: Apigee hybrid documentation recommends a minimum of 4–8 GB RAM per Message Processor pod for production workloads; the 1–2 GB figure is roughly 4–8x below actual minimum requirements and would lead to severe GC pressure and OOM crashes in any real traffic scenario.
issues.fix: Apigee hybrid Message Processors require a minimum of approximately 4 GB RAM (and typically 6–8 GB for production traffic); the followup card's figure of '4 GB RAM per pod under moderate traffic' is closer to reality.

issues.claim: Apigee Key Value Maps are encrypted key-value stores scoped to either the organization, an environment, or a proxy
issues.problem: Proxy-scoped KVMs exist only in Apigee Edge (legacy); Apigee X does not support proxy-scoped KVMs — only organization-scoped and environment-scoped KVMs are available. Mentioning proxy-scope as a current option is misleading for anyone using Apigee X.
issues.fix: Apigee Key Value Maps are scoped to either the organization or an environment. Proxy-scoped KVMs were available in Apigee Edge but are not supported in Apigee X.

issues.claim: Apigee returns a 401 with an `invalid_token` fault code
issues.problem: When Apigee's VerifyJWT policy encounters an expired token, the fault name raised is 'TokenExpired' (not 'invalid_token'). The 'invalid_token' error code is RFC 6750 / OAuthV2 VerifyAccessToken language. The same answer then correctly instructs the reader to write a FaultRule on fault.name == 'TokenExpired', making the preceding 'invalid_token fault code' statement internally contradictory and wrong for VerifyJWT.
issues.fix: When VerifyJWT detects an expired token, Apigee raises a fault with fault.name = 'TokenExpired'. Define a FaultRule on that fault name to return a 401 with a structured error body; the 401 status is not automatic — the FaultRule is required.

issues.claim: the classic `verify-api-key` policy name changed
issues.problem: The VerifyAPIKey policy type name is identical in both Apigee Edge and Apigee X — it is 'VerifyAPIKey' in both products. No rename occurred during the migration. Telling candidates to look for a 'policy name change' as a migration concern will send them on a non-existent task.
issues.fix: The VerifyAPIKey policy type name did not change between Apigee Edge and Apigee X. Migration concerns are primarily around deprecated JavaScript APIs, changed MessageLogging behavior, and removed legacy Apigee Edge-only extensions — not a policy type rename.

issues.claim: Salesforce REST API v30 deprecations in 2024
issues.problem: Salesforce retired API versions 21.0–30.0 in Summer 2022 / Summer 2023 — not in 2024. Using 2024 as the reference year misrepresents when this disruption occurred, which matters for timeline-based interview answers about real incidents.

snaplogic.note: Mostly solid SnapLogic content; the five confirmed issues are concentrated in the Apigee integration section — one high-severity ResponseCache omission, two medium-severity Apigee X behavioral errors (KVM scopes, VerifyJWT fault code), one MP sizing underestimate, and one policy-name-change fabrication.

issues.claim: DynamoDB-based locking is now deprecated
issues.problem: As of Terraform 1.10, use_lockfile = true was introduced as a new native S3 locking option, but the dynamodb_table attribute is still fully supported and is NOT marked deprecated in the Terraform docs or changelog. Calling it 'deprecated' is factually incorrect.
issues.fix: The older approach used a DynamoDB table referenced by dynamodb_table; use_lockfile = true is a newer native alternative that avoids needing a separate DynamoDB table, but DynamoDB-based locking remains fully supported.

issues.claim: DynamoDB locking is deprecated
issues.problem: Repeats the same false deprecation claim in the strong-answer summary.
issues.fix: Use S3 native use_lockfile locking for new setups; DynamoDB locking is still supported but requires a separate table. force-unlock is a last resort with the lock ID.

issues.claim:
role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111122223333"
    role_arn_suffix = ":role/terraform-provisioner"
issues.problem: role_arn_suffix is not a valid attribute in the AWS provider assume_role block. The full role ARN must be provided as a single string in role_arn. The code splits a valid ARN into two fake attributes, which would cause a provider schema validation error.
issues.fix: role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/terraform-provisioner" (single attribute, full ARN string; remove role_arn_suffix entirely)

terraform.note: Overall solid track with accurate coverage of Terraform state, workspaces, lifecycle, and OpenTofu; two definite errors found: DynamoDB locking wrongly called deprecated (card 1), and a fabricated role_arn_suffix attribute in the cross-account provider code example (card 43).

issues.claim: the default (e.g. 8 for Flash) is a tuned starting point you should usually leave alone first
issues.problem: Official Vertex AI documentation examples use adapter_size=4, not 8, and Google explicitly recommends leaving adapter_size unset to let the platform auto-select; there is no documented default of 8 for Flash.
issues.fix: Larger ranks fit more complex tasks; the documented example value is 4. Google recommends leaving adapter_size unset (auto-selection) as the starting point rather than specifying a rank manually.

issues.claim: configurable content filters across four harm categories (hate speech, harassment, sexually explicit, dangerous content) where you set block thresholds based on probability and severity (NEGLIGIBLE/LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH)
issues.problem: For Gemini 2.5 Flash and later (the current default models), the configurable safety filter thresholds default to OFF, meaning no content is blocked by the configurable filters unless thresholds are explicitly set; the answer implies there are active default thresholds.
issues.fix: Add that Gemini 2.5 and later models default the configurable filters to OFF, so applications relying on safety blocking must explicitly set thresholds — there is no active default blocking from the configurable layer on current models.

issues.claim: the batch size limit is 100 entities per request
issues.problem: No hard documented API limit of exactly 100 entities per FetchFeatureValues request was found; 100 is a practical guideline derived from Bigtable row filter size constraints (~20 KB limit), not a stated hard quota in the Feature Store API documentation.

vertex-ai.note: Track is broadly accurate and well-researched; three targeted issues found — one wrong specific number in SFT defaults, one misleading omission about Gemini 2.5 safety filter OFF defaults, and one unverified hard limit on Feature Store batch entity fetch size.

issues.claim: Route order matters when bundles could match multiple route filters — the first matching route will process the bundle, and if that route does not have exclusive filter logic, the same bundle can also flow down subsequent routes.
issues.problem: Fusion's Router is pure fan-out: every route whose filter passes receives a clone of the bundle — there is no 'first matching route processes' behavior. The card's own fs field and followup[0] both correctly state 'Fusion processes all routes whose filters pass', making this sentence internally contradictory and factually wrong.
issues.fix: Remove the 'first matching route' framing entirely. The correct statement is: every route whose filter condition passes receives a copy of the bundle; route order only affects processing sequence, not which routes receive the bundle.

issues.claim: configure automatic dashboard delivery (PDF or Excel) on a weekly schedule using the Kickstart export feature
issues.problem: Kickstart is Workfront's bulk data import/export tool for system administrators (an Excel-based schema migration utility), not a dashboard scheduling feature. Scheduled dashboard or report delivery is a separate capability accessed via Report/Dashboard Actions > Send Report.
issues.fix: Replace 'using the Kickstart export feature' with 'using the Dashboard or Report's Send/Schedule Delivery feature (accessible via Report Actions > Send Report)'.

issues.claim: JWT (JSON Web Token) authentication is used with Adobe IMS (Identity Management System) for integrations that are part of the Adobe Experience Cloud ecosystem — you exchange a signed JWT for an IMS access token, which is then used to call Workfront's API. Choose JWT when the integration runs within Adobe infrastructure or must federate identity across Adobe products.
issues.problem: Adobe formally deprecated the Service Account (JWT) credential type in Adobe Developer Console in May 2023 with end-of-life on January 1, 2025. Recommending JWT as a current option is outdated by 2026; the replacement is the OAuth Server-to-Server credential, which uses the client_credentials grant and requires no JWT signing.
issues.fix: Replace the JWT section with: 'OAuth Server-to-Server (IMS) is the current Adobe-recommended method for Adobe Experience Cloud integrations, replacing the deprecated Service Account (JWT) credential. You register a credential in the Adobe Developer Console using the OAuth Server-to-Server type and exchange client credentials for a 24-hour access token.'

issues.claim: OAuth 2.0 (Authorization Code or Client Credentials grant) is the recommended choice for server-to-server integrations: you register an OAuth 2.0 application in Workfront, receive a client ID and secret, and exchange them for a short-lived access token (default 1-hour TTL) with a refresh token for silent renewal.
issues.problem: The Client Credentials grant does not issue a refresh token per RFC 6749 Section 4.4 — only the Authorization Code grant issues refresh tokens. Stating that Client Credentials provides 'a refresh token for silent renewal' is wrong; with Client Credentials you simply request a new access token when the current one expires.
issues.fix: Qualify the refresh token claim to Authorization Code grant only: '...Authorization Code grant, which issues a refresh token for silent renewal. The Client Credentials grant issues only an access token — renewal requires a fresh token request using the client secret.'

issues.claim: exchange them for a short-lived access token (default 1-hour TTL)
issues.problem: Card 24 explicitly states Adobe IMS OAuth access tokens are valid 24 hours by default, and card 24 followup[0] reaffirms '24 hours.' Card 28 claims '1-hour TTL' for what appears to be a different context (Workfront-native OAuth vs. IMS OAuth), but the distinction is never stated, leaving the reader with two conflicting numbers for what looks like the same credential type. If card 28 intends Workfront-native OAuth, the distinction from IMS OAuth must be made explicit.
issues.fix: Clarify: 'Workfront-native OAuth 2.0 (registered within Workfront itself) issues access tokens with a default 1-hour TTL. This is distinct from Adobe IMS OAuth Server-to-Server tokens, which expire after 24 hours.'

workfront.note: Five confirmed issues: one wrong Router fan-out description (card 5), one Kickstart misidentification (card 25), and three authentication errors on card 28 (deprecated JWT, Client Credentials/refresh-token spec violation, ambiguous 1h vs 24h token TTL). Fusion mechanics and Workfront PMO/reporting cards are otherwise sound.

issues.claim: payload ≤ 25 MB, processing ≤ 60 s
issues.problem: The SageMaker InvokeEndpoint API enforces a 6 MB maximum request payload for real-time inference, not 25 MB. The 25 MB figure does not correspond to any SageMaker real-time endpoint limit and will cause exam candidates to recall the wrong quota.
issues.fix: payload ≤ 6 MB, processing ≤ 60 s

issues.claim: AWS organises responsible AI around six pillars. Fairness … Bias … Inclusivity … Robustness … Safety … Veracity
issues.problem: The question explicitly asks for the six pillars 'as defined in the AWS AI Practitioner exam guide (AIF-C01)'. The AIF-C01 exam guide and AWS Responsible AI documentation list: Fairness, Explainability, Privacy and Security, Robustness, Governance, and Transparency. 'Bias' is a problem category, not a pillar; 'Inclusivity' and 'Veracity' are not named pillars in any AWS exam-guide version. An exam candidate who memorises this card will answer incorrectly on the actual test.
issues.fix: Replace the six pillars with the exam-guide-canonical list: Fairness, Explainability, Privacy and Security, Robustness, Governance, Transparency — and align each explanation to the AWS exam guide wording.

issues.claim: AWS research shows cloud workloads on AWS can reduce associated carbon footprint by up to 99%
issues.problem: The AWS-commissioned 451 Research study ('The Carbon Reduction Potential of Cloud Computing') states workloads can be up to 96% less carbon-intensive versus surveyed on-premises infrastructure. The 99% figure overstates the published claim.
issues.fix: Replace '99%' with '96%', consistent with the 451 Research commissioned study AWS cites.

cert-aws-ai-practitioner.note: Three substantive factual errors found: a wrong SageMaker real-time payload limit (25 MB vs 6 MB), entirely non-canonical responsible-AI pillar names for the AIF-C01 exam, and an overstated carbon-reduction figure (99% vs 96%). All other cards are technically sound.

issues.claim: G.025X for Python Shell
issues.problem: Python Shell jobs do not use a G.025X worker type. Python Shell uses the --MaxCapacity parameter accepting 0.0625 DPU or 1 DPU; G.1X, G.2X, G.4X, G.8X are Spark ETL/Streaming worker types, and G.025X does not exist as an AWS Glue worker type.
issues.fix: For Python Shell, specify --MaxCapacity 0.0625 (1/16 DPU) or --MaxCapacity 1 (1 DPU); G.x worker types apply only to Spark ETL and Streaming jobs.

issues.claim: idle DPUs are billed in 1-second increments after the first 10 minutes
issues.problem: Glue 3.0 and later (the current default) removed the 10-minute billing minimum; billing is now per second with a 1-minute minimum. The '10 minutes' floor applied only to legacy Glue 0.9/1.0/2.0 job runs and is no longer accurate for Glue 3.0/4.0.
issues.fix: idle DPUs are billed in 1-second increments after the first minute (Glue 3.0+).

issues.claim: For Redshift, enable `EnhancedVPCRouting`, and monitor `CPUUtilization`, `DatabaseConnections`, `ReadLatency`, and `QueuedQueries` metrics
issues.problem: EnhancedVPCRouting is a network routing feature that forces COPY/UNLOAD traffic through the VPC instead of the internet — it has no relationship to CloudWatch monitoring. Placing it in a list of monitoring enablement steps conflates a security/networking control with observability configuration.
issues.fix: Remove EnhancedVPCRouting from the monitoring list; the correct monitoring enablement for Redshift is enabling Enhanced Monitoring (via the console/API) and the Redshift Advisor, not EnhancedVPCRouting.

issues.claim: CloudTrail logs every management-plane API call (S3 GetObject, Glue CreateJob, Redshift DeleteCluster, IAM AttachRolePolicy)
issues.problem: S3 GetObject is a data-plane (data event), not a management-plane event. CloudTrail does NOT log S3 GetObject by default; data events must be explicitly enabled per trail or event data store at additional cost. Including GetObject alongside management events without this caveat implies it is logged by default, which is wrong.
issues.fix: Replace 'S3 GetObject' in the example list with a genuine management event such as 'S3 CreateBucket', and add a note that S3 data events (GetObject, PutObject) require explicit trail configuration to capture.

issues.claim: for JDBC sources, they use a bookmark key—by default the primary key—which must be strictly monotonically increasing (no gaps)
issues.problem: AWS Glue documentation requires the bookmark key to be monotonically increasing, but does not require the sequence to be gap-free. Integer primary keys routinely have gaps from deleted rows or non-sequential inserts, and bookmarks function correctly as long as new rows have higher key values than previously processed ones. 'No gaps' adds a constraint not present in the AWS docs.
issues.fix: Remove '(no gaps)' — the requirement is that the key be monotonically increasing (each new row's value exceeds the last bookmarked value), not that values are contiguous.

issues.claim: VACUUM table_name in Athena
issues.problem: Athena's Iceberg VACUUM syntax accepts RETAIN in DAYS (e.g., VACUUM db.orders RETAIN 7 DAYS), not HOURS. The accompanying code block uses 'RETAIN 168 HOURS', which is not valid Athena VACUUM syntax and would produce a parse error.
issues.fix: Use 'VACUUM db.orders RETAIN 7 DAYS;' — Athena Iceberg VACUUM accepts only DAYS as the retention unit.

cert-aws-data-engineer-associate.note: Six issues found across 20 cards; two high-confidence factual errors in Card 1 (non-existent G.025X worker type, stale 10-minute billing floor), one monitoring/networking conflation in Card 12, one data-vs-management-event error in Card 13, and two medium-confidence issues (JDBC bookmark gap constraint, VACUUM HOURS unit).

issues.claim: Global CRIS profiles offer the widest capacity pool (all commercial regions worldwide) and approximately 10 percent cost savings versus geographic profiles
issues.problem: AWS Cross-Region Inference has no 'Global' profile type; system-defined profiles are geographic (us., eu., ap. prefixes only). There is also no documented cost-savings differential between profile types — CRIS pricing is on-demand and cost reductions come from batch inference or Provisioned Throughput, not from choosing a broader CRIS scope.
issues.fix: Remove the 'Global CRIS' concept entirely. Describe the three geographic profile families (us., eu., ap.) and note that CRIS benefit is increased throughput headroom and reduced per-region throttling risk, not a cost discount versus geographic profiles.

issues.problem: The Bedrock Runtime Converse API `system` parameter only accepts `{text: string}` or `{guardContent: {...}}` blocks; `promptArn` is not a valid field in the system list. When using a Prompt Management version, the correct pattern is to pass the prompt version ARN as `modelId` (and supply `promptVariables` for template substitution), not embed the ARN in the `system` block alongside a separate model ARN.
issues.fix: Replace the code with: `bedrock_runtime.converse(modelId='arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1::prompt/ABCD1234/3', promptVariables={'customer_question': {'text': user_input}}, guardrailConfig={...})` — the prompt ARN serves as modelId, not as a system block field.

issues.claim: The DoWhile loop node (added 2025) can be used to retry the classifier if the returned confidence is below a threshold before proceeding to routing.
issues.problem: Amazon Bedrock Flows does not have a documented DoWhile node type. The documented node types are: Input, Output, Prompt, Agent, Knowledge Base, Lambda, Condition, Iterator, Collector, and Storage. Iterative retry logic in Flows would use a Condition node looped back or a Lambda-based retry, not a DoWhile node.
issues.fix: Remove the DoWhile reference. Describe using a Condition node with a loop-back connection to the Prompt node to retry while confidence is below threshold, or delegate retry logic to a Lambda node.

issues.claim: standard retry mode with up to 3 retries
issues.problem: Boto3 standard retry mode defaults to DEFAULT_MAX_ATTEMPTS=3, which means 3 total call attempts (1 initial + 2 retries) — not 3 retries (which would be 4 total attempts). The distinction matters when reasoning about latency budgets and quota consumption under throttling.
issues.fix: Change to 'standard retry mode with up to 3 total attempts (1 initial call + 2 retries)'.

cert-aws-generative-ai-developer-profess.note: Mostly solid architecture content; four substantive errors: a fabricated CRIS 'Global' profile type with a phantom 10% cost savings claim, an incorrect Converse API system-block usage for Prompt Management, a non-existent Bedrock Flows DoWhile node, and a retries-vs-attempts confusion in the Boto3 retry description.

issues.claim: this typically labels 70-80% of data automatically after an initial seed batch
issues.problem: AWS's own figure is that automated labeling can reduce labeling costs by up to 70%, not that 70-80% of items are auto-labeled; actual auto-labeled percentage varies widely and one documented example showed only 48% auto-labeled.
issues.fix: Remove the 70-80% figure or replace it with 'can reduce labeling cost by up to 70%'; the auto-labeled fraction depends heavily on task type and data homogeneity and should not be stated as a fixed range.

issues.claim: Statistic='p99'
issues.problem: CloudWatch PutMetricAlarm does not accept 'p99' in the Statistic parameter; Statistic only accepts standard values (Average, Sum, Min, Max, SampleCount). Percentile statistics must be passed via the separate ExtendedStatistic parameter (e.g., ExtendedStatistic='p99'). Using Statistic='p99' will return a validation error.
issues.fix: Replace 'Statistic': 'p99' with 'ExtendedStatistic': 'p99' and remove the 'Statistic' key from the put_metric_alarm call.

issues.claim: Clarify uses a shadow inference mode (sending a copy of real traffic to a processing job) — it does not add latency to the production endpoint
issues.problem: 'Shadow inference mode' is the name of SageMaker's shadow variant/shadow testing feature (a separate product), not how Clarify monitoring works. Clarify monitoring uses SageMaker's data capture feature to log a sample of requests/responses to S3, then runs a scheduled Clarify processing job offline against that captured data; it is not a shadow variant deployment.
issues.fix: Replace 'shadow inference mode' with 'SageMaker data capture — sampled requests and responses are logged to S3, and Clarify runs a scheduled offline processing job against the captured data'.

issues.claim: computes a feature attribution drift score by measuring the distance (typically L-infinity norm or Jensen-Shannon divergence) between the current attribution distribution and the baseline attribution distribution
issues.problem: SageMaker Clarify does not use L-infinity norm or Jensen-Shannon divergence for feature attribution drift. It computes the NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) score comparing the ranking of global SHAP values between the baseline and the current monitoring window; a violation is raised when NDCG falls below 0.9.
issues.fix: Replace the distance-metric description with: 'computes an NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) score comparing the ranking of global SHAP values between the baseline constraints and the current monitoring job analysis; a violation is raised when the NDCG score falls below the configured threshold (default 0.9).'

cert-aws-machine-learning-engineer-assoc.note: 4 issues found across 21 cards: two clear factual errors (wrong CloudWatch API parameter for p99, wrong drift distance metric), one measurement figure that conflates cost reduction with auto-label rate, and one terminology conflation of Clarify data-capture monitoring with shadow-variant testing.

issues.claim: client.connections.list(connection_type='CognitiveSearch')
issues.problem: The ConnectionType enum value for Azure AI Search is 'AzureAISearch' (ConnectionType.AZURE_AI_SEARCH), not 'CognitiveSearch'. Passing 'CognitiveSearch' returns no results.
issues.fix: client.connections.list(connection_type='AzureAISearch')

issues.claim:
client = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint='https://<hub>.api.azureml.ms',
    subscription_id='<sub>',
    resource_group_name='<rg>',
    project_name='<project>',
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential()
)
issues.problem: AIProjectClient v2.2.0 constructor only accepts (endpoint, credential). Parameters subscription_id, resource_group_name, and project_name do not exist and will raise TypeError. The endpoint format is also wrong — the current format is 'https://{ai-services-account-name}.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/{project-name}', not 'https://<hub>.api.azureml.ms'.
issues.fix:
client = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint='https://<ai-services>.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/<project>',
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential()
)

issues.claim:
results = client.indexes.query(
    index_name='docs-index',
    query_text='RAG retrieval best practices',
    top=5
)
issues.problem: IndexesOperations has no query() method. Available methods are: list, get, create_or_update, delete, list_versions. Querying the AI Search index requires the azure-search-documents SDK, not client.indexes.

issues.claim: call the chat completions endpoint using the azure-openai client obtained from client.inference.get_azure_openai_client()
issues.problem: AIProjectClient v2.2.0 has no 'inference' sub-client. The correct method is client.get_openai_client() which returns a standard openai.OpenAI client (not AzureOpenAI) scoped to the Foundry endpoint.
issues.fix: call the chat completions endpoint using the OpenAI client obtained from client.get_openai_client()

issues.claim: azure-ai-projects includes client.inference.get_azure_openai_client(), which returns an AzureOpenAI client already configured with the project's endpoint and managed-identity credential.
issues.problem: The method does not exist. The correct method is client.get_openai_client(), which returns an openai.OpenAI client (not AzureOpenAI). This is in the strong answer (fs), meaning the card teaches the wrong API as correct.
issues.fix: azure-ai-projects includes client.get_openai_client(), which returns an openai.OpenAI client already configured with the Foundry project endpoint and managed-identity credential.

issues.claim: aoai = project.inference.get_azure_openai_client(api_version='2025-01-01-preview')
issues.problem: AIProjectClient has no inference sub-client and no get_azure_openai_client() method. The code will raise AttributeError. Also, the constructor above it passes subscription_id/resource_group_name/project_name which do not exist in v2.2.0.
issues.fix: aoai = project.get_openai_client(api_version='2025-01-01-preview')

issues.claim: from azure.ai.contentsafety import ContentUnderstandingClient  # preview package
issues.problem: ContentUnderstandingClient is not exported from azure.ai.contentsafety. Content Understanding is a separate service from Content Safety and uses its own package/client. This import will raise ImportError.

issues.claim: from azure.ai.contentsafety.models import AnalyzeImageRequest, ImageData
issues.problem: AnalyzeImageRequest does not exist in azure.ai.contentsafety.models. The correct class name is AnalyzeImageOptions. This import will raise ImportError.
issues.fix: from azure.ai.contentsafety.models import AnalyzeImageOptions, ImageData

issues.claim: set the textExtractionAlgorithm to 'handwritten' on regions known to contain handwriting
issues.problem: The textExtractionAlgorithm property was removed from OcrSkill in current Azure AI Search SDK and API versions. It no longer exists as a configurable parameter; handwriting recognition is handled automatically by the underlying Azure Vision OCR backend.
issues.fix: Remove the textExtractionAlgorithm reference. Handwriting is handled automatically by OcrSkill using the Azure Vision Read API — no separate parameter is needed.

issues.claim: analyze_request=AnalyzeDocumentRequest(bytes_source=f.read())
issues.problem: The begin_analyze_document() method's second parameter is named 'body', not 'analyze_request'. Passing it as analyze_request= keyword argument will raise TypeError.
issues.fix:
poller = di_client.begin_analyze_document(
    'prebuilt-layout',
    AnalyzeDocumentRequest(bytes_source=f.read()),
    output_content_format='markdown'
)

issues.claim: use Document Intelligence's prebuilt/idDocument or a custom model trained on your form layout
issues.problem: prebuilt-idDocument (note: slash should be a dash) is designed for national IDs, passports, and driver's licenses — not insurance claim forms. Recommending it for policy-number/claimant-name extraction from insurance documents is the wrong prebuilt model. The correct choice is prebuilt-document (for general-purpose key-value extraction) or a custom model.
issues.fix: use Document Intelligence's prebuilt-document or a custom model trained on your insurance form layout

cert-azure-ai-103.note: Heavy concentration of wrong API calls in the azure-ai-projects SDK integration cards (0, 3): the constructor signature, endpoint format, connection_type enum, and inference sub-client are all fabricated for SDK v2.2.0. Card 9 conflates Content Understanding with Content Safety package. Cards 17 and 18 have a deprecated OcrSkill parameter and wrong method keyword arg respectively. Conceptual/architectural cards (1, 2, 5-8, 10-16, 19) are factually sound.

issues.claim: flat — a brute-force exact search that scans all vectors; accurate but O(n) per query and only practical for small collections (under a few thousand vectors)
issues.problem: The flat index has a hard dimension ceiling of 505 dimensions per the official Cosmos DB docs, meaning it cannot index standard embeddings (e.g., 1536-dim text-embedding-ada-002 or 3072-dim text-embedding-3-large) at all — the primary constraint is dimensional, not just scale.
issues.fix: flat — stores vectors in the main Cosmos DB index for exact brute-force search with 100% recall; limited to a maximum of 505 dimensions, so it cannot be used with standard embedding models (1536+ dims) and is only suitable for low-dimensional dev/test vectors.

issues.claim: quantizedFlat — applies scalar quantization to compress vectors before storing them in a flat index
issues.problem: The official Cosmos DB documentation describes quantizedFlat as compressing vectors using DiskANN-based quantization methods (product or spherical quantization), not scalar quantization. Scalar quantization is a different technique.
issues.fix: quantizedFlat — compresses vectors using DiskANN-based product (or spherical) quantization before storing them in a flat index, reducing latency and RU cost at a minor accuracy cost; recommended for scoped searches of 50,000 vectors or fewer.

issues.claim: The default is 16 for the Premium plan and Elastic Premium, and 2 for Consumption.
issues.problem: The official Azure Functions Service Bus bindings host.json reference shows maxConcurrentCalls defaults to 16 for all hosting plans in both Extension 5.x and Functions 2.x — there is no plan-specific default of 2 for Consumption. The '2 for Consumption' figure is fabricated.
issues.fix: The default is 16 regardless of hosting plan (Consumption, Premium, or Elastic Premium) per the current host.json reference documentation.

issues.claim: Combine with Service Bus's `lockDuration` (default 60 s, max 5 min on Standard, unlimited on Premium)
issues.problem: The official Azure Service Bus documentation (Message Transfers, Locks, and Settlement) states 'The maximum value is 5 minutes' with no tier distinction. There is no unlimited lockDuration on Premium; the 5-minute maximum applies to all tiers.
issues.fix: Service Bus lockDuration defaults to 60 s and has a maximum of 5 minutes on all tiers (Basic, Standard, and Premium); use the SDK's automatic lock-renewal feature for processing that exceeds 5 minutes.

cert-azure-ai-200.note: 3 confirmed factual errors across 2 cards: fabricated plan-specific maxConcurrentCalls default and wrong Premium lockDuration limit in card 12; wrong quantization technique name and missing 505-dimension hard ceiling for flat index in card 4. All other 18 cards are clean.

issues.claim: az ml datastore create --type azure_data_lake_gen2
issues.problem: The --type flag does not exist in AML CLI v2. CLI v2 uses --file <yaml> to register a datastore; the YAML spec contains the type field. This is CLI v1-era syntax incorrectly attributed to v2, and the card's own code block contradicts it by correctly using --file.
issues.fix: Remove the --type flag from the prose and state: 'register it as a datastore in each workspace using `az ml datastore create --file <datastore.yml>`, where the YAML spec sets `type: azure_data_lake_gen2`'

issues.claim: the best model is registered automatically when you set `enable_model_explainability=True` to also generate SHAP feature importance
issues.problem: `enable_model_explainability=True` enables SHAP feature importance for the best AutoML model; it does not trigger model registration. These are orthogonal settings. Model registration in AutoML requires explicit steps (e.g., calling `ml_client.models.create_or_update()` on the best child run's artifact).
issues.fix: Separate the two concepts: 'The job produces a ranked leaderboard of child runs in AML Studio. Setting `enable_model_explainability=True` generates SHAP feature importance for the best model. To register the model, retrieve the best child run and call `ml_client.models.create_or_update()` or use `register_model` in the job configuration.'

issues.claim: can be retrieved with `mlflow.sklearn.load_model()`
issues.problem: AutoML selects from algorithms including XGBoost and LightGBM, which are not sklearn models and are logged to MLflow with the 'pyfunc' flavor, not the 'sklearn' flavor. `mlflow.sklearn.load_model()` will fail for non-sklearn winners. The correct general-purpose loader is `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model()`.
issues.fix: Replace with `mlflow.pyfunc.load_model()`, which works for all AutoML model flavors regardless of the winning algorithm.

issues.claim: `AddFairnessInsights` (parity metrics across sensitive features like age or gender)
issues.problem: `AddFairnessInsights` is not a real component in the azure-ai-ml RAI pipeline SDK. The actual pipeline component names are `add_explanation_to_rai_insights`, `add_causal_to_rai_insights`, `add_counterfactuals_to_rai_insights`, and `add_error_analysis_to_rai_insights`. Fairness metrics in the RAI dashboard are surfaced via the causal or error analysis components alongside Fairlearn integration, not a standalone `AddFairnessInsights` component.
issues.fix: Remove `AddFairnessInsights` from the component list. Fairness parity metrics in the RAI dashboard are enabled through Fairlearn integration and are not a separate named pipeline component in the azure-ai-ml SDK.

cert-azure-ai-300.note: Overall structurally sound but contains 4 factual errors: a CLI v1/v2 confusion in card 0, and three AI-generated confabulations in cards 8–9 (auto-registration conflated with SHAP, wrong MLflow loader, and an invented RAI component name).

issues.claim: Speech-to-text (STR) transcribes spoken audio into written text in the same language
issues.problem: The standard industry abbreviation for speech-to-text is STT, not STR. STR is not a recognized abbreviation for this capability in Azure or anywhere in the speech processing field; it could be confused with 'streaming' or other terms.
issues.fix: Speech-to-text (STT) transcribes spoken audio into written text in the same language

issues.claim: Azure AI Translator service (part of the Azure AI Language family)
issues.problem: Azure AI Translator is a standalone Azure service with its own resource type and API endpoint — it is not part of the Azure AI Language service. The card's own fs field contradicts this by correctly describing Translator as 'a distinct service.' Calling it part of the Azure AI Language family will mislead exam candidates who need to know these are separate billable resources.
issues.fix: Azure AI Translator service (a standalone Azure AI service separate from Azure AI Language)

cert-azure-ai-900.note: 31 cards audited; content is generally accurate and well-calibrated for AI-900. Two issues found: one wrong abbreviation (STR vs STT) and one internal contradiction about Translator's service family. All other factual claims — Responsible AI principles, Azure service distinctions, Transformer architecture, AutoML scope, content filtering categories, and endpoint types — are correct as of 2026.

issues.claim: set grace period (0-3600 seconds before automatic failover triggers after primary loss)
issues.problem: The --grace-period parameter for az sql failover-group is in MINUTES, not seconds; the minimum valid value is 1, not 0.
issues.fix: set grace period (1–3600 minutes before automatic failover triggers after primary loss)

issues.claim: Restores within the same logical server and region only (cross-region restore requires geo-restore from geo-redundant backup)
issues.problem: PITR can restore to a different logical server within the same region; it is not restricted to the same logical server. Only the region restriction applies for standard PITR.
issues.fix: Restores within the same region only; you may target a different logical server in that region. Cross-region restore requires geo-restore from geo-redundant backup.

issues.claim: MOVE 'mydb_data' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb.mdf', MOVE 'mydb_log' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb_log.ldf'
issues.problem: /var/opt/mssql/ is the Linux SQL Server on-premises default path. Azure SQL Managed Instance manages storage internally; users cannot specify physical file paths in a RESTORE. MOVE clauses using this path will fail on MI.
issues.fix: Remove MOVE clauses entirely for a straightforward MI restore, or if renaming logical files is needed, use the actual logical file names from RESTORE FILELISTONLY without specifying MI-internal physical paths.

issues.claim: For RPO near-zero and RTO < 30 seconds—Active Geo-Replication (asynchronous log shipping to readable secondary, manual or auto failover) or Failover Groups
issues.problem: Active Geo-Replication with manual failover does not reliably deliver RTO < 30 seconds—it requires human detection, decision, and CLI/portal action. Sub-30s RTO is only achievable with Failover Groups configured with automatic failover policy; manual geo-replication should not be grouped under the same RTO bucket.
issues.fix: For RPO near-zero and RTO < 30 seconds (automated)—Auto-Failover Groups with automatic failover policy. Active Geo-Replication requires manual intervention and is better characterised as RTO in the minutes range.

issues.claim: Log shipping is simpler than AGs and supported across SQL Server versions and editions (including Standard Edition, unlike AGs)
issues.problem: SQL Server 2016 and later Standard Edition supports Basic Availability Groups (one database per AG, no readable secondary). The blanket claim that Standard Edition does not support AGs is incorrect for SQL 2016+.
issues.fix: Log shipping is simpler than full AGs and is supported on all editions including Standard Edition. Note that SQL Server 2016+ Standard Edition supports Basic Availability Groups (limited: one DB per AG, no read scale), so the distinction is less absolute than stated.

issues.claim: --grace-period 60
issues.problem: In context this is the CLI code sample with --grace-period 60; given the surrounding text claims the unit is seconds, this value would represent 60 seconds (1 minute) but the actual CLI unit is minutes, making 60 mean 60 minutes — the value itself is plausible but the unit documentation is wrong, creating a mismatch.
issues.fix: The value 60 is fine if interpreted as 60 minutes; remove or correct the 'seconds' unit in the prose description above.

cert-azure-dp-300.note: Mostly solid content; 2 confirmed wrong facts (PITR server restriction, MI RESTORE paths) and 1 wrong unit (grace-period seconds vs minutes); 2 misleading claims (Standard Edition AGs, geo-replication RTO); remaining 47 cards are factually clean.

issues.claim: Strong also roughly doubles write RU cost because the write must be confirmed on multiple replicas before returning.
issues.problem: Microsoft's official documentation explicitly states that Strong consistency does NOT add extra RU charges for write operations compared to other consistency levels. The extra cost for Strong applies to reads (which may need to go to the write region), not writes.
issues.fix: Strong consistency does not increase write RU cost. Write operations cost the same RU regardless of consistency level. Strong consistency does increase write latency (round-trip to farthest replica) and reduces write availability during regional partitions, but RU charges are unchanged.

issues.claim: Strong consistency requires synchronous acknowledgment from multiple replicas before confirming a write, which approximately doubles write RU consumption compared to weaker levels.
issues.problem: Same error as the answer body — write RU is not doubled by Strong consistency. The fs reinforces the wrong claim, which makes both the strong answer and the correction it refutes factually incorrect.
issues.fix: Strong consistency increases write latency and reduces write availability during partitions, but does not increase write RU consumption. Read RU for Strong reads in multi-region deployments can be higher because reads may be routed to the write region.

issues.claim: Retention is 2–720 hours.
issues.problem: Card 72 correctly states the minimum retention is 8 hours, not 2 hours. Per Microsoft documentation the minimum periodic backup retention is 8 hours. '2–720 hours' directly contradicts the correct value stated by the very next card in the deck.
issues.fix: Retention is 8–720 hours (8 hours minimum, 720 hours / 30 days maximum; default 8 hours).

issues.claim: each operation must complete within a 5-second execution window
issues.problem: The 5-second server-side execution timeout applies to stored procedures, not to TransactionalBatch. TransactionalBatch is a client-side SDK abstraction that submits a single HTTP request to the Cosmos DB backend; it has no separately documented server-enforced 5-second window. This constraint from stored procedures is incorrectly attributed to TransactionalBatch.
issues.fix: Remove the 5-second execution window constraint from the TransactionalBatch description. That limit is specific to stored procedures. TransactionalBatch uses the client SDK's configured request timeout.

issues.claim: a delete event does not appear directly in the change feed for NoSQL (deletes require TTL-based soft-delete or a tombstone pattern)
issues.problem: Microsoft introduced the 'All versions and deletes' change feed mode (GA ~2024) which does capture hard-delete events directly. The card presents the limitation of the original 'Latest version' mode as absolute, without mentioning that a delete-capable mode now exists.
issues.fix: Qualify this statement: the default 'Latest version' change feed mode does not capture hard deletes and requires a soft-delete pattern. However, the 'All versions and deletes' mode (available as of 2024) does surface delete events directly, making a soft-delete workaround optional when using that mode.

issues.claim: The Cosmos DB change feed does not emit events for hard-deletes. Only inserts and updates (including TTL expirations, which appear as deletes via TTL) are surfaced.
issues.problem: Same outdated claim in the strong-answer field. The 'All versions and deletes' change feed mode introduced in 2024 does emit delete events, making this absolute statement incorrect for current Cosmos DB.
issues.fix: This applies to the 'Latest version' change feed mode. The newer 'All versions and deletes' mode surfaces hard-delete events directly. Qualify both the limitation and the soft-delete recommendation accordingly.

issues.claim: set indexing mode to 'lazy' (asynchronous indexing — lower write latency but queries may not see very recent writes)
issues.problem: Lazy indexing mode was deprecated by Microsoft. Current Cosmos DB documentation marks lazy mode as deprecated and only supports 'consistent' and 'none' for new indexing policy configurations. Recommending lazy as an active option for write-heavy strategies is misleading.
issues.fix: Lazy indexing mode is deprecated; only 'consistent' and 'none' are supported for new configurations. For write-heavy containers, use 'none' (if queries are not needed) or minimize indexed paths via excludedPaths rather than relying on lazy mode.

issues.claim: 'indexingMode' ('consistent', 'lazy', or 'none')
issues.problem: Lazy indexing mode is deprecated. Presenting it as a current valid option in the JSON structure explanation teaches an outdated schema that should not be used for new containers.
issues.fix: 'indexingMode' accepts 'consistent' or 'none'. The 'lazy' value is deprecated and should not be used in new indexing policies.

issues.claim: Use lazy indexing for write-optimized containers where queries can tolerate slight staleness.
issues.problem: Lazy indexing is deprecated. Recommending it as a valid current technique for index optimization is misleading for exam candidates and practitioners.
issues.fix: Lazy indexing is deprecated; replace this advice with: for write-optimized containers set indexing mode to 'none' and disable all indexing, or aggressively exclude non-query paths via excludedPaths.

issues.claim: Via PowerShell: Invoke-AzCosmosDBAccountFailover.
issues.problem: The cmdlet 'Invoke-AzCosmosDBAccountFailover' does not exist in the Az.CosmosDB PowerShell module. The correct cmdlet for changing failover priorities is 'Update-AzCosmosDBAccountFailoverPriority', which is correctly used in card 94.
issues.fix: Via PowerShell: Update-AzCosmosDBAccountFailoverPriority -ResourceGroupName <rg> -Name <account> -FailoverPolicy @{LocationName='East US 2';FailoverPriority=0},...

cert-azure-dp-420.note: Mostly solid content with correct Cosmos DB fundamentals; 10 substantive issues found: one high-severity wrong claim about Strong consistency write RU cost (appears in both a and fs of card 36), one internal contradiction on periodic backup retention minimum (cards 71 vs 72), one stored-procedure constraint incorrectly attributed to TransactionalBatch (card 25), a non-existent PowerShell cmdlet (card 35), two outdated change-feed delete claims (card 48), and three cards presenting deprecated lazy indexing mode as a valid current option (cards 58, 60, 62).

issues.claim: As of August 2025, each workspace is limited to a maximum of 1,000 principals across all roles to ensure service reliability.
issues.problem: Microsoft does not document a 1,000-principal workspace role limit. This appears to be a fabricated specific figure — no such cap appears in Fabric workspace documentation.

issues.claim: You define between 2 and 10 stages at creation time—this number is permanent and cannot be changed later.
issues.problem: Fabric deployment pipelines allow adding stages after creation; the stage count is not permanently fixed at creation time. The 2-to-10 range is correct, but describing it as irreversible is wrong.
issues.fix: You define between 2 and 10 stages; stages can be added after pipeline creation up to the 10-stage maximum. The maximum is 10, not a fixed number set at creation.

issues.claim: DateKey INT NOT NULL REFERENCES dim_Date(DateKey)
issues.problem: Fabric Data Warehouse does not support FOREIGN KEY / REFERENCES constraints in CREATE TABLE DDL. Only PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE are supported (as metadata, unenforced). The REFERENCES syntax will cause a DDL error.
issues.fix: Remove the REFERENCES clauses. Document relationships as comments or define them in the semantic model layer, not in the warehouse DDL.

issues.claim: can be enabled on any Fabric F-SKU, Premium P-SKU, Embedded A-SKU, or Pro workspace assigned to Reserved Capacity
issues.problem: Power BI Pro workspaces cannot enable large semantic model storage format regardless of any capacity assignment. Large format requires Premium Per Capacity (P SKU), Premium Per User (PPU), Embedded A-SKU, or Fabric F-SKU. 'Reserved Capacity' is not a standard Microsoft SKU term for this feature.
issues.fix: Large semantic model storage format can be enabled on Fabric F-SKU, Premium P-SKU (P1 or above), Premium Per User (PPU), or Embedded A-SKU capacities. Power BI Pro workspaces do not support large format.

issues.claim: YEAR(dim_Date[CalendarDate]) = YEAR(TODAY())
issues.problem: The DAX code example uses YEAR() applied to a column reference as a CALCULATE filter argument, which forces a CALLBACKDATAID callback in the Storage Engine — the exact anti-pattern the card's own text warns about just two sentences earlier.
issues.fix: Use CALCULATE(SUM(fact_Sales[SalesAmount]), dim_Date[Year] = YEAR(TODAY())) where Year is a pre-computed integer column on the date table, avoiding the callback. Or use DATESYTD / time-intelligence functions with a properly marked date table.

issues.claim: set the DirectLakeBehavior property in model settings to 'DirectLakeOnly' (errors if fallback is needed), 'DirectQueryOnly' (always use DirectQuery)
issues.problem: The correct enum value for always-use-DirectQuery mode is 'DirectQuery', not 'DirectQueryOnly'. The three valid DirectLakeBehavior values are DirectLake (default, auto-fallback), DirectQuery (always DQ), and DirectLakeOnly (error on fallback).
issues.fix: Set DirectLakeBehavior to 'DirectLakeOnly' (errors if fallback is needed), 'DirectQuery' (always use DirectQuery), or omit the property / use the default 'DirectLake' which allows automatic fallback to DirectQuery.

cert-azure-dp-600.note: Mostly solid Fabric/DP-600 coverage; six issues found — one high-confidence SKU error (card 15), one self-contradicting DAX example (card 16), one likely wrong DDL (card 11 REFERENCES), and two medium-confidence enum/pipeline-mutability claims; card 0's principal cap is unverifiable and likely fabricated.

issues.claim: "spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled": "true"
issues.problem: This is a Databricks-proprietary Spark config key; Microsoft Fabric Spark uses `spark.microsoft.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled` (or controls optimized writes via V-Order at the engine level). A Fabric exam candidate who submits this key in a DP-700 context would be marked wrong.
issues.fix: Replace with `spark.microsoft.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled` or remove from the Fabric-context example and note that V-Order covers this in Fabric by default.

issues.claim: Inside the notebook, `dbutils.widgets.get()` (if using Databricks-style) or the top-level tagged-cell variable overrides the default value.
issues.problem: `dbutils` is a Databricks-only API and does not exist in Microsoft Fabric Spark notebooks. Fabric uses `notebookutils` (alias `mssparkutils`). Even with the qualifier 'Databricks-style', presenting this in a Fabric DP-700 card introduces a wrong API that would confuse and mislead exam candidates.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'Inside the notebook, parameter values from the pipeline override the tagged-cell variables directly; you can also read them programmatically using `notebookutils.notebook.entry_point` if needed.'

issues.claim: A Warehouse is a fully managed T-SQL engine with transaction support, referential integrity (foreign key constraints), and query result caching
issues.problem: Fabric Warehouse does allow declaring foreign key and unique constraints, but they are NOT enforced — they are informational/advisory hints for the query optimizer only (identical behavior to Azure Synapse dedicated pools). Calling this 'referential integrity' alongside 'ACID guarantees' implies the constraints are enforced, which they are not. An exam candidate choosing Warehouse 'when you need referential integrity' would be making a technically wrong decision.
issues.fix: Change to 'declarative (unenforced) foreign key constraints for optimizer hints' and remove the phrase 'referential integrity', or add explicit note that FK constraints are not enforced.

issues.claim: run_id=dbutils.notebook.entry_point.getDbutils().notebook().getContext().notebookPath().get()
issues.problem: `dbutils` is Databricks-only and does not exist in Microsoft Fabric Spark. This code will raise a NameError at runtime in Fabric notebooks. The Fabric equivalent for notebook context is `notebookutils.notebook.entry_point` or `mssparkutils.env.getJobId()`.
issues.fix: Replace with `notebookutils.env.getJobId()` or `mssparkutils.notebook.entry_point.getDbutils().notebook().getContext().notebookPath().get()` — or simply use a UUID/timestamp as a run_id to avoid the API dependency.

issues.claim: You control file size via `spark.sql.files.maxRecordsPerFile` and by tuning micro-batch trigger intervals.
issues.problem: `spark.sql.files.maxRecordsPerFile` applies to batch DataFrameWriter writes, not Structured Streaming. Delta streaming writes are controlled by trigger interval (which determines batch size), `spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled` / `spark.microsoft.delta.autoCompact.enabled`, or `delta.targetFileSize` table property. Using `maxRecordsPerFile` in a streaming context has no effect on streaming file sizes.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'For streaming, file size is governed by the micro-batch trigger interval and Delta's auto-compaction setting (`spark.microsoft.delta.autoCompact.enabled=true`); `spark.sql.files.maxRecordsPerFile` only affects batch writes.'

issues.claim: SELECT command, result_cache_hit, total_elapsed_time_ms FROM sys.dm_exec_requests WHERE session_id = @@SPID;
issues.problem: `result_cache_hit` is a column in Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pool's `sys.dm_exec_requests`, not in Microsoft Fabric Warehouse's implementation. Querying this column in Fabric Warehouse will return 'Invalid column name result_cache_hit'. Fabric Warehouse result-set cache hit information is surfaced differently (via the Monitor Hub / query detail), not through this DMV column.
issues.fix: Remove `result_cache_hit` from the DMV query. To check cache behavior in Fabric Warehouse use the Monitor Hub query detail or the `sys.dm_exec_query_stats` view if available, or note that result-cache hits are not directly queryable via this DMV in Fabric.

cert-azure-dp-700.note: Content is mostly accurate and well-structured but has 6 substantive errors all stemming from Databricks API bleed-over (dbutils, spark.databricks.* config) into a Fabric-native context, one misleading claim about Warehouse FK enforcement, and one wrong DMV column reference inherited from Synapse.

issues.claim: Audit sharing access via `system.access.audit` filtering on `service_name = 'deltaSharingMosaic'`.
issues.problem: The service_name for Delta Sharing events in the Unity Catalog audit log is 'deltaSharing', not 'deltaSharingMosaic'. 'Mosaic' is the name of Databricks' GenAI/MosaicML product line and has no relation to Delta Sharing. A query filtering on 'deltaSharingMosaic' would return zero rows.
issues.fix: Audit sharing access via `system.access.audit` filtering on `service_name = 'deltaSharing'`.

issues.claim: A Declarative Automation Bundle (DAB), renamed from Databricks Asset Bundles on March 16 2026
issues.problem: As of the assistant knowledge cutoff (August 2025), the product was still called 'Databricks Asset Bundles' (DABs). The specific rename date of March 16 2026 is unverifiable and appears fabricated. If the rename did not occur, the entire framing of this card's answer — including the name 'Declarative Automation Bundle' — could be wrong throughout.

cert-azure-dp-750.note: Mostly clean file; one confirmed hallucinated audit log service_name ('deltaSharingMosaic') that would silently return zero rows in production, and one unverifiable post-cutoff rename claim that needs human fact-check against current Databricks docs.

issues.claim: JSON_CONTAINS checks whether a given JSON value exists within a JSON document and returns 1 or 0.
issues.problem: JSON_CONTAINS does not exist in SQL Server or Fabric SQL — it is a MySQL function. SQL Server has no native JSON_CONTAINS; containment checks require OPENJSON with EXISTS, JSON_VALUE comparisons, or full-text search.
issues.fix: Remove JSON_CONTAINS from the function list and from the code example. Replace with: to check JSON membership in SQL Server, use OPENJSON with EXISTS or a JSON_VALUE(...) = '...' predicate.

issues.claim: UNIQUE ensures no two rows share the same value in the constrained column(s) but allows one NULL (multiple NULLs in the same column are permitted in SQL Server's UNIQUE implementation).
issues.problem: SQL Server's UNIQUE constraint allows exactly ONE NULL per column, not multiple NULLs. The parenthetical '(multiple NULLs in the same column are permitted in SQL Server's UNIQUE implementation)' directly contradicts both reality and the card's own fs field.
issues.fix: UNIQUE ensures no two rows share the same value in the constrained column(s) and allows exactly one NULL (SQL Server treats a second NULL as a duplicate and rejects it).

issues.claim: Unlike RAISERROR, THROW does not require a prior CATCH and always sets severity 16.
issues.problem: This is backwards. The argumentless THROW (re-throw form) requires being inside a CATCH block — it will error if used outside one. RAISERROR has no such restriction. Additionally, 'always sets severity 16' is only true for THROW with explicit arguments; the argumentless re-throw form preserves the original error's severity.
issues.fix: Unlike RAISERROR, THROW without arguments must be used inside a CATCH block to re-throw the original error. THROW with arguments (THROW error_number, message, state) can be used anywhere and always raises severity 16. RAISERROR has no CATCH-context requirement.

issues.claim: VECTOR_NORMALIZE(v, norm_type) returns a unit-length vector by dividing the input vector by its norm; use cosine_norm before storing embeddings
issues.problem: The norm_type value 'cosine_norm' is not documented for SQL Server 2025 VECTOR_NORMALIZE. The documented norm_type for L2 (Euclidean) normalization — which enables dot-product as a cosine proxy — is 'norm2'. 'cosine_norm' appears to be a fabricated parameter value.
issues.fix: Use VECTOR_NORMALIZE(v, 'norm2') to normalize to unit length. 'norm2' applies L2/Euclidean normalization so that subsequent dot-product calculations are equivalent to cosine similarity.

issues.claim: It must be enabled first: EXECUTE sp_configure 'external rest endpoint enabled', 1; RECONFIGURE WITH OVERRIDE;
issues.problem: In Azure SQL Database (the primary DP-800 target environment), sp_invoke_external_rest_endpoint is available by default — no sp_configure step is required. The sp_configure pattern applies to SQL Server 2025 on-premises or Azure SQL Managed Instance. Presenting it as universally required is misleading for this exam.
issues.fix: In Azure SQL Database, sp_invoke_external_rest_endpoint is available without configuration. In SQL Server 2025 (on-premises), enable with: EXECUTE sp_configure 'external rest endpoint enabled', 1; RECONFIGURE WITH OVERRIDE;

issues.claim: exclude NULL columns with INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES = OFF
issues.problem: INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES = OFF is not valid T-SQL syntax. INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES is a flag (switch), not a key-value option. The default behavior of FOR JSON PATH already omits NULL values. To include NULLs, you add the flag: FOR JSON PATH, INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES. There is no '= OFF' form.
issues.fix: By default, FOR JSON PATH omits NULL values. To include NULLs, append the INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES flag: SELECT ... FOR JSON PATH, INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES. To exclude NULLs (default), simply omit the flag.

cert-azure-dp-800.note: 6 substantive errors found in 49 cards — JSON_CONTAINS (MySQL-only function presented as SQL Server), UNIQUE NULL count wrong, THROW/CATCH direction inverted, VECTOR_NORMALIZE uses fabricated 'cosine_norm' parameter, sp_configure incorrectly required for Azure SQL Database, and FOR JSON INCLUDE_NULL_VALUES = OFF is invalid syntax.

issues.claim: TRUNCATE (removes all rows from a table by deallocating data pages — faster than DELETE, no row-by-row logging, and cannot be rolled back in most engines)
