issues.problem: In SQL Server and Azure SQL Database specifically, TRUNCATE TABLE CAN be rolled back when wrapped in an explicit BEGIN TRANSACTION…ROLLBACK TRANSACTION block; page deallocation is logged in the transaction log and the rollback fully restores the table.
issues.fix: Replace 'cannot be rolled back in most engines' with 'can be rolled back inside an explicit transaction in SQL Server and Azure SQL Database, unlike in MySQL where TRUNCATE issues an implicit commit and cannot be rolled back'.

issues.claim: is generally non-transactional (or has limited rollback support)
issues.problem: This contradicts how SQL Server / Azure SQL Database actually works: TRUNCATE inside an explicit transaction is fully transactional and rolls back cleanly. Describing it as 'non-transactional' is factually wrong for the engine this exam targets.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'In SQL Server and Azure SQL Database, TRUNCATE is transactional — it can be rolled back inside an explicit transaction. The distinction from DELETE is minimal logging and no WHERE clause support, not non-transactionality.'

issues.claim: Azure Database for MariaDB was also a valid historical choice but is being retired in September 2025, so it should not be selected for new deployments.
issues.problem: As of the current date (2026-06-11), Azure Database for MariaDB was already retired on September 19, 2025. Using future tense 'is being retired' is stale and will confuse exam candidates.
issues.fix: Replace 'is being retired in September 2025' with 'was retired on September 19, 2025'.

issues.claim: define('DB_USER', 'wpuser@mywpserver');
issues.problem: The username@servername format (e.g., wpuser@mywpserver) is the legacy Azure Database for MySQL Single Server convention. Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server — which the answer explicitly recommends — requires the username alone without the @servername suffix.
issues.fix: Change to: define('DB_USER', 'wpuser');

issues.claim: Microsoft has announced that Azure Database for MariaDB will be retired in September 2025
issues.problem: As of 2026-06-11, Azure Database for MariaDB was retired on September 19, 2025 — this is past tense, not an upcoming announcement. The wording presents a completed event as future.
issues.fix: Replace 'will be retired in September 2025' with 'was retired on September 19, 2025'.

issues.claim: Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pools and Azure SQL Data Warehouse are the data warehouse offerings.
issues.problem: Azure SQL Data Warehouse was renamed to Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pools in November 2019. They are not two separate services — Azure SQL Data Warehouse is simply the old product name. Listing both as distinct current offerings implies two products where only one exists.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'Azure Synapse Analytics dedicated SQL pools (formerly Azure SQL Data Warehouse) are the data warehouse offering on Azure.'

cert-azure-dp-900.note: Mostly solid foundational content; four high-confidence errors found: TRUNCATE rollback behavior is wrong for Azure SQL (cards 7a and 7fs), the MySQL Flexible Server connection-string uses the deprecated @servername username format (card 9 code), Azure SQL Data Warehouse is listed as a separate current service when it was renamed to Synapse dedicated SQL pools in 2019 (card 16), and the MariaDB retirement is written in future tense after it already occurred (cards 8 and 9).

issues.claim: Visual calculations were released to General Availability in May 2026.
issues.problem: Visual calculations reached GA in Power BI Desktop's April 2025 release, not May 2026 — the date is off by approximately one year.
issues.fix: Visual calculations were released to General Availability in April 2025.

issues.claim: The classic workspace experience (deprecated) automatically synced membership with an M365/Office 365 group and was limited to four roles.
issues.problem: Classic workspaces supported only three roles — Admin, Member, and Contributor. The Viewer role did not exist in the classic workspace model; it was introduced with the new workspace experience. Saying classic workspaces had four roles is factually incorrect.
issues.fix: The classic workspace experience (deprecated) automatically synced membership with an M365/Office 365 group and was limited to three roles (Admin, Member, Contributor). The Viewer role was introduced in the new workspace experience.

issues.claim: Contributor: can create, edit, and delete their own content in the workspace
issues.problem: The phrase 'their own content' incorrectly restricts the edit permission. Contributors can create, edit, and publish any content in the workspace, not just content they personally created. The limitation to 'their own' content applies specifically to deletion, not to editing.
issues.fix: Contributor: can create, edit, publish, and delete content in the workspace (delete rights may be limited to content they created); cannot manage workspace settings, add members, or update apps.

issues.claim: Error bars: available for bar and scatter charts
issues.problem: Error bars in Power BI's Analytics pane are available for scatter/bubble charts and line/area charts, but not for bar or column charts. Listing bar charts as a supported chart type for error bars is incorrect.
issues.fix: Error bars: available for scatter/bubble charts and line/area charts; not available for bar or column charts.

cert-azure-pl-300.note: Generally solid PL-300 coverage with correct DAX patterns, RLS logic, and service administration details; 4 factual errors found: a wrong GA date for visual calculations, an incorrect role count for classic workspaces, a misleading Contributor permission scope, and error bars incorrectly attributed to bar charts.

issues.claim: enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
issues.problem: tiktoken is OpenAI's tokenizer library; it produces GPT-4o token counts which differ from Claude's tokenizer, giving inaccurate context-budget estimates for any Databricks agent running a Claude or Llama model.
issues.fix: Use the Anthropic Python SDK's client.messages.count_tokens() for Claude models, or the appropriate tokenizer (e.g., transformers AutoTokenizer) for the specific model being served.

issues.claim: CONTEXT_WINDOW = 128000
issues.problem: 128K is GPT-4 Turbo's context window. All Claude 3.x and Claude 4 models (including the databricks-claude-opus-4 used in card 6's code) have a 200K context window, not 128K. Hardcoding 128K causes compaction to trigger ~36% too early.
issues.fix: CONTEXT_WINDOW = 200000 for Claude models. Use the model's actual context window, or retrieve it dynamically.

issues.claim: if the model's context window is 128K, there is ample room
issues.problem: The code example in this same card uses 'databricks-claude-opus-4', whose context window is 200K, not 128K. Citing 128K as the relevant constraint for Claude Opus 4 is factually incorrect and could mislead budget-token sizing decisions.
issues.fix: Replace '128K' with '200K' to match Claude Opus 4's actual context window.

issues.claim: return [r["chunk_text"] for r in results.get("result", {}).get("data_array", [])]
issues.problem: The Databricks Vector Search SDK returns data_array as a list of rows where each row is a list (values in column_names order), not a dict. Indexing r["chunk_text"] on a list raises TypeError. The correct approach is to look up the column index from column_names first.
issues.fix: col_names = results.get('result', {}).get('column_names', []); idx = col_names.index('chunk_text'); return [row[idx] for row in results.get('result', {}).get('data_array', [])]

issues.claim:
ALTER FUNCTION catalog.sales.get_customer_orders
  SET COMMENT '...'
issues.problem: Databricks SQL does not support ALTER FUNCTION ... SET COMMENT. The documented syntax for updating a Unity Catalog function's comment is COMMENT ON FUNCTION catalog.schema.func IS 'text', or redefining the function with CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION and a COMMENT clause.
issues.fix: COMMENT ON FUNCTION catalog.sales.get_customer_orders IS 'Returns order-header rows...'

issues.problem: client.search_traces() returns mlflow.entities.Trace objects, not Span objects. Trace objects do not have .outputs, .name, or .start_time_ms attributes at the top level. To iterate spans, you must access trace.data.spans for each Trace, and individual spans use .start_time_ns (nanoseconds), not .start_time_ms.

cert-databricks-context-engineer.note: Mostly sound conceptual content; the errors cluster in code samples — wrong tokenizer library, incorrect Claude context window size, VectorSearch data_array indexing, invalid Databricks SQL syntax for ALTER FUNCTION, and the MLflow search_traces/Span confusion. No polarity flips detected.

issues.claim: Lineage data captured after September 1, 2024 is retained indefinitely in the metastore
issues.problem: Databricks Unity Catalog lineage data is retained for 1 year (365 days), not indefinitely. There is no documented policy that changed this to indefinite retention.
issues.fix: Lineage data is retained for 1 year in the Unity Catalog metastore.

issues.claim: Databricks Assistant — rebranded as Genie Code in March 2026 — is a context-aware AI coding assistant
issues.problem: As of August 2025 (knowledge cutoff), Databricks Assistant had not been rebranded to 'Genie Code.' The 'Genie' brand refers to AI/BI Genie (natural-language BI spaces), not the code assistant. This appears to be a fabricated future rebranding that cannot be verified.
issues.fix: Databricks Assistant is the AI coding assistant embedded in notebooks and the SQL editor; it is distinct from AI/BI Genie. Do not assert a 'Genie Code' rebranding — no such rebranding is documented.

issues.claim: Remote result cache — serverless-only, persists query results as workspace system data across warehouse restarts
issues.problem: Databricks result caching (the server-side result cache that persists across sessions) is available on Pro SQL warehouses as well as Serverless, not exclusively on Serverless warehouses. Restricting this feature to Serverless-only is factually incorrect.
issues.fix: The server-side result cache is available on Pro and Serverless SQL warehouses (not Classic). It is not Serverless-exclusive.

issues.claim: In Unity Catalog, SELECT on a catalog does not cascade SELECT to tables — it only grants SELECT on the catalog object itself. You must also grant USE SCHEMA and SELECT at the schema or table level.
issues.problem: In Unity Catalog, GRANT SELECT ON CATALOG does propagate (cascade) the SELECT privilege to all tables within that catalog — both existing and future ones. What does NOT cascade object-level SELECT is USE CATALOG (which only permits navigation into the catalog). The answer conflates USE CATALOG with SELECT ON CATALOG. The fj scenario ('granting SELECT on a catalog automatically gives SELECT on all tables') is actually correct behavior in UC, making the fj and fs polarity at minimum misleading.
issues.fix: USE CATALOG (navigation privilege) does not cascade SELECT. However, GRANT SELECT ON CATALOG does cascade SELECT to all tables in that catalog. The minimum privilege chain for read access is USE CATALOG + USE SCHEMA + SELECT on the table — because SELECT on catalog is overly broad. Revise to clarify the difference between USE CATALOG and SELECT ON CATALOG.

issues.claim: system.query.history table in Unity Catalog provides the same metrics programmatically, with columns like execution_duration_ms, rows_produced, spill_bytes, and from_result_cache
issues.problem: The system.query.history system table does not have a top-level column named 'spill_bytes'. Spill metrics in the system table are nested inside the metrics struct (e.g., metrics.total_task_duration_ms). The column 'spill_bytes' as a flat column does not exist in the documented schema, making code samples that reference it invalid.

issues.claim: How does Databricks Assistant (now Genie Code) help analysts
issues.problem: The question embeds the unverified rebranding 'now Genie Code' as established fact. As of August 2025 Databricks Assistant had not been renamed; the parenthetical propagates the same fabricated claim from the answer into the question stem.
issues.fix: How does Databricks Assistant help analysts in the SQL editor and notebooks, and what are its key capabilities?

cert-databricks-data-analyst.note: Overall content is well-structured and covers the cert domain accurately, but contains one fabricated product rebranding (Genie Code), one wrong retention policy (lineage data indefinite vs 1-year), and one wrong scope restriction (result cache Serverless-only vs Pro+Serverless). The SELECT-on-catalog cascade claim also warrants a human review.

issues.claim: Databricks Connect v2 (for Databricks Runtime 13+) supports the DataFrame API, Spark SQL, and UDFs, but does not support RDD APIs or Structured Streaming.
issues.problem: Databricks Connect v2 added Structured Streaming support starting with DBR 14.1. Stating that Structured Streaming is unsupported is outdated for any runtime >= 14.1, which covers the majority of current production workloads.
issues.fix: Databricks Connect v2 (for Databricks Runtime 13+) supports the DataFrame API, Spark SQL, and UDFs. RDD APIs are not supported. Structured Streaming support was added in DBR 14.1 — on DBR 13.x it is not supported, but on 14.1+ it is.

issues.claim: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs), now officially called Declarative Automation Bundles
issues.problem: As of 2026 the product is still officially named 'Databricks Asset Bundles' (DABs) in all Databricks documentation and CLI tooling. 'Declarative Automation Bundles' is not the official name — this appears to be a hallucinated rename.
issues.fix: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) are the official name. Drop the parenthetical rename claim.

issues.claim: In 2026, Databricks deprecated the Terraform-based engine in favor of the Direct Deployment Engine, making bundle deployment faster and reducing the dependency on external IaC tooling.
issues.problem: This is a specific, dated factual claim that does not correspond to any known Databricks deprecation announcement. Databricks Asset Bundles have always used their own deployment engine (not Terraform), so the framing of 'deprecating the Terraform-based engine' is incorrect. Databricks Terraform Provider exists as a separate community/partner tool and was not deprecated. This appears to be a hallucinated fact.

issues.claim: Declarative Automation Bundles can define and deploy any Databricks resource type including jobs, Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, clusters, model serving endpoints, SQL warehouses, and dashboards—all as YAML alongside code.
issues.problem: The fs propagates the hallucinated 'Declarative Automation Bundles' name. It should refer to 'Databricks Asset Bundles'. The substance is otherwise correct.
issues.fix: Databricks Asset Bundles can define and deploy any Databricks resource type including jobs, Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, clusters, model serving endpoints, SQL warehouses, and dashboards—all as YAML alongside code.

issues.claim: Materialized views recompute their result from scratch (or incrementally if Delta supports it) and are the right choice for aggregate or join outputs that derive from slowly-changing inputs.
issues.problem: In Lakeflow (DLT), materialized views do support incremental refresh for some patterns, but the parenthetical 'or incrementally if Delta supports it' is vague and misleading. More importantly, in DLT a dataset backed by `dlt.read_stream` is always a Streaming Table, not a Materialized View — the code example in this same card contradicts this by labeling `clean_events` (which uses `dlt.read_stream`) as a 'materialized view'.

issues.claim: # Silver: materialized view with expectation
issues.problem: The code comment labels `clean_events` as a 'materialized view', but it is defined with `dlt.read_stream('raw_events')`, which makes it a Streaming Table in DLT/Lakeflow. A Materialized View must use `dlt.read()` (batch read). Using `dlt.read_stream` on a streaming table produces another streaming table, not a materialized view.
issues.fix: Change the comment to '# Silver: streaming table with expectation' and update the prose answer to clarify that the example creates a streaming table, not a materialized view.

issues.claim: the pipeline event log (a Delta table named `<pipeline_id>.event_log`)
issues.problem: The event log in Lakeflow / DLT is surfaced as a system table at `system.lakeflow` or accessed via `event_log` within the pipeline's target schema, not as a table literally named `<pipeline_id>.event_log`. The naming convention given does not match the actual Unity Catalog table path where the event log is queryable (it is stored in the pipeline's storage location, not under a catalog path using the pipeline ID as the first name component).

issues.claim: For Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, you pass parameters at the pipeline configuration level and read them inside a pipeline with `spark.conf.get('pipelines.parameter_name')`.
issues.problem: In Lakeflow / DLT, pipeline parameters are accessed via `spark.conf.get('pipelines.<key>')` when using the legacy DLT parameter mechanism. However, with Unity Catalog pipelines and the newer parameter passing API, parameters can also be accessed differently. The prefix 'pipelines.' is correct for DLT pipeline configuration values, but readers may confuse this with arbitrary Spark conf keys. This is accurate enough but could mislead about the exact key prefix format for passed job parameters vs pipeline configuration properties.

cert-databricks-de-associate.note: Mostly solid conceptual content; the critical issues are a hallucinated product rename ('Declarative Automation Bundles'), a hallucinated 2026 Terraform-engine deprecation in card 10, an outdated claim that Databricks Connect v2 does not support Structured Streaming (fixed in DBR 14.1), and a wrong code comment in card 8 that mislabels a streaming table as a materialized view.

issues.claim: Databricks Asset Bundles (now called Declarative Automation Bundles)
issues.problem: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) were not renamed to 'Declarative Automation Bundles'. The official product name remains Databricks Asset Bundles. 'Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines' is a separate product (formerly Delta Live Tables). This fabricated name is propagated across ~8 cards.
issues.fix: Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs)

issues.claim: Auto Loader's schema inference samples the first 50 GB or 1,000 files (whichever is reached first)
issues.problem: The Databricks documentation states Auto Loader samples 1 GB of data (not 50 GB) alongside up to 1,000 files for schema inference. The 50 GB figure is incorrect.
issues.fix: Auto Loader's schema inference samples up to 1 GB of data or 1,000 files (whichever is reached first)

issues.claim: the recipient can then use pandas (pd.read_delta_sharing_table()) or the delta-sharing Python client to read the table
issues.problem: pandas has no function called read_delta_sharing_table(). The correct API is delta_sharing.load_as_pandas(profile_file, table_url) from the delta-sharing Python package. This is a fabricated function name.
issues.fix: the recipient uses the delta-sharing Python client: delta_sharing.load_as_pandas('<profile_file>', '<share_name>.<schema_name>.<table_name>')

issues.claim: Adaptive Query Execution's skew join optimization (enabled by default in Databricks Runtime 7.3+)
issues.problem: DBR 7.3 LTS runs Spark 3.0.x, in which AQE was shipped but disabled by default (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=false). AQE was enabled by default starting Spark 3.2 / DBR 10.x. Databricks may patch defaults, but claiming 7.3+ as the default-enabled threshold is inconsistent with known version history.
issues.fix: Adaptive Query Execution's skew join optimization (enabled by default in Databricks Runtime 10.x / Spark 3.2+)

issues.claim: SELECT current_setting('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions');
issues.problem: current_setting() is a PostgreSQL built-in function; it does not exist in Spark SQL or Databricks SQL and will throw AnalysisException: undefined function current_setting. To display a Spark config value use: SET spark.sql.shuffle.partitions; (no SELECT wrapper).
issues.fix: SET spark.sql.shuffle.partitions;

issues.claim: Delta's time travel feature retains the deleted data in old Parquet files for the default 30-day retention window
issues.problem: The 30-day default applies to delta.logRetentionDuration (transaction log history). The property that governs how long deleted data files are retained before VACUUM can remove them is delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration, which defaults to 7 days (interval 7 days). Conflating the two is factually wrong for GDPR planning purposes.
issues.fix: Delta's time travel feature retains deleted data in old Parquet files for the default 7-day retention window (delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration = interval 7 days)

issues.claim: run: pip install databricks-cli
issues.problem: pip install databricks-cli installs the legacy Python-based Databricks CLI (v0.18.x), which does not support the 'databricks bundle' subcommand. The subsequent step 'databricks bundle deploy --target staging' would fail. The new Go-based CLI (v0.200+) must be installed via the official GitHub Action (uses: databricks/setup-databricks-cli@v0.x) or via curl to the binary release.
issues.fix: uses: databricks/setup-databricks-cli@v0.x  # or: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/databricks/setup-databricks-cli/main/setup.sh | sh

issues.claim: PySpark's DeltaTable.merge() API or Spark SQL MERGE INTO handles both phases in a single atomic transaction … WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT adds the new record with effective_start = today and is_current = true
issues.problem: The MERGE code is broken for changed records. WHEN NOT MATCHED fires only when the source row has NO match in the target (i.e., a brand-new customer). For an existing customer whose attributes changed, the MATCHED clause closes the old record but the source row is then 'matched', so WHEN NOT MATCHED never fires — the new version of the record is never inserted. A correct SCD2 implementation requires either two separate MERGE/INSERT statements or a UNION ALL source that duplicates changed rows.
issues.fix: SCD Type 2 requires a two-statement approach: (1) MERGE to close changed records, (2) a separate INSERT INTO ... SELECT from source WHERE customer_id changed. Both can be wrapped in a transaction. The single-MERGE approach as written silently drops the new version for changed customers.

cert-databricks-de-professional.note: 8 substantive bugs across 56 cards: a fabricated product rename (DABs), wrong Auto Loader sample size (50 GB vs 1 GB), nonexistent pandas function, wrong Delta deleted-file retention default (30d vs 7d), broken SCD2 MERGE that drops new versions of changed records, a Postgres-only function used in Spark SQL, wrong DBR version for AQE default, and a GitHub Actions step that installs the legacy CLI which cannot run 'databricks bundle'.

issues.claim: ).choices[0].message.content AS category
issues.problem: In Databricks SQL, ai_query() against a chat endpoint returns a STRING directly, not an OpenAI-style struct with a choices array. The .choices[0].message.content accessor is the Python openai SDK pattern and is not valid SQL syntax; this query will fail at parse or execution time.
issues.fix: ai_query('databricks-meta-llama-3-3-70b-instruct', CONCAT(...)) AS category

issues.claim: ).choices[0].message.content AS sentiment"""
issues.problem: Same ai_query() return-type error as card 26: Databricks SQL ai_query() returns a STRING, not a choices struct. The .choices[0].message.content accessor will cause a SQL type/parse error inside a selectExpr() call.
issues.fix: ai_query('databricks-meta-llama-3-3-70b-instruct', CONCAT(...)) AS sentiment

issues.claim: AND tenant_id = tenant_id
issues.problem: The SQL WHERE clause compares the table column tenant_id to itself, which is always TRUE. The function parameter is also named tenant_id, causing a name collision; the predicate never filters by the caller's tenant, completely defeating the multi-tenant isolation the example is supposed to demonstrate.
issues.fix: Rename the function parameter to avoid collision, e.g., CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION ... (input_tenant_id STRING) ... AND user_tenant_map.tenant_id = input_tenant_id

issues.claim: Databricks AI Security Framework (DASF v3.0)
issues.problem: The DASF was publicly at v1.0/v1.1 as of early 2025. 'v3.0' appears to be an extrapolated version number with no confirmed public release; citing a specific version that may not exist can mislead exam candidates who look it up.

cert-databricks-genai-associate.note: Mostly solid content with good Databricks-native depth; two critical code bugs (ai_query SQL return type in cards 26 and 54) and one logic bug (self-referential SQL predicate in card 35's multi-tenant row-filter example) need immediate correction before this track is used for exam prep.

issues.claim: compute the column mean and standard deviation using `df.agg(F.mean, F.stddev)`
issues.problem: You cannot pass unbound function objects (F.mean, F.stddev) directly to df.agg(). They must be called with a column name, e.g. df.agg(F.mean('col'), F.stddev('col')). Passing bare function references raises a TypeError in PySpark.
issues.fix: compute the column mean and standard deviation using `df.agg(F.mean('col').alias('mean'), F.stddev('col').alias('stddev'))`

issues.claim: it requires a `rawPredictionCol` or `probabilityCol` containing the probability or raw score for the positive class
issues.problem: `BinaryClassificationEvaluator` has no `probabilityCol` parameter. Its MRO includes only `HasRawPredictionCol`; it accepts `rawPredictionCol` only (which can be a double score or a two-element vector). The `probabilityCol` claim is false.
issues.fix: it requires a `rawPredictionCol` column containing the raw score or probability vector for the positive class (a double or length-2 vector); there is no `probabilityCol` parameter on this evaluator

issues.claim: Configure it with `MonitoringAnalysis` specifying the table name, timestamp column, and model output column.
issues.problem: `MonitoringAnalysis` is not a real Databricks API class. The Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring Python API uses `databricks.sdk.service.catalog.CreateMonitor` (SDK) or the `databricks-lakehouse-monitoring` library with profile types such as `InferenceLog` or `TimeSeries`. No class called `MonitoringAnalysis` exists in the public API.

cert-databricks-ml-associate.note: Mostly solid content; three factual errors found: a broken PySpark agg() call syntax, a nonexistent probabilityCol parameter on BinaryClassificationEvaluator, and a fabricated MonitoringAnalysis API class name in the Lakehouse Monitoring card.

issues.claim: Optuna on Databricks leverages two MLflow 3.0 integration classes: MlflowStorage (uses the MLflow Tracking Server as Optuna's storage backend, enabling persistence and resume of studies) and MlflowSparkStudy (launches parallel Optuna trials across PySpark executors)
issues.problem: No public MLflow 3.0 classes named MlflowStorage or MlflowSparkStudy exist. There is no mlflow.optuna module in MLflow. The actual Databricks-recommended approach for distributed Optuna uses optuna-integration with MLflowCallback for tracking, and optuna-distributed or Hyperopt SparkTrials for Spark parallelism. This API is fabricated.

issues.claim: MlflowSparkStudy is specifically designed to distribute Optuna trials across PySpark executors. Each executor runs an independent trial and results are coordinated through MlflowStorage on the tracking server
issues.problem: The fs correction doubles down on the fabricated API. MlflowSparkStudy does not exist in any public MLflow or Optuna package. The fs field is supposed to correct the fj, but it references a non-existent class, making the 'correct' answer itself wrong.

issues.claim: from mlflow.optuna import MlflowStorage, MlflowSparkStudy
issues.problem: This import does not exist — there is no mlflow.optuna module. Running this code raises ImportError. The correct Databricks approach uses optuna with MLflowCallback from optuna_integration.mlflow, and for Spark parallelism uses optuna-distributed or Hyperopt SparkTrials.
issues.fix: from optuna_integration.mlflow import MLflowCallback  # for tracking; use optuna-distributed or hyperopt.SparkTrials for Spark parallelism

issues.claim: def predict_udf(iterator: Iterator[pd.DataFrame]) -> Iterator[pd.Series]:
issues.problem: The Iterator SCALAR_ITER pandas UDF receives an Iterator of pd.Series (one per input column) or Iterator[Tuple[pd.Series, ...]] for multiple columns — not Iterator[pd.DataFrame]. An Iterator[pd.DataFrame] signature is for mapInPandas, not a scalar UDF. When called with three columns (age, tenure, product_tier), the correct type hint is Iterator[Tuple[pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series]].
issues.fix: def predict_udf(iterator: Iterator[Tuple[pd.Series, pd.Series, pd.Series]]) -> Iterator[pd.Series]:  (and add Tuple to the typing import)

issues.claim: z_stat, p_value = stats.proportions_ztest(
issues.problem: proportions_ztest does not exist in scipy.stats. It is in statsmodels.stats.proportion (from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportions_ztest). The import 'from scipy import stats' followed by stats.proportions_ztest() would raise AttributeError.
issues.fix:
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportions_ztest
z_stat, p_value = proportions_ztest(count=[champion.conversions, challenger.conversions], nobs=[champion.n, challenger.n])

issues.claim: (drift_type = 'chi_squared_test' AND drift_value > 0.05)
issues.problem: The direction of the chi-squared threshold is wrong for detecting drift. If drift_value stores the p-value, the alert condition should be drift_value < 0.05 (small p-value = drift detected), not > 0.05. If drift_value stores the test statistic, then 0.05 is a meaningless threshold (chi-squared statistics are unbounded positive values, e.g., the critical value at p=0.05 for 1 df is 3.84). Card index 25 in the same file explicitly states 'Chi-squared p-value: <0.05 significant', contradicting this > 0.05 condition.
issues.fix: (drift_type = 'chi_squared_test' AND drift_value < 0.05)  -- assuming drift_value stores the p-value

cert-databricks-ml-professional.note: The track has one severe fabricated-API issue (mlflow.optuna MlflowSparkStudy/MlflowStorage) that pervades an entire card and its fs correction; one iterator pandas UDF type-hint bug; one wrong scipy API (proportions_ztest); and one chi-squared threshold polarity error. Remaining 44 cards are factually sound.

issues.claim: shows count/distinct where applicable
issues.problem: summary() has no distinct-count statistic. For string columns it computes count, min, and max only — there is no 'distinct' row in summary() output.
issues.fix: summary() works on numeric columns for stats like mean and percentiles, but it also includes count, min, and max for string columns. It does not raise an error on strings — it simply skips inapplicable statistics.

issues.claim: they do not support dict methods like .keys() or .items() without first converting with row.asDict()
issues.problem: pyspark.sql.Row has a native .keys() method that returns the list of field names without calling asDict(). Only .items() and full dict-style iteration require asDict().
issues.fix: Row objects are not standard dicts — they do not support dict methods like .items() or full dict iteration without first converting with row.asDict(). However, Row.keys() is available directly and returns the list of field names.

issues.claim: dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() (Databricks Runtime 13.3 LTS+, Spark 3.5+)
issues.problem: DBR 13.3 LTS ships with Spark 3.4.x, not Spark 3.5. Pairing 'DBR 13.3 LTS+' with 'Spark 3.5+' is internally inconsistent. dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark requires Spark 3.5, which corresponds to DBR 14.0+.
issues.fix: dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() (Spark 3.5+, Databricks Runtime 14.0+)

issues.claim: dropDuplicates() used alongside withWatermark() does NOT guarantee bounded state — it still maintains all distinct keys and only uses the watermark to determine when to emit results
issues.problem: Per Spark documentation, dropDuplicates() combined with withWatermark() DOES evict (bound) state — Spark drops state for keys whose event time falls before (max_event_time - watermark_threshold). The fj therefore describes correct behavior. The semantic difference between dropDuplicates+watermark and dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark is about dedup guarantee scope (global vs. within-window), not whether state is bounded. The answer body itself contradicts the fs by saying 'Spark can safely evict state for keys whose event time is older than max_event_time minus the watermark threshold'.

cert-databricks-spark-developer.note: Mostly solid content; four substantive errors found — a fabricated 'distinct' stat in summary(), a wrong Row.keys() claim, an internally inconsistent DBR/Spark version pair, and a factually inverted fs on dropDuplicates+watermark state bounding.

issues.claim: delivers those changes in near-real-time to BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Cloud SQL, or Spanner
issues.problem: Spanner is not a GA Datastream destination on the same footing as BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Cloud SQL; it was in limited preview/preview GA as of 2024-2025 and is absent from the primary destination list in Google's current Datastream documentation.
issues.fix: Remove Spanner from the list; state 'BigQuery, Cloud Storage, or Cloud SQL' and note Spanner support is in preview.

issues.claim: BigQuery recommends Avro for streaming load jobs.
issues.problem: BigQuery's documentation recommends Avro for batch LOAD jobs (bq load / Storage Write API batch mode), not 'streaming load jobs'. Streaming inserts (tabledata.insertAll / Storage Write API streaming mode) accept JSON rows or protobuf, not Avro files. 'Streaming load jobs' is a non-standard conflation of two different BQ ingestion paths.
issues.fix: BigQuery recommends Avro for batch load jobs. Streaming inserts use JSON rows (insertAll) or protobuf (Storage Write API); Avro is not used for streaming inserts.

issues.claim: buffers messages for up to 7 days (configurable)
issues.problem: 7 days is the default message retention duration, not the maximum. Since 2021 Pub/Sub supports configurable retention from 10 minutes up to 31 days. Stating 7 days as the ceiling is factually incorrect.
issues.fix: Pub/Sub buffers messages with a default retention of 7 days, configurable from 10 minutes up to 31 days.

issues.claim: it delivers events exactly once (or retries on transient failures) and is designed for triggering serverless reactions
issues.problem: The parenthetical '(or retries on transient failures)' contradicts exactly-once delivery — retries imply at-least-once semantics. Eventarc provides at-least-once delivery with exponential-backoff retries, not exactly-once delivery.
issues.fix: Eventarc delivers events at-least-once with exponential-backoff retries on transient failures.

issues.claim: built-in SQL functions `DLP_DEID` and `DLP_REID` perform deterministic encryption/decryption at query time using Cloud KMS-wrapped keys
issues.problem: DLP_DEID and DLP_REID do not exist as BigQuery native SQL functions. BigQuery's column-level encryption uses AEAD functions (AEAD.ENCRYPT / AEAD.DECRYPT_BYTES). SDP de-identification is invoked via the SDP API or a Dataflow template, not through BigQuery SQL functions with these names.
issues.fix: Remove the DLP_DEID/DLP_REID claim. BigQuery native column encryption uses AEAD.ENCRYPT and AEAD.DECRYPT_BYTES SQL functions. For SDP-based de-identification in BigQuery pipelines, use the Dataflow 'Stream DLP Data DeID to BigQuery' template or the SDP API; there are no native BigQuery SQL functions named DLP_DEID or DLP_REID.

issues.claim: the fine-grained reader role (`datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedViewer`)
issues.problem: The IAM role for accessing policy-tagged columns is roles/datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader, not categoryFineGrainedViewer. Using the wrong role name would cause access denied errors in practice.
issues.fix: Replace `datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedViewer` with `datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader`.

cert-gcp-associate-data-practitioner.note: Six substantive errors found: one invented BigQuery SQL function pair (DLP_DEID/DLP_REID), one wrong IAM role name (Viewer vs Reader), one wrong Eventarc delivery guarantee, one wrong Pub/Sub max-retention ceiling, one wrong BigQuery format recommendation (streaming vs batch load), and one uncertain Datastream destination (Spanner GA status). The rest of the 26 cards are factually sound.

issues.claim: Modern systems like Imagen use latent diffusion, where denoising happens in a compressed latent space rather than pixel space, making generation far more compute-efficient.
issues.problem: Imagen (including the original Imagen 1 line) is a pixel-space cascaded diffusion model, not a latent diffusion model. Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) are primarily associated with Stable Diffusion / the CompVis work. This claim directly contradicts card index 1 in the same file, which correctly states Imagen uses a cascaded diffusion architecture operating in pixel space with super-resolution stages.
issues.fix: Remove the sentence attributing latent diffusion to Imagen. Imagen uses a cascaded pixel-space diffusion approach (base model → super-resolution upsampler); latent diffusion (operating in a VAE-encoded latent space) is the architecture of Stable Diffusion and similar models.

issues.claim: Gemma is released under the Apache 2.0 license, so teams can modify and redistribute without royalties.
issues.problem: Gemma is NOT released under Apache 2.0. It is released under Google's custom Gemma Terms of Use, which impose prohibited-use restrictions and usage policy requirements that Apache 2.0 does not. Stating it is Apache 2.0 would lead a candidate to answer exam questions about Gemma's licensing incorrectly.
issues.fix: Gemma is released under the Gemma Terms of Use (a custom Google license), not Apache 2.0. The terms allow commercial use but include prohibited-use clauses and require compliance with Google's usage policies.

issues.claim: Unity AI Gateway for rate limiting and PII filtering
issues.problem: 'Unity AI Gateway' is not a recognized Google Cloud product. The real GCP products for these concerns are Apigee (API management and rate limiting) and Model Armor (AI-specific prompt/response filtering and PII redaction). This appears to be a hallucinated product name.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'Apigee or API Gateway for rate limiting, and Model Armor for prompt/response safety inspection and PII filtering'.

cert-gcp-generative-ai-leader.note: Three substantive errors found: Imagen misclassified as a latent-diffusion model (it is pixel-space cascaded diffusion), Gemma's license incorrectly stated as Apache 2.0 (it is Google's custom Gemma Terms of Use), and a hallucinated product name 'Unity AI Gateway' (real products are Apigee + Model Armor). All other cards are factually sound.

issues.claim: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL, is fully managed but offers higher performance (4× faster OLTP than Cloud SQL, 100× faster analytics)
issues.problem: Google's published AlloyDB benchmarks compare against Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL (or standard PostgreSQL), not against Cloud SQL. The 4× figure is never stated as a Cloud SQL comparison in Google's documentation; claiming it is inflates the implied gap and will mislead a candidate who looks up the source.
issues.fix: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL, is fully managed but offers higher performance (up to 4× faster OLTP than Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL, up to 100× faster analytics than standard PostgreSQL) and is optimized for PostgreSQL-compatible workloads that outgrow standard Cloud SQL.

issues.claim: keep Oracle LogMiner running in reverse so that if AlloyDB has a defect post-cutover, you can flip connection strings back and Oracle catches up via CDC from AlloyDB binlog
issues.problem: Two errors in one sentence: (1) AlloyDB is PostgreSQL-compatible and uses WAL (write-ahead log), not binlog — binlog is MySQL terminology. (2) Oracle LogMiner reads Oracle redo logs only; it cannot read AlloyDB/PostgreSQL WAL. The described reverse mechanism is technically incoherent. A real fallback would use DMS reverse replication or Datastream to capture AlloyDB change data, not Oracle LogMiner.
issues.fix: Before cutover, enable DMS reverse replication (in preview for Oracle→AlloyDB) so that if AlloyDB has a defect post-cutover, you can flip connection strings back and Oracle catches up via CDC streaming from AlloyDB WAL using Datastream or the DMS failback job.

issues.claim: DMS handles the DML data migration via CDC over the homogeneous SQL Server source connector
issues.problem: SQL Server to Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL is a heterogeneous migration, not homogeneous. DMS uses a heterogeneous connector for this path. Labeling it 'homogeneous' is factually incorrect and will confuse a candidate studying the DMS documentation where homogeneous (same engine) and heterogeneous (cross-engine) are explicitly distinguished.
issues.fix: DMS handles the DML data migration via CDC over the heterogeneous SQL Server source connector.

issues.claim: automated backups take one full backup per day during a configurable backup window; they are stored in Cloud Storage and replicated across two regions by default
issues.problem: Cloud SQL automated backups are stored in a single region by default (the same region as the instance, in multiregional Cloud Storage for high availability within that geography). Cross-region backup — which stores a backup copy in a geographically separate region — is a distinct, opt-in paid feature. Saying 'replicated across two regions by default' implies cross-region backup is on by default, which it is not.
issues.fix: they are stored in Cloud Storage in the same region as the instance by default (cross-region backup is a separate opt-in feature that stores an additional copy in a different region)

cert-gcp-professional-cloud-database-eng.note: 4 substantive issues across 9 cards: wrong performance-benchmark baseline (AlloyDB vs Cloud SQL), technically incoherent reverse-replication fallback using MySQL terminology on a PostgreSQL target, wrong heterogeneous/homogeneous label for SQL Server→PostgreSQL DMS path, and a misleading default-replication claim for automated backups. The migration-tool selection card (7) and IAM/Auth Proxy card (1) are clean.

issues.claim: ENCRYPT_VALUE() is an AEAD column encryption function designed for deterministic lookups
issues.problem: ENCRYPT_VALUE() does not exist in BigQuery standard SQL. BigQuery AEAD encryption uses AEAD.ENCRYPT(), AEAD.DECRYPT_STRING(), and KEYS.NEW_KEYSET(); treating a non-existent function as a real one with specific properties will mislead candidates into thinking it exists.
issues.fix: Replace with: 'AEAD.ENCRYPT() and related AEAD functions provide column-level encryption in BigQuery, but they are not a classification-and-masking pipeline...' (or simply note that there is no ENCRYPT_VALUE() function in BigQuery — the suggestion is doubly wrong because the function does not exist).

issues.claim: Dataflow handles parallelism, worker autoscaling, and exactly-once semantics (via shuffle) automatically.
issues.problem: Exactly-once semantics in batch Dataflow come from the runner's deterministic retry model, not from Dataflow Shuffle. Shuffle is a performance/cost optimization that offloads GroupByKey operations to Google's infrastructure; it has no direct causal relationship to exactly-once guarantees. Attributing exactly-once to shuffle conflates two unrelated features.
issues.fix: Remove the parenthetical: '...and exactly-once semantics automatically.' Optionally add: 'Dataflow Shuffle improves GroupByKey performance and cost but is separate from the runner's exactly-once fault-tolerance guarantee.'

issues.claim: Each replicated row includes metadata columns (_metadata_source, _metadata_operation, _metadata_timestamp)
issues.problem: Datastream's BigQuery destination stores metadata in a RECORD column called 'datastream_metadata' containing fields such as 'change_type' (not '_metadata_operation'), 'source_timestamp', 'uuid', 'is_deleted', and 'sort_keys'. The column names listed (_metadata_source, _metadata_operation, _metadata_timestamp) do not match the documented Datastream BigQuery metadata schema.
issues.fix: Correct to: 'Each replicated row includes a datastream_metadata RECORD column containing fields such as change_type (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), source_timestamp, uuid, and is_deleted.'

issues.claim: storage type is permanent — you choose SSD or HDD at instance creation and cannot change it later. If requirements evolve, you must migrate to a new instance.
issues.problem: As of 2023-2024, Cloud Bigtable supports in-place storage type changes between SSD and HDD; migration to a new instance is no longer required. This caveat was historically accurate but is no longer correct.
issues.fix: Remove or update the caveat: 'Note: Cloud Bigtable now supports changing storage type in-place; a full instance migration is no longer required when requirements evolve.'

issues.claim: HDD nodes can hold up to 16 TB each, so fewer nodes are needed compared to SSD (5 TB/node)
issues.problem: Per Cloud Bigtable documentation, HDD nodes have a recommended maximum of 8 TB per node, not 16 TB. The SSD figure of 5 TB/node is also above the documented recommended optimum of ~2.5 TB/node (though a higher hard cap exists). Claiming HDD holds 2x more than it actually does misleads cost/node-count calculations.
issues.fix: Correct to approximately: 'HDD nodes hold up to 8 TB each, compared to ~2.5 TB recommended per SSD node, still yielding meaningful node-count savings for large archives.'

issues.claim: The default is AUTO_SPLIT, which uses 80/20 train/eval for datasets under 100k rows and switches to a stratified random split based on the label column for larger datasets.
issues.problem: BigQuery ML AUTO_SPLIT uses a deterministic hash-based 20% evaluation split regardless of dataset size; it does not automatically switch to a stratified split for datasets over 100k rows. Stratified splitting requires explicitly setting STRATIFY_COLUMN in the OPTIONS clause. The 100k-row threshold and automatic stratification are not documented behaviors.
issues.fix: Correct to: 'AUTO_SPLIT uses a deterministic hash-based 80/20 train/eval split. For stratified splits, you must explicitly set the STRATIFY_COLUMN option — it is not applied automatically at any row-count threshold.'

cert-gcp-professional-data-engineer.note: 6 substantive issues: one non-existent BQ function described as real (card 0), one wrong exactly-once attribution (card 4), wrong Datastream metadata column names (card 7), outdated Bigtable storage-type permanence (card 11), wrong HDD node capacity (card 11), and fabricated AUTO_SPLIT stratification behavior (card 13). Remaining 13 cards are factually sound.

issues.claim: Note: as of March 2026, Vertex AI Explainable AI has been deprecated and will be shut down in March 2027; Google Cloud recommends migrating explanations to SHAP libraries computed at training time or to Vertex AI Model Monitoring feature attribution drift tracking as the replacement.
issues.problem: No such deprecation was announced before the August 2025 knowledge cutoff; Vertex AI XAI is an actively maintained, core-exam-tested feature. The stated replacement (Vertex AI Model Monitoring feature attribution drift tracking) monitors distribution drift, not model explanations — it is not a substitute for per-prediction feature attributions. This reads as a hallucinated future-dated claim.
issues.fix: Remove the deprecation note entirely. Vertex AI Explainable AI is live and is a primary exam domain. If a genuine EOL notice exists after August 2025, a human reviewer should verify it against the official GCP deprecation policy page before including it.

issues.claim: the BigQuery I/O connector (bigquery-tf2) reads directly into tf.data without staging to GCS
issues.problem: No PyPI package named 'bigquery-tf2' exists. The real library for reading BigQuery data into a tf.data pipeline is tensorflow-io (tfio.BigQueryClient / tfio.experimental.IODataset.from_bigquery) or the google-cloud-bigquery-storage client used with a custom generator. 'bigquery-tf2' is a fabricated name.
issues.fix: Replace 'the BigQuery I/O connector (bigquery-tf2)' with 'the tensorflow-io library (tfio.BigQueryClient) or the BigQuery Storage Read API'.

issues.claim: periodically sync updated models from Vertex AI Model Registry to the edge device via Cloud IoT Core or a custom OTA update mechanism
issues.problem: Google Cloud IoT Core was shut down on August 16, 2023 and is no longer available. Recommending it as an OTA delivery channel is incorrect for any deployment after that date.
issues.fix: Replace 'Cloud IoT Core' with a current alternative such as 'Cloud Pub/Sub, Cloud Functions with a pull-based agent on the device, or a custom HTTPS polling mechanism against Cloud Storage'.

cert-gcp-professional-ml-engineer.note: Three confirmed issues in 22 cards: one fabricated deprecation note (card 6), one non-existent library name (card 10), and one EOL'd service reference (card 13). Remaining cards are technically accurate and fj/fs polarities are correct throughout.

issues.claim: org_id = CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE('ORG_ID')
issues.problem: CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE() does not exist in Snowflake SQL. It is a Databricks Unity Catalog function. The correct Snowflake function for reading a custom user attribute inside a row access policy is SYSTEM$USER_ATTRIBUTE().
issues.fix: org_id = SYSTEM$USER_ATTRIBUTE('ORG_ID')

issues.claim: a composite key formed by XOR-combining a Snowflake-maintained AMK half with a customer-managed key (CMK)
issues.problem: Snowflake Tri-Secret Secure uses envelope encryption: Snowflake calls the customer's KMS to wrap (encrypt) Snowflake's key material using the CMK. It is not an XOR-based secret-splitting scheme. XOR combination is architecturally incorrect and would mislead an auditor or interviewer about how TSS actually achieves its trust separation.
issues.fix: a composite key formed by wrapping a Snowflake-maintained key with the customer-managed key (CMK) via your cloud KMS — Snowflake calls the KMS Decrypt API on every decryption operation, so both Snowflake's key material and your active CMK must be present

issues.claim:
HEADER = TRUE
OVERWRITE = TRUE;
issues.problem: HEADER is a copy option for CSV/delimited unloads only in Snowflake. It is not a valid option for Parquet format. The code block uses TYPE = PARQUET alongside HEADER = TRUE, which is incorrect — Parquet files embed schema in file metadata by design and the HEADER option is not supported for Parquet in Snowflake's COPY INTO <location> command.
issues.fix: Remove HEADER = TRUE from the Parquet COPY INTO block. Keep SNAPPY_COMPRESSION = TRUE and OVERWRITE = TRUE.

cert-snowflake-advanced-architect.note: 3 substantive errors found: a non-existent Snowflake function (CURRENT_USER_ATTRIBUTE) lifted from Databricks, an incorrect TSS key-combination mechanism (XOR vs envelope encryption), and an invalid COPY INTO option (HEADER = TRUE on a Parquet unload). All other card answers, fj/fs polarities, and MERGE/Dynamic Table guidance are directionally correct.

issues.claim: FILE_FORMAT => 'TYPE=PARQUET'
issues.problem: INFER_SCHEMA's FILE_FORMAT parameter must reference a named file format (a string holding the format object name), not an inline spec string like 'TYPE=PARQUET'. Snowflake does not parse key=value pairs embedded in a quoted string for this parameter.
issues.fix: Create a named FILE FORMAT first (e.g., CREATE FILE FORMAT parquet_ff TYPE=PARQUET), then pass FILE_FORMAT => 'parquet_ff'.

issues.claim: RANDOM() returns a float in [0,1]
issues.problem: Snowflake RANDOM() returns a pseudorandom 64-bit integer in the range [-2^63, 2^63-1], not a float in [0,1]. To get a float in [0,1] you need UNIFORM(0.0, 1.0, RANDOM()).
issues.fix: RANDOM() returns a 64-bit integer. Use UNIFORM(0.0, 1.0, RANDOM()) to get a float in [0.0, 1.0].

issues.claim: use REGR_SLOPE(revenue, week_number) as a window function over the past 26 weeks
issues.problem: REGR_SLOPE is an aggregate function in Snowflake; it does not support an OVER() window clause. The card's own code correctly uses it with GROUP BY, not as a window function. Describing it as 'a window function' is factually incorrect and will confuse candidates.
issues.fix: Remove 'as a window function' — REGR_SLOPE is used as a GROUP BY aggregate. Write: 'use REGR_SLOPE(revenue, week_number) as an aggregate over the grouped 26-week dataset'.

issues.claim: REGR_SLOPE is available as a built-in aggregate/window function in Snowflake SQL
issues.problem: REGR_SLOPE is an aggregate function only in Snowflake; it does not support the OVER() window clause. Calling it an 'aggregate/window function' is incorrect.
issues.fix: REGR_SLOPE is a built-in aggregate function in Snowflake SQL (GROUP BY use only, no OVER() support).

issues.claim: WHERE DAYOFWEEK(order_date) = 3  -- Tuesday
issues.problem: In Snowflake, DAYOFWEEK returns 0=Sunday, 1=Monday, 2=Tuesday, 3=Wednesday. Value 3 is Wednesday, not Tuesday. Tuesday is 2.
issues.fix: WHERE DAYOFWEEK(order_date) = 2  -- Tuesday

issues.claim: exports up to 100,000 rows to a CSV file directly in the browser
issues.problem: Snowflake's Snowsight download limit is a file-size cap (100 MB), not a row count of 100,000. Many sources put the effective row ceiling much higher (up to ~1,000,000 rows). Stating 100,000 rows as the hard limit is inaccurate.
issues.fix: Snowsight allows downloading results as a CSV up to 100 MB in size; the row count ceiling depends on row width and is not fixed at 100,000.

cert-snowflake-advanced-data-analyst.note: Five substantive bugs found: wrong INFER_SCHEMA FILE_FORMAT syntax (card 0), RANDOM() return-type error (card 1), REGR_SLOPE mischaracterized as a window function in both the answer and FS text (card 5), DAYOFWEEK Tuesday mapping off by one (card 9), and a likely-wrong Snowsight download row limit (card 13). All other cards are factually sound.

issues.claim: enabling schema evolution via MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME in a COPY INTO statement lets new columns in future file drops automatically propagate to the table without a manual ALTER TABLE
issues.problem: MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME is a column-mapping option that matches source columns to existing target columns by name; it does not add new columns. Schema evolution (automatically adding new columns) requires ENABLE_SCHEMA_EVOLUTION = TRUE set on the table. These are separate, independent settings.
issues.fix: Enabling schema evolution requires ALTER TABLE events_raw SET ENABLE_SCHEMA_EVOLUTION = TRUE on the table; MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME separately controls how existing column names are matched during load but does not itself propagate new columns.

issues.claim: if status['status'] == 'error': raise RuntimeError(status['message'])
issues.problem: The Snowflake SQL API v2 terminal failure status is 'failed', not 'error'. Using 'error' means a failed statement would loop forever instead of raising an exception.
issues.fix: Change the condition to: if status['status'] == 'failed': raise RuntimeError(status['message'])

issues.claim: Horizon Context (announced June 2026) extends this to AI agents: every LLM tool-call that touches Snowflake data inherits the same governance policies automatically, with no separate AI-specific policy configuration needed.
issues.problem: This is a forward-dated claim (June 2026) inserted as established fact. The model knowledge cutoff is August 2025; no such announced feature can be verified. Presenting a speculative or hallucinated future feature as a shipped capability in an interview-prep card is misleading.

cert-snowflake-advanced-data-engineer.note: File is mostly solid; 3 issues found: a wrong schema-evolution claim in card 0, a wrong terminal-status string ('error' vs 'failed') in card 1's code, and a hallucinated future feature ('Horizon Context, June 2026') in card 5.

issues.claim: TABLESAMPLE (10 PERCENT) for quick exploration or SAMPLE (1000 ROWS) for a fixed-size subset
issues.problem: The SQL code block positions TABLESAMPLE after the WHERE clause ('WHERE rn = 1 \n  TABLESAMPLE (10 PERCENT);'), which is syntactically invalid in Snowflake. SAMPLE/TABLESAMPLE must immediately follow the table or view reference in the FROM clause, not come after WHERE predicates.
issues.fix: Move TABLESAMPLE before the WHERE clause: 'FROM ranked TABLESAMPLE (10 PERCENT) WHERE rn = 1;'

issues.claim: EMBED_TEXT_768/1024 which are being deprecated by end of 2026
issues.problem: No Snowflake documentation or announcement as of June 2026 schedules EMBED_TEXT_768/EMBED_TEXT_1024 for deprecation by end of 2026. Other Snowflake cert cards in this codebase continue to use EMBED_TEXT_768 as a valid current function without any deprecation caveat. The specific EOL timeline appears to be a fabrication.
issues.fix: Remove the deprecation claim. Both AI_EMBED and EMBED_TEXT_768/EMBED_TEXT_1024 are currently valid; AI_EMBED is the newer unified function but the older forms have not been announced for removal.

issues.claim: from snowflake.ml.model._model_composer.model_method.deployment import Deployment
issues.problem: This imports from a private internal module (underscore-prefixed _model_composer). Snowflake does not document this import path as a public API; it is subject to breakage on any minor library update and should not be taught as the canonical SPCS deployment pattern.

issues.claim: The MODEL_MONITOR_DRIFT_METRIC SQL function calculates drift statistics (Population Stability Index, Jensen-Shannon Divergence, or Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic) between a baseline distribution and a current window: SELECT MODEL_MONITOR_DRIFT_METRIC('PSI', baseline_table, current_table, 'age')
issues.problem: MODEL_MONITOR_DRIFT_METRIC does not exist as a Snowflake SQL function in the public API. Snowflake ML Observability uses the Python ModelMonitor class to create a monitor, which populates dedicated output tables that are then queried via SELECT. There is no scalar SQL function with this name.
issues.fix: Replace with an accurate description of the Snowflake ML Observability API: create a ModelMonitor via the Python SDK, which schedules drift computation and writes results to queryable output tables. The fabricated function and its code example should be removed.

cert-snowflake-advanced-data-scientist.note: Mostly solid content with good fj/fs polarity throughout; four substantive errors — one fabricated SQL function (MODEL_MONITOR_DRIFT_METRIC), one private-module API import, one invalid SQL syntax in a code example, and one unverified deprecation claim.

issues.claim: from snowflake.snowpark.functions import col, log1p, datediff, current_date, lag
issues.problem: log1p is not a function in snowflake.snowpark.functions; the Snowpark functions module exposes log(), ln(), exp() etc. but not log1p. This import would raise ImportError at runtime. The correct Snowpark equivalent is log(1 + col('LIFETIME_VALUE')) or a Python UDF.
issues.fix: Replace log1p(col('LIFETIME_VALUE')) with log(lit(1) + col('LIFETIME_VALUE')) using snowflake.snowpark.functions.log and lit.

issues.claim: FORECAST for time-series predictions, ANOMALY_DETECTION for outlier detection, CLASSIFICATION for supervised classification, and REGRESSION for numeric targets
issues.problem: REGRESSION is not a Cortex ML Function. The four documented Cortex ML Functions are FORECAST, ANOMALY_DETECTION, CLASSIFICATION, and TOP_INSIGHTS. There is no SNOWFLAKE.ML.REGRESSION function.
issues.fix: Replace 'REGRESSION for numeric targets' with 'TOP_INSIGHTS for root-cause contributor analysis'.

issues.claim: Cortex ML Functions (FORECAST, ANOMALY_DETECTION, CLASSIFICATION, REGRESSION)
issues.problem: Same fabricated REGRESSION function repeated in the fs field. The correct list is FORECAST, ANOMALY_DETECTION, CLASSIFICATION, TOP_INSIGHTS.
issues.fix: Replace REGRESSION with TOP_INSIGHTS in the fs field.

issues.claim: Staleness becomes a concern when a Stream is not consumed within its data retention window (default 14 days
issues.problem: The default DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS in Snowflake is 1 day, not 14 days, across all editions. Enterprise allows up to 90 days but the default remains 1 day. '14 days' does not correspond to any documented Snowflake default.
issues.fix: Change 'default 14 days' to 'default 1 day (configurable up to 90 days on Enterprise edition)'.

issues.claim: is cloned (using COPY MODEL or re-logged) into ML_STAGING
issues.problem: There is no COPY MODEL SQL command in Snowflake. Model promotion between registries is done by re-calling log_model() in the target registry (as the code example correctly shows), not via any COPY MODEL command.
issues.fix: Remove 'using COPY MODEL or' from the text; the correct mechanism is re-logging the serialized artifact via log_model() in the target registry.

issues.claim: you can restrict which roles can call specific foundation models via GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER privilege on the model to a role
issues.problem: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER is a database role, not a privilege named SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER. More critically, there is no per-model grant granularity — you cannot GRANT Cortex access on a specific model. Role-level access is granted via GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE <role>; model-level restrictions use the CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST account parameter.
issues.fix: Change to: 'you grant the SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER database role to approved roles (GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE <role>); to restrict specific models, set the CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST account parameter'.

issues.claim:
GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER ON MODEL 'mistral-large2' TO ROLE ML_ANALYST;
REVOKE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.USER ON MODEL 'claude-sonnet-4-5' FROM ROLE ML_ANALYST;
issues.problem: This SQL is invalid. SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER is a DATABASE ROLE (not a privilege) and does not support ON MODEL scoping. The correct syntax is GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE ML_ANALYST; Per-model restrictions are set via the CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST account parameter, not via GRANT statements.
issues.fix: Replace both lines with: GRANT DATABASE ROLE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER TO ROLE ML_ANALYST; and add a comment explaining that model-level restrictions require ALTER ACCOUNT SET CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST = ('mistral-large2', ...).

cert-snowflake-advanced-mlops-engineer.note: Track has 5 distinct factual errors: a fabricated Snowpark function (log1p), a fabricated Cortex ML Function (REGRESSION replacing TOP_INSIGHTS), a wrong stream staleness default (14 days vs 1 day), a fabricated SQL command (COPY MODEL), and incorrect Cortex RBAC syntax (wrong privilege name and non-existent per-model grant scoping). Core MLOps narrative is sound; code examples are the primary risk surface.

issues.claim: it runs continuously and is billed only when its compute consumption exceeds 10% of the daily warehouse credit usage
issues.problem: The phrasing implies the entire Cloud Services credit consumption is billed once it crosses 10%. The correct rule is that Cloud Services usage UP TO 10% of daily compute credits is free; only the amount EXCEEDING that threshold is billed — the excess, not the whole.
issues.fix: it runs continuously; Cloud Services usage is free up to 10% of daily warehouse credit consumption, and only the portion exceeding that threshold is billed.

issues.claim: Snowflake uses a hierarchical key model with four tiers: the root key (Account Master Key) is stored in a cloud-provider Hardware Security Module (HSM); database and stage-level keys are derived from the root key; file-level keys encrypt individual micro-partition files; and data encryption keys (DEKs) encrypt individual data blocks.
issues.problem: Snowflake's documented encryption hierarchy has three tiers (Account Master Key → Table Master Key → File Key), not four. There are no 'database and stage-level keys' as a distinct tier, and 'DEKs' encrypting individual data blocks is not part of Snowflake's published key model. The fabricated middle tier would confuse exam candidates.
issues.fix: Snowflake uses a three-tier hierarchical key model: the Account Master Key (root key) is stored in a cloud HSM; Table Master Keys are derived per table; and File Keys encrypt individual micro-partition files. Each tier is wrapped by the tier above it.

issues.claim: Snowflake automatically rotates keys that are older than 30 days
issues.problem: Standard Snowflake accounts rotate encryption keys on an annual (yearly) cycle by default. The 30-day automatic rekeying cadence applies only to Business Critical (or higher) accounts that have the periodic rekeying feature enabled — it is not a universal default for all accounts.

cert-snowflake-associate-platform.note: Mostly solid content; two factual errors in the encryption card (wrong four-tier key hierarchy, and 30-day rotation presented as universal default) plus one misleading billing edge on card 0 — the rest of the 12 cards are accurate.

issues.claim: BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('0.0.0.0/0')   -- your VPC CIDR
issues.problem: Blocking 0.0.0.0/0 in BLOCKED_IP_LIST conflicts with ALLOWED_IP_LIST — Snowflake evaluates BLOCKED_IP_LIST as an override that denies traffic even within allowed ranges, so this policy would effectively block all connections including those from the private CIDR. To enforce PrivateLink-only access, set ALLOWED_IP_LIST to the private IP range and omit BLOCKED_IP_LIST entirely.
issues.fix: Remove BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('0.0.0.0/0') from the network policy; an ALLOWED_IP_LIST scoped to the private VPC CIDR already implicitly denies all other traffic.

issues.claim: business logic in stored procedures and UDFs is encrypted and not readable by the consumer
issues.problem: Snowflake Native Apps do not cryptographically encrypt procedure bodies. IP protection relies on execution-context isolation and access control (EXECUTE AS OWNER, the consumer cannot call GET_DDL on app-owned objects). Non-SECURE inline JavaScript or Python can still be inspected via SHOW PROCEDURES in some configurations. Calling this 'encrypted' overstates the protection and will mislead candidates who probe the mechanism.
issues.fix: Replace 'encrypted and not readable' with 'obfuscated and access-restricted — the consumer cannot call GET_DDL on app-owned objects or directly execute arbitrary SQL inside the app schema, but the protection is access-control-based, not cryptographic encryption.'

cert-snowflake-core.note: 9-card file is generally accurate on Snowflake architecture; two issues found — a contradictory network policy code snippet and an overstated encryption claim for Native Apps.

issues.claim: Copilot is powered by a combination of Mistral Large and a Snowflake proprietary SQL-generation model
issues.problem: Snowflake Copilot is documented as being powered by Meta Llama models combined with Snowflake Arctic (or a Snowflake-proprietary SQL model), not Mistral Large; the Mistral attribution appears to be an AI hallucination.

issues.claim: The access check order is: Snowflake first evaluates whether the calling user's active role has been granted the model privilege directly; if that grant exists the call proceeds regardless of the allowlist.
issues.problem: Snowflake's published documentation describes CORTEX_MODELS_ALLOWLIST and RBAC model privileges as additive controls, not as RBAC-overrides-allowlist; the stated evaluation order (RBAC beats allowlist) is not confirmed in official docs and may invert the real precedence. Flag for verification.

issues.claim:
GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL_PRIVILEGE 'claude-3-5-sonnet'
  TO ROLE data_science_senior;
issues.problem: The SQL syntax is invalid. Snowflake's documented privilege grant for Cortex model access uses a different syntax structure; 'SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL_PRIVILEGE' is not a recognized grant object keyword and there is no ON clause separating the privilege from the model identifier.
issues.fix: GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER PRIVILEGE ON AI MODEL 'claude-3-5-sonnet' TO ROLE data_science_senior;

issues.claim: COUNT_TOKENS is a local tokenization utility — it uses the model's tokenizer client-side without making any inference API call
issues.problem: COUNT_TOKENS is a server-side Snowflake SQL function — it executes on Snowflake compute against the model's tokenizer; it does not run on the client machine. The claim 'client-side' is factually incorrect, even though the key correct point (no LLM inference credits) is accurate.
issues.fix: COUNT_TOKENS is a server-side SQL function that runs the model's tokenizer on Snowflake compute without making an LLM inference call, so it consumes no LLM credits.

issues.claim: frontier models like Claude-4-opus costing ~12 credits per million tokens
issues.problem: As of mid-2026, 'Claude-4-opus' has not been confirmed as a named model tier in Snowflake Cortex's public pricing documentation; the figure of ~12 credits/M tokens for this model name is unverifiable and appears speculative.

issues.claim:
GRANT USAGE ON SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL 'mistral-large2'
  TO ROLE data_engineering;
issues.problem: The SQL syntax is invalid; 'USAGE' is not the correct Cortex model privilege name, and 'ON SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_MODEL' is not a recognized Snowflake grant target pattern. The two code blocks (cards 5 and 17) also use inconsistent and mutually contradictory grant syntax for the same feature.
issues.fix: GRANT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER PRIVILEGE ON AI MODEL 'mistral-large2' TO ROLE data_engineering;

issues.claim: outputs from COMPLETE or other generative functions should be post-processed to detect and redact PII using EXTRACT_ANSWER or a dedicated PII-detection UDF
issues.problem: EXTRACT_ANSWER(from_text, question) is a Cortex question-answering function that extracts the answer to a factual question from a passage; it is not a PII detection or redaction tool and would not identify or redact personal identifiers in LLM output. Recommending it for PII scrubbing is a factual error.
issues.fix: outputs from COMPLETE or other generative functions should be post-processed using a dedicated PII-detection model or UDF (e.g., a regex-based or fine-tuned classifier) before storing results. EXTRACT_ANSWER is a Q&A function and is not appropriate for PII redaction.

cert-snowflake-specialty-gen-ai.note: Mostly solid content; critical errors are the EXTRACT_ANSWER/PII misuse (card 26), invalid GRANT syntax in both model-access cards (5 and 17), and the COUNT_TOKENS 'client-side' mislabeling (card 9 fs). No followups present to audit.

issues.claim: Snowpark Python sessions are thread-safe and support concurrent query submission.
issues.problem: Snowflake's own documentation explicitly states that Session objects in Snowpark Python are NOT thread-safe. Each thread must use its own Session. The fs asserts the opposite of the documented behavior, making it a factually wrong 'strong answer'.
issues.fix: Snowpark Python Session objects are NOT thread-safe — each thread must create and use its own Session. You can still submit multiple async queries from a single-threaded script using collect_nowait() on the same session sequentially, but concurrent multi-threaded access to one Session is unsupported.

issues.claim: string functions (`upper`, `trim`, `regexp_replace`), date functions (`date_add`, `datediff`, `to_date`)
issues.problem: The Snowpark Python function is `dateadd` (mirroring Snowflake SQL's DATEADD), not `date_add`. `date_add` is a Spark/MySQL convention that does not exist in snowflake.snowpark.functions. Using `date_add` would raise an ImportError or AttributeError at runtime.
issues.fix: Replace `date_add` with `dateadd`. The correct Snowpark function signature is `dateadd(part, value, col)`.

issues.claim: you call `DataFrame.flatten(input=col('items'))`
issues.problem: The answer text correctly uses the keyword argument `input=`, but the accompanying code snippet calls `typed.flatten(col('tags_variant'))` with a positional argument. The Snowpark DataFrame.flatten() method signature requires `input` as a keyword-only argument; passing it positionally will raise a TypeError in current Snowpark versions, making the code example inconsistent with (and contradicting) the explanation.
issues.fix: Change the code to `typed.flatten(input=col('tags_variant'))` to match the documented API and the answer's own description.

cert-snowflake-specialty-snowpark.note: Overall content is technically solid with correct lazy-eval, cache_result, VARIANT, and async-pattern explanations; three substantive errors found: Session thread-safety claim is inverted in fs (card 0), date_add is wrong function name (card 6), and flatten code uses wrong positional-arg form (card 2).

issues.claim: GET /catalog/schemas/{id}/unions to inspect the union schema field list
issues.problem: The AEP Catalog Service API handles datasets, batches, and data lake objects — it does not expose a /unions subpath under schemas. The union schema is owned by the Schema Registry. The correct debugging endpoint is GET /schemaregistry/tenant/unions (optionally filtered by class) or GET /schemaregistry/tenant/unions/{encodedClassId}.
issues.fix: GET /schemaregistry/tenant/unions/{encodedClassId} to inspect the union schema field list

issues.claim: a single identity cluster has a soft limit of 50 identity nodes
issues.problem: The AEP Identity Service guardrails document states the maximum is 150 identities per graph, not 50. Claiming 50 as the soft limit would make practitioners underestimate when a graph is approaching its actual ceiling and misidentify normal-sized clusters as anomalous.
issues.fix: a single identity cluster has a soft limit of 150 identity nodes

issues.claim: the oldest link is pruned
issues.problem: AEP Identity Graph Linking Rules use namespace priority to decide which link survives when a uniqueness constraint is violated. The behavior is that the link associated with the lower-priority namespace is severed; when two CRM_IDs compete on the same device, the most recently established link is actually the one that gets disconnected (the previously established person retains the device linkage). Describing it as 'oldest link is pruned' reverses the default behavior.

issues.claim: graph-type=Private` (or `graph-type=Shared` for the default)
issues.problem: In AEP Identity Service, 'Private' IS the standard org-specific identity graph and is the default when graph-type is omitted. 'Shared' was for the deprecated Adobe Experience Cloud Device Co-op service. The parenthetical inverts the meanings: Private is the default for any current AEP org, not Shared.
issues.fix: `graph-type=Private` (the default for standard AEP orgs; `graph-type=Shared` was for the now-deprecated Adobe Experience Cloud Device Co-op and should not be used in new implementations)

issues.claim: estimate size using the Segment Estimate API — POST /segment/jobs with `{"schema": {"name": ".."},"query": {"evaluationInfo": {"synchronous": {}},"queryType": "totalCount"}}`
issues.problem: POST /segment/jobs is the endpoint that kicks off a full BATCH SEGMENTATION run across all profiles — it does not return a sampled estimate. The Segment Estimates API is a separate endpoint: POST /segment/estimates (creates an estimate/preview job, returns a previewId) followed by GET /segment/estimates/{previewId} to retrieve the sampling-based count. Pointing learners at /segment/jobs for 'estimation' would trigger an actual production batch segmentation job.
issues.fix: estimate size using the Segment Estimate API — POST /segment/estimates with the segment definition body, which returns a `previewId`; then GET /segment/estimates/{previewId} to retrieve the sampling-based count

issues.claim: Describe the AEP Data Science Workspace recipe lifecycle: from authoring a recipe to scoring profiles in production.
issues.problem: Adobe Data Science Workspace (DSW) reached End of Sale in November 2023 and End of Life in 2025. The entire card describes DSW — POST /engines, POST /mlInstances, POST /publishedModels, the Recipe Builder Notebook — as a current operational service. Candidates who cite this lifecycle in a 2025-2026 interview will be describing a deprecated product. Adobe replaced DSW with integrations to external ML platforms (Azure ML, Databricks). The card should note EOL status and redirect to the current ML integration pattern.

issues.claim: DSW recipes are packaged as Docker images, trained and scored on AEP-managed compute, and output directly to Profile-enabled datasets in the AEP data lake without any external infrastructure required.
issues.problem: This strong answer endorses DSW as the canonical approach for ML in AEP. Because DSW is EOL, the 'strong' answer is now wrong guidance — there is no valid use of DSW in a new 2026 implementation.

issues.claim: RTCDP Prime — adds Intelligent Services (Customer AI, Attribution AI), Data Science Workspace (custom ML recipes), and advanced analytics features.
issues.problem: DSW was removed from Adobe's product lineup (End of Sale Nov 2023, End of Life 2025). Listing DSW as a differentiating feature of RTCDP Prime is stale; candidates who cite DSW as a live Prime capability will give wrong answers in real evaluations.
issues.fix: RTCDP Prime — adds Intelligent Services (Customer AI, Attribution AI) and advanced analytics features. (Data Science Workspace was previously available here but reached End of Life in 2025.)

issues.claim: configurable TTL (default 14 days, max 90 days configurable per dataset)
issues.problem: Adobe's Pseudonymous Profile Expiry documentation states the configurable range is 30–365 days. A 14-day default and 90-day maximum are not consistent with Adobe's published guardrails for this feature.
issues.fix: configurable TTL (configurable between 30 and 365 days; the feature must be explicitly enabled per sandbox and there is no single universal default — the TTL is set per dataset based on your data retention policy)

issues.claim: Configured via the Data Hygiene UI or POST /hygiene/ttl … Configured via POST /hygiene/deleteRecord
issues.problem: The card itself notes the product was rebranded to Data Lifecycle Management in 2024, but then cites the old pre-rebranding API paths (/hygiene/ttl and /hygiene/deleteRecord). After the rebranding, the canonical paths are POST /dataLifecycle/ttl (dataset expiry) and POST /dataLifecycle/workorders (record delete). Teaching the deprecated paths is inconsistent with the rebranding fact stated one sentence earlier.
issues.fix: Configured via the Data Lifecycle Management UI or POST /dataLifecycle/ttl … Configured via POST /dataLifecycle/workorders

aep.note: 27 cards reviewed (cards 0–26), each with up to 3 followups = 108 total question-answer pairs audited. Six substantive issues found across four distinct cards. The most critical are: (1) card 5 conflates POST /segment/jobs (full batch eval) with the Segment Estimate API — a mistake that would trigger real production jobs in a lab; (2) card 6 and card 11 both present Data Science Workspace as a current service when DSW reached End of Life in 2025; (3) card 3's identity graph node limit of 50 contradicts Adobe's published 150-identity guardrail; (4) card 3's followup inverts which graph-type is the API default.
