The rest of the content — schema design, DULE, identity stitching mechanics, Journey Optimizer, Sandbox Tooling, Query Service, Computed Attributes, Profile fragments, B2B Edition, lookalike audiences — is accurate and well-described at the stated levels.

issues.claim: if result.docs and float(result.docs[0].dist) >= THRESHOLD:
issues.problem: Redis KNN with a COSINE distance metric returns a cosine distance value (0 = identical, 2 = fully opposite), not a cosine similarity. The check `dist >= 0.95` therefore triggers a cache hit when the vectors are 95%+ distant (nearly opposite), meaning the cache never correctly serves similar queries. For a correctly configured cosine-distance index the check must be `dist <= (1 - THRESHOLD)` i.e. `dist <= 0.05`. Alternatively, use an IP (inner product) metric on normalized vectors, rename the variable to `score`, and keep `score >= THRESHOLD`.
issues.fix: if result.docs and float(result.docs[0].dist) <= (1 - THRESHOLD):

issues.claim: content filters (toxic, hate, sexual, violence — each with a 1-5 strength threshold)
issues.problem: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails content filter strength uses a four-value enum — NONE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH — not a 1-to-5 numeric scale. The API parameter is `inputStrength` / `outputStrength` with string values. Describing it as '1-5' will mislead engineers configuring the API or Terraform.
issues.fix: content filters (toxic, hate, sexual, violence — each with a NONE / LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH strength setting)

issues.claim: Run the guardrail at each strength level (1-5) and measure false-positive rate
issues.problem: Same factual error as the main answer: Bedrock Guardrails content filter strengths are NONE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH — not numeric 1-5 levels. The calibration procedure described is otherwise sound.
issues.fix: Run the guardrail at each strength level (NONE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH) and measure false-positive rate

issues.claim: ToolCorrectnessness (did it call the right tools with correct arguments?)
issues.problem: Double-'ness' typo: the actual DeepEval metric class is ToolCorrectnessMetric (single 'ness'). The same answer correctly uses 'ToolCorrectnessMetric' in its code block, confirming the prose spelling is wrong.
issues.fix: ToolCorrectness (did it call the right tools with correct arguments?)

issues.claim: ActionSequenceOrder (was the order of tool calls logical?)
issues.problem: There is no DeepEval metric named ActionSequenceOrder. The card presents it as one of three real agent-specific DeepEval metrics alongside TaskCompletion and ToolCorrectness. DeepEval's agent-oriented metrics include TaskCompletionMetric, ToolCorrectnessMetric, ConversationCompletenessMetric, and ConversationRelevancyMetric — not ActionSequenceOrder.

issues.claim: Claude 3.5 and Claude 3 Opus are the dominant choices for production agents in early 2026.
issues.problem: By early 2026 the dominant Anthropic models for agents are Claude 3.7 Sonnet (launched February 2025) and the Claude 4 generation (claude-sonnet-4-5, claude-haiku-4-5, launched 2025). 'Claude 3.5' and 'Claude 3 Opus' are both superseded; presenting them as dominant in 2026 will send learners to the wrong models.

issues.claim: Bedrock supports Amazon Titan Embed v2 (up to 8,192 input tokens, 1,536 dimensions)
issues.problem: Amazon Titan Embed Text v2 (amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0) produces 1,024 dimensions by default and supports 256 or 512 as well. 1,536 dimensions belongs to Titan Embed Text v1 (and OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002). Misstating the v2 dimension count will cause misconfigured vector stores and index incompatibilities.
issues.fix: Bedrock supports Amazon Titan Embed v2 (up to 8,192 input tokens, 1,024 dimensions — configurable to 256 or 512)

issues.claim: No. As of early 2026, Claude's prompt caching applies only to the system prompt and prefix of the human turn. Thinking blocks are generated per request and cannot be cached or replayed.
issues.problem: This is incorrect as of 2025. Anthropic's API supports including thinking blocks from a previous assistant turn in the messages array for multi-turn conversations, and those turns are eligible for prompt caching. Thinking blocks CAN be cached when they appear in the cached prefix of a conversation. The claim that they 'cannot be cached or replayed' will cause engineers to over-pay for thinking on multi-turn agentic sessions.

issues.claim: Titan Embed v2 offers 1536-dimensional embeddings with 8192 token context
issues.problem: Same error as card 44: Amazon Titan Embed Text v2 produces 1,024 dimensions by default (256 and 512 also configurable). 1,536 dimensions is the v1 output size.
issues.fix: Titan Embed v2 offers 1024-dimensional embeddings (256/512 also supported) with 8192 token context

agentic-ai.note: 144 top-level cards audited plus all followups. Nine issues found across eight cards. Most critical: (1) Bedrock Guardrails strength scale is NONE/LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH not 1-5 (cards 33 body and followup); (2) Titan Embed v2 dimension count wrong as 1,536 — correct is 1,024 (cards 44 and 64 followup); (3) 'ActionSequenceOrder' is a hallucinated DeepEval metric name (card 37); (4) Redis KNN cosine distance check is inverted in the TokenOps code example (card 21 — cache would never hit for similar vectors). The thinking-block caching claim (card 56 followup) is flagged medium-confidence for human review as the Anthropic API behaviour changed in 2025.

issues.claim: On Anthropic and OpenAI, cache hits can reduce cost 80–90% for repeated static prefixes.
issues.problem: OpenAI prompt caching gives a 50% discount on cached input tokens, not 80–90%. Only Anthropic's cache reads are priced at ~10% of the base input token price (a ~90% discount). Stating 80–90% for both providers is factually wrong for OpenAI.
issues.fix: On Anthropic, cache reads are priced at ~10% of the base input token price (~90% savings). On OpenAI, prompt cache hits reduce input token cost by ~50%. Structuring the agent prompt with the stable part first maximizes cache hit rates.

issues.claim: Extended thinking (available on Claude 3.7 and above) generates reasoning in a separate internal scratchpad that does not count toward the output token budget and is not included in the context passed to the next turn — it is invisible to downstream steps and cheaper at inference time.
issues.problem: Extended thinking tokens ARE output tokens and ARE billed by Anthropic. The claim 'does not count toward the output token budget' is wrong. Additionally, 'cheaper at inference time' is wrong — enabling extended thinking makes the current invocation more expensive (more output tokens generated). The real benefit is that thinking tokens do not appear in subsequent turns' input context, so they do not compound across a long agent session.
issues.fix: Extended thinking (available on Claude 3.7 and above) generates reasoning in a separate internal scratchpad that is billed as output tokens but is not included in the context passed to the next turn — it is invisible to downstream steps. Unlike standard CoT, the thinking tokens do not accumulate in the input of each subsequent turn, so they do not compound costs over a long session.

issues.claim: Extended thinking on Claude 3.7+ keeps reasoning in a separate scratchpad — not in the context, not billed to downstream turns.
issues.problem: 'Not billed to downstream turns' is technically accurate (thinking tokens do not appear in the next turn's input) but is misleadingly framed to imply extended thinking has no billing cost. Thinking tokens are billed as output tokens on the current turn; they are simply absent from the next turn's input.

issues.claim: The SnapStart feature (2022) pre-boots a VM and takes a memory snapshot, restoring the snapshot on invocation to cut start latency to ~10ms.
issues.problem: AWS Lambda SnapStart (announced Nov 2022) is a Lambda-level product feature that snapshots the initialized JVM state for Java functions — it is not a generic Firecracker microVM capability. Presenting it as a Firecracker feature a team can apply to arbitrary agent containers is misleading. Firecracker does expose a snapshot/restore API, but that is a distinct lower-level mechanism, and SnapStart specifically is a Java/JVM Lambda feature.

issues.claim: For production use DatabaseSessionService (Cloud Firestore or Postgres via SQLAlchemy) or VertexAiSessionService for Agent Engine deployments.
issues.problem: DatabaseSessionService in Google ADK uses SQLAlchemy and supports only SQL databases (Postgres, SQLite, MySQL, etc.). It does not support Cloud Firestore. Firestore-backed sessions use VertexAiSessionService, not DatabaseSessionService.
issues.fix: For production use DatabaseSessionService (Postgres or other SQL databases via SQLAlchemy) or VertexAiSessionService for Agent Engine deployments and Firestore-backed managed sessions.

issues.claim: svc = DatabaseSessionService(db_url='firestore://my-project/my-collection')
issues.problem: DatabaseSessionService accepts a SQLAlchemy connection URL. 'firestore://' is not a valid SQLAlchemy URL scheme and will fail at runtime. The code example would need a valid SQLAlchemy URL such as 'postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@host/dbname' to function. For Firestore, VertexAiSessionService is the correct class.
issues.fix: svc = DatabaseSessionService(db_url='postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@host/dbname')

issues.claim: Each receives a context and can return a modified version or raise CallbackAction.SKIP to abort the step.
issues.problem: Google ADK callbacks use a return-value-based skip mechanism, not exceptions. For before_tool_call, returning a non-None dict bypasses the tool and uses that dict as the result. For before_model_call, returning an LlmResponse skips the model call. There is no 'raise CallbackAction.SKIP' API in google-adk. The code example in the same card correctly demonstrates the return-based pattern, contradicting this prose description.
issues.fix: Each receives a context and can return a modified version or return a non-None value to abort and substitute the step's result — before_tool_call returns a dict to skip tool execution, before_model_call returns an LlmResponse to skip the model call.

issues.claim: 'schema': [f.name for f in rows[0]._xxx_values] if rows else [],
issues.problem: rows[0]._xxx_values is a non-existent private attribute on a google.cloud.bigquery Row object. This code raises AttributeError at runtime. The correct way to extract field names from a BigQuery result row is rows[0].keys().
issues.fix: 'schema': list(rows[0].keys()) if rows else [],

agents.note: 152 cards audited across all levels (Jr through Principal). The file is generally well-structured and factually solid on agent architecture, MCP, multi-agent patterns, LangGraph, RabbitMQ, and containerization content. Eight substantive issues were found, concentrated in three areas: (1) Anthropic API specifics — extended thinking billing is systematically misrepresented in card 58; (2) Google ADK implementation details — DatabaseSessionService does not support Firestore (card 111), CallbackAction.SKIP does not exist as a raise-based API (card 115), and the BigQuery schema extraction code uses a non-existent private attribute (card 118); (3) Cross-provider caching discounts — the 80–90% figure is Anthropic-only; OpenAI is 50% (card 8). The Firecracker SnapStart issue (card 104) is medium-confidence: SnapStart is technically a Lambda-layer product, not a raw Firecracker API, but the underlying snapshot capability exists in Firecracker itself.

issues.claim: Tools like LinearB (previously GitPrime) or Jira's velocity report expose cycle time and WIP trends
issues.problem: LinearB and GitPrime are two separate companies and products. GitPrime was acquired by Pluralsight in 2019 and rebranded as Pluralsight Flow. LinearB is an independent engineering analytics company (acquired Waydev in 2022) and has no historical connection to GitPrime.
issues.fix: Tools like Pluralsight Flow (formerly GitPrime), LinearB, or Jira's velocity report expose cycle time and WIP trends

issues.claim: EAC = BAC/CPI = $549,450 — $49,450 over budget
issues.problem: The arithmetic is wrong. BAC/CPI = 500,000 / (200,000/220,000) = 500,000 × 1.1 = $550,000, not $549,450. The over-budget figure should be $50,000, not $49,450.
issues.fix: EAC = BAC/CPI = $550,000 — $50,000 over budget

issues.claim: the project is getting $0.91 of value per dollar spent, meaning it is 9% over budget
issues.problem: CPI = 0.9091 does not mean 9% cost overrun. The cost overrun is (AC − EV)/EV = (220,000 − 200,000)/200,000 = 10%, not 9%. Equivalently, (1 − CPI)/CPI = 10%.
issues.fix: the project is getting $0.91 of value per dollar spent, meaning it is approximately 10% over budget

issues.claim: PMI's PMBOK 7th edition recommends presenting both metrics together because a project can have SPI > 1 (ahead of schedule on hours) while CPI < 1 (over budget)
issues.problem: PMBOK 7th edition (2021) is a principles-based guide (12 principles, 8 performance domains) and does not contain prescriptive EVM metric presentation guidance. Detailed EVM recommendations — including presenting CPI and SPI together — are in PMBOK 6th edition (2017, Chapter 7) and the PMI Practice Standard for Earned Value Management. Citing the 7th edition as the source of this specific recommendation is factually incorrect.
issues.fix: PMI's PMBOK 6th edition and the PMI Practice Standard for Earned Value Management recommend presenting both metrics together because a project can have SPI > 1 (ahead of schedule on hours) while CPI < 1 (over budget)

issues.claim: EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)/CPI = $714,286 + $476,190 = $1,190,476 (same as formula 1 for this case)
issues.problem: The parenthetical 'for this case' implies this equality is a numerical coincidence. It is not — EAC3 = AC + (BAC−EV)/CPI is algebraically identical to EAC1 = BAC/CPI for every possible set of inputs (since AC = EV/CPI, substituting gives BAC/CPI). Presenting them as two separate formulas that yield different forecasts, then noting they happen to match here, will confuse learners and lead them to believe these are distinct forecasts. PMBOK 6th lists a true third formula: EAC = AC + [(BAC−EV)/(CPI×SPI)], which genuinely differs.
issues.fix: EAC = AC + (BAC − EV) / CPI = $714,286 + $476,190 = $1,190,476 — this formula is algebraically identical to BAC/CPI and always produces the same result; the true third independent formula is EAC = AC + [(BAC − EV) / (CPI × SPI)], which weights both schedule and cost efficiency

agile-pm.note: Five substantive issues found across 26 cards. Card 6 has three errors clustered around the EVM worked example (arithmetic error in EAC, wrong cost-overrun percentage, and a wrong PMBOK edition citation). Card 3 has a factual brand-history error (LinearB ≠ GitPrime; GitPrime became Pluralsight Flow). Card 10's EAC section is misleading because it treats two algebraically identical formulas as producing different forecasts. Remaining cards — Scrum timeboxes, SAFe PI duration, ROAM categories, INVEST criteria, OKR attainment calibration, Brooks's Law, DORA metric names, Little's Law heuristic, and all fj/fs polarity checks — are factually sound.

issues.claim: by either including worked examples in the prompt ('Let's think step by step')
issues.problem: 'Let's think step by step' is the zero-shot CoT trigger phrase (Kojima et al. 2022) — it contains no worked examples. Worked examples are few-shot CoT (Wei et al. 2022). The card presents these as the same technique, which they are not: zero-shot CoT appends the instruction with no demonstrations; few-shot CoT provides step-by-step solved examples in the prompt. Conflating them will mislead any candidate asked to distinguish the two.
issues.fix: by either appending a zero-shot instruction to the prompt ('Let's think step by step', no worked examples required) or by providing few-shot worked examples that demonstrate step-by-step reasoning

issues.claim: Non-compliance carries fines up to €15M or 3% of global turnover for transparency violations.
issues.problem: The fine tiers in EU AI Act Article 99 are not uniform. €15M / 3% applies to violations of high-risk AI system obligations and GPAI provider obligations. Violations of the limited-risk transparency obligations (e.g., failure to disclose that users are interacting with a chatbot) fall under the lower tier: €7,500,000 or 1.5% of worldwide annual turnover. Labeling €15M/3% as the penalty 'for transparency violations' overstates the fine for the limited-risk category and conflates two different tiers.
issues.fix: Non-compliance carries fines up to €15M or 3% of global turnover for violations of high-risk AI system obligations; transparency disclosure violations carry fines up to €7.5M or 1.5% of global turnover.

issues.claim: Article 52 requires that users of chatbots and emotion-recognition systems be notified they are interacting with AI
issues.problem: Article 52 is from the 2021 European Commission proposal. In the enacted EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, published in the Official Journal), the chatbot and deepfake transparency obligations are in Article 50. Citing the proposal article number in a card about compliance obligations will send learners to the wrong provision in the actual law.
issues.fix: Article 50 requires that users of chatbots and emotion-recognition systems be notified they are interacting with AI

issues.claim: Article 15 gives the right to 'meaningful information about the logic involved'
issues.problem: GDPR Article 15 is the right of access — it gives data subjects the right to obtain copies of their personal data and ancillary information about processing. The right to meaningful information about the logic of automated decisions derives from Article 22 (and Recital 71) of the GDPR, which governs automated individual decision-making. Attributing the logic-explanation right to Article 15 is a clear factual error that could mislead candidates preparing for privacy/compliance interviews.
issues.fix: Article 22 grants data subjects the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions with significant effects, and Recital 71 clarifies this includes the right to obtain 'meaningful information about the logic involved'

issues.claim: sequential testing framework like the Robbins-Monroe procedure to avoid false positives from multiple testing
issues.problem: The Robbins-Monro algorithm (1951, Robbins and Monro) is a stochastic approximation method for finding roots of regression functions — it has nothing to do with sequential hypothesis testing. The relevant frameworks for always-valid sequential testing in A/B contexts are the mixture Sequential Probability Ratio Test (mSPRT), e-values, or the 'always-valid inference' framework (Johari et al. 2022). This is a confident misattribution of a well-known algorithm name to an unrelated methodology.
issues.fix: sequential testing framework like the mixture Sequential Probability Ratio Test (mSPRT) or always-valid inference to avoid false positives from multiple testing

ai-product-mgmt.note: 26 cards audited (78 total answer blocks including followups). Five confirmed factual errors found: one ML methodology error (CoT zero-shot vs few-shot conflation), two EU AI Act article-number/penalty-tier errors, one GDPR article misattribution, and one statistical method misname. No polarity or scenario-integrity issues detected. Pricing figures (GPT-4o, Claude 3 Haiku) and PSI thresholds are labeled with appropriate context or are standard industry values. The Nature Medicine 2019 radiologist study attribution is plausible but unverified — not flagged because confidence is insufficient to call it wrong.

issues.claim: parallelism (default 32) caps running+queued tasks per scheduler across the whole deployment
issues.problem: The Airflow `parallelism` setting caps only RUNNING task instances, not running+queued. The Airflow docs define it as 'the max number of task instances that should run simultaneously.' Queued tasks are not counted against this limit — they wait until running slots open. Saying 'running+queued' is factually wrong and would confuse someone trying to understand why tasks remain queued.
issues.fix: parallelism (default 32) caps the number of task instances running simultaneously across the whole deployment

issues.claim: limited executor choices (Celery only as of 2025)
issues.problem: AWS added KubernetesExecutor support to Amazon MWAA in late 2024 (announced at re:Invent 2024, available in MWAA 2.9.2). Stating 'Celery only as of 2025' is factually incorrect and would cause engineers to incorrectly rule out MWAA for workloads that need per-task pod isolation.

issues.claim: MWAA runs CeleryExecutor only — if you need KubernetesExecutor for per-task isolation or GPU tasks, MWAA is a hard no.
issues.problem: Same error as in the main answer: MWAA added KubernetesExecutor support in late 2024. The 'hard no' verdict is no longer correct and would lead a candidate to give a wrong answer in an interview.

issues.claim: Use Airflow's KubernetumExecutor PodTemplateFile to standardize the worker environment.
issues.problem: Two problems: (1) 'KubernetumExecutor' is a typo for 'KubernetesExecutor'. (2) More importantly, the advice is wrong in context: this followup asks how to prevent environment drift in CeleryExecutor, but a KubernetesExecutor PodTemplateFile is a Kubernetes-specific concept with no applicability to Celery workers. CeleryExecutor workers are standardized via Docker images and pinned requirements.txt, not via a PodTemplateFile.
issues.fix: Bake a consistent Docker image for all workers and update it in CI/CD alongside DAG deployments. Pin all package versions in requirements.txt and use a controlled base image. Never let workers update packages independently — all workers must run the identical image to prevent drift.

issues.claim: custom XCom backends (Airflow 2.7+) available for larger payloads
issues.problem: The custom XCom backend interface (subclassing BaseXCom and setting AIRFLOW__CORE__XCOM_BACKEND) was available since Airflow 2.0/2.1, not 2.7. What arrived in Airflow 2.7 is the specific built-in `XComObjectStorageBackend`. Saying 'custom XCom backends (Airflow 2.7+)' implies the entire capability is new in 2.7, which is wrong and undersells how long this feature has been available.
issues.fix: custom XCom backends (available since Airflow 2.1; the built-in XComObjectStorageBackend arrived in Airflow 2.7) available for larger payloads

issues.claim: the practical rule is to keep XComs under about 48 KB
issues.problem: There is no official Airflow-documented 48 KB XCom limit. The XCom column is BYTEA in PostgreSQL (no practical size limit from Airflow), MEDIUMBLOB in MySQL (16 MB limit from MySQL). The '48 KB' figure is an undocumented community heuristic that varies by source. Presenting it as 'the practical rule' implies an authoritative threshold that does not exist in the documentation.

issues.claim: a Triggerer with at least two replicas (Airflow 3 supports HA Triggerers)
issues.problem: HA Triggerer support (running multiple Triggerer instances) was introduced in Airflow 2.6.0, not Airflow 3. The parenthetical 'Airflow 3 supports HA Triggerers' implies this is a new Airflow 3 feature, which is incorrect and would mislead users on Airflow 2.6/2.7/2.8 into thinking they cannot run HA Triggerers.
issues.fix: a Triggerer with at least two replicas (HA Triggerers supported since Airflow 2.6)

issues.claim: Airflow has the widest provider library (~90 operators in 2025)
issues.problem: Apache Airflow has approximately 80–90 official provider PACKAGES, but each package contains many operators, sensors, hooks, and transfers. The total count of individual operator classes across all providers is in the thousands. Describing the breadth as '~90 operators' dramatically understates Airflow's operator count and conflates provider packages with individual operator classes. A candidate repeating '~90 operators' in an interview would sound poorly informed.
issues.fix: Airflow has the widest provider library (~90 official provider packages, each containing multiple operators, sensors, and hooks) in 2025

airflow.note: 49 cards reviewed (including all followups). Most of the content is technically solid. The main substantive errors are: (1) parallelism described as capping running+queued tasks when it only caps running tasks; (2) MWAA incorrectly stated as CeleryExecutor-only — AWS added KubernetesExecutor support in late 2024; (3) a followup for CeleryExecutor environment drift incorrectly recommends using a KubernetesExecutor PodTemplateFile (wrong tool for the wrong executor, plus a typo 'KubernetumExecutor'); (4) HA Triggerers attributed to Airflow 3 when they arrived in Airflow 2.6; (5) the XCom custom backend feature dated to 2.7+ when the interface predates that (only the built-in XComObjectStorageBackend is 2.7+); (6) the '~90 operators' claim should say ~90 provider packages. The 48 KB XCom limit is flagged as a made-up heuristic with no official Airflow source.

issues.claim: (6) Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — URL parameters that cause the server to fetch arbitrary internal targets; (7) Security Misconfiguration — CORS wildcard, debug endpoints in prod.
issues.problem: The OWASP API Security Top 10 2023 list places API6:2023 as 'Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows', API7:2023 as SSRF, and API8:2023 as Security Misconfiguration. The card skips API6 entirely, so SSRF is mislabeled as #6 (should be #7) and Security Misconfiguration as #7 (should be #8).
issues.fix: (6) Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows — business logic that can be abused at scale (e.g. bulk account creation, automated purchases); (7) Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — URL parameters that cause the server to fetch arbitrary internal targets; (8) Security Misconfiguration — CORS wildcard, debug endpoints in prod.

issues.claim: On a 50ms RTT network that is at least 75ms wasted before the first HTTP byte.
issues.problem: On a 50ms RTT network the TCP three-way handshake costs exactly 1 RTT = 50ms until the connection is established (client sends ACK+data after receiving SYN-ACK). 75ms is not a standard latency measurement; it appears to count three individual one-way trips (SYN=25ms + SYN-ACK=25ms + ACK=25ms) which is non-standard. The time to first HTTP response byte is 2 RTTs = 100ms (handshake + request).
issues.fix: On a 50ms RTT network that is at least 50ms wasted on the TCP handshake alone (1 RTT), and 100ms before the first HTTP response byte (handshake + request round-trip).

issues.claim: via graphql-ws protocol in 2024+, replacing the deprecated subscriptions-transport-ws
issues.problem: graphql-ws was released in 2020 and became the dominant standard well before 2024. The same card's own followup answer states 'All major GraphQL servers adopted graphql-ws by 2022.' The '2024+' framing directly contradicts the followup and is factually wrong.
issues.fix: via graphql-ws protocol (standard since 2021–2022), replacing the deprecated subscriptions-transport-ws

issues.claim: authentication was passed as a query parameter in the WebSocket URL (exposing tokens in server logs)
issues.problem: The subscriptions-transport-ws protocol specification uses connection_init message payload (connectionParams) for passing auth tokens — not URL query parameters. This is the same channel mechanism that graphql-ws uses. Passing tokens in URL query params was an anti-pattern adopted by some application-level implementations, not a protocol design flaw. Presenting it as the protocol's designed auth mechanism mischaracterizes the actual difference.

issues.claim: The server GRPC_ARG_KEEPALIVE_PERMIT_WITHOUT_CALLS must also be set to true if you want keepalives on idle connections, or the client sees silent disconnects during quiet periods.
issues.problem: GRPC_ARG_KEEPALIVE_PERMIT_WITHOUT_CALLS is a CLIENT-side channel argument that controls whether the client sends keepalive pings when there are no active RPCs. The client needs this set to true to send keepalives on idle connections. The server uses different arguments (e.g. GRPC_ARG_HTTP2_MAX_PINGS_WITHOUT_DATA) to govern incoming ping rates. Attributing this arg solely to 'the server' and ignoring that the client must also have it set is wrong.
issues.fix: The client GRPC_ARG_KEEPALIVE_PERMIT_WITHOUT_CALLS must also be set to true if you want keepalives sent on idle connections; without it the client will not send pings when there are no active RPCs, causing silent disconnects during quiet periods.

issues.claim: WebSocket adds a framing layer with a 2–10 byte header per frame containing opcode, payload length, and a masking key.
issues.problem: Including a masking key (4 bytes) in the header makes the maximum 14 bytes (2 base + 8 extended-length + 4 mask key), not 10. The range 2–10 bytes is only correct for unmasked server-to-client frames. Client-to-server frames are always masked (RFC 6455 requires it), so their header range is 6–14 bytes. The statement simultaneously claims a 10-byte ceiling and mentions the masking key, which is internally inconsistent.
issues.fix: WebSocket adds a framing layer with a 2–14 byte header per frame: 2 bytes of base header (opcode, payload length field), up to 8 bytes of extended payload length, and (for client-to-server frames only) a 4-byte masking key.

apis.note: 91 cards audited in full, including all followup questions. The OWASP API Top 10 2023 numbering error (card 57) and the graphql-ws '2024+' dating (card 58) are the highest-confidence factual errors. The TCP 75ms claim (card 59) and the gRPC GRPC_ARG_KEEPALIVE_PERMIT_WITHOUT_CALLS attribution to 'the server' (card 63) are also confirmed wrong. The subscriptions-transport-ws URL-auth claim and the WebSocket frame header size inconsistency are flagged as misleading but with medium confidence. The vast majority of content — OAuth2/JWT flows, rate limiting algorithms, pagination patterns, HTTP semantics, cache-control, circuit breakers, mTLS, CORS, CAP/PACELC, GraphQL N+1/DataLoader, REST design principles — checked out correct against current (2026) standards. The Clarity-specific cards (70–79) contain operational advice that is plausible but unverifiable from public sources; no confident factual errors were found there beyond noting that some Clarity API limits and behaviors are self-described as 'as of early 2026' and may drift.

issues.claim: Tools like pg_rewrite/pglogical or MySQL's pt-online-schema-change rewrite the table online in the background, using triggers to sync changes while the original stays live, avoiding the lock.
issues.problem: pg_rewrite is not a user-facing online DDL tool — it is an internal PostgreSQL catalog rewrite system used by the query planner. pglogical is a logical replication extension, not an online table-rewrite tool. The correct PostgreSQL tool for online table rewrites is pg_repack (formerly pg_reorg).
issues.fix: Tools like pg_repack (formerly pg_reorg) for PostgreSQL or MySQL's pt-online-schema-change rewrite the table online in the background, using triggers to sync changes while the original stays live, avoiding the lock.

issues.claim: add nullable, backfill, then ADD CONSTRAINT NOT NULL as a NOT VALID constraint, validate it in a separate pass, then SET NOT NULL — avoiding the full table lock.
issues.problem: PostgreSQL does not support NOT VALID on NOT NULL constraints. NOT VALID is only valid for CHECK and FOREIGN KEY constraints. 'ADD CONSTRAINT col_name NOT NULL NOT VALID' is invalid syntax. The correct approach is 'ADD CONSTRAINT col_not_null CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID', then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT, then SET NOT NULL.
issues.fix: add nullable, backfill, then ADD CONSTRAINT col_not_null CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID, validate it in a separate pass with VALIDATE CONSTRAINT, then SET NOT NULL — on Postgres 12+ the planner detects the validated check constraint and skips the full table scan.

issues.claim: EdgeWorkers have tight CPU time limits (roughly 50ms)
issues.problem: Akamai EdgeWorkers' documented CPU time budget is approximately 10ms per request (standard tier), not roughly 50ms. 50ms overstates the actual limit by 5x and contradicts card 137's followup which states 120ms — the file has two internally inconsistent values and both are wrong.
issues.fix: EdgeWorkers have tight CPU time limits (roughly 10ms per request)

issues.claim: In early 2026 EdgeWorkers supports up to 120ms CPU time per request at the edge
issues.problem: Akamai EdgeWorkers' documented CPU time limit is approximately 10ms per request (standard tier). 120ms overstates the actual limit by roughly an order of magnitude and directly contradicts card 131 in the same file, which says 'roughly 50ms'. Both values are wrong; 120ms is the more egregious and more likely to mislead a candidate.
issues.fix: In early 2026 EdgeWorkers supports up to approximately 10ms CPU time per request at the edge (standard tier)

issues.claim: cloud computing regions have ~15 PoPs globally
issues.problem: AWS alone had 33+ regions as of early 2026, plus hundreds of CloudFront edge locations. Azure and GCP each have comparable footprints. '~15 PoPs globally' for cloud computing providers is significantly wrong regardless of whether PoPs means regions, AZs, or CDN edge locations.

architecture.note: All 179 cards audited. The file is high quality overall — CAP/PACELC, saga/CQRS/hexagonal, Kafka mechanics, AWS Well-Architected 6 pillars, Redis hash slots, Spanner TrueTime, and DDD patterns are all accurate. Five issues found: wrong Postgres online-DDL tool names (pg_rewrite/pglogical vs pg_repack), invalid PostgreSQL NOT NULL NOT VALID constraint syntax, two internally inconsistent and overstated Akamai EdgeWorkers CPU limits (50ms and 120ms in the same file; documented ~10ms), and a severely understated cloud region count (~15 vs 33+ AWS regions alone in 2026).

issues.claim: SnapStart only applies to Java.
issues.problem: Lambda SnapStart expanded beyond Java in 2023. AWS added support for Python 3.12+ and .NET 8+ runtimes. The card's own 'a' field correctly states 'now supports Java, Python 3.12+, and .NET 8+', making the 'fs' internally contradictory and outdated. A candidate giving the 'fs' answer would be penalized for stating incorrect scope.
issues.fix: Provisioned Concurrency keeps a warm pool running at all times (you pay continuously), while SnapStart takes a snapshot after init and restores it in milliseconds for free — they're mutually exclusive, and SnapStart supports Java, Python 3.12+, and .NET 8+.

aws.note: Audited all 180 top-level cards and 413 followups across the full aws.json file (528 KB, read in 13 chunks). One confirmed factual error found: the 'fs' (strong answer) field in card 25 (Lambda cold starts / SnapStart) states SnapStart only applies to Java, which directly contradicts the card's own 'a' field and AWS documentation as of 2026. All other claims verified accurate: S3 strong consistency (Dec 2020), SQS FIFO 300/3000 TPS, Lambda 15-min/10GB limits, Kinesis EFO 70ms latency, Route 53 10s health check intervals, Aurora 6-way replication, Redshift AQUA, EMR Serverless auto-scaling, Bedrock guardrails, and SST v3 Ion Pulumi-backed IaC claims all checked out.

issues.claim: Strong/Bounded cost ~2x RU and limit geo-writes.
issues.problem: Bounded Staleness does NOT limit geo-writes. Only Strong consistency restricts to a single write region. Bounded Staleness is explicitly the strongest consistency level available with multi-region (active-active) writes — the followup answer on the same card correctly states this. The FS groups Strong and Bounded Staleness together for 'limit geo-writes', which is factually wrong for Bounded Staleness.
issues.fix: Strong/Bounded cost ~2x RU; Strong also blocks multi-region writes (Bounded Staleness does not).

issues.claim: Your Azure Databricks cluster running on AKS node pools is experiencing CPU throttling. Explain how AKS node pool autoscaling interacts with Databricks cluster autoscaling, and the Performance Tuning steps specific to this layered architecture.
issues.problem: Azure Databricks does not run on customer-managed AKS node pools. Azure Databricks manages its own VM compute plane via Azure Resource Manager — workers are provisioned as Azure VMs (optionally inside a customer VNet via VNet injection), not as Kubernetes pods in a customer AKS cluster. The entire card premise, including the answer's references to 'container pull', 'pause pods', and 'AKS spot node pools for Databricks workers', describes an architecture that does not exist for Azure Databricks. Open-source Spark-on-AKS is a separate pattern and a different product.

azure.note: 154 top-level cards reviewed (335 followup Q&A pairs also examined, total 489 question-answer pairs). Two high-confidence factual errors found. The rest of the content is accurate: TU limits (40 on Standard), SHIR node limit (4), Cosmos DB hot-partition limits (10k RU/s / 20 GB), Hyperscale 128 TB limit, Management Group depth (6 levels), blob tier minimum periods, Durable Functions backends, IQP compatibility levels, and all RBAC/Conditional Access/networking claims checked out. The Log Analytics 'built on Azure Data Explorer' claim (card 45) was reviewed but not flagged — Microsoft's own documentation uses equivalent phrasing ('based on Azure Data Explorer').

issues.claim: set a five-minute rollback timer
issues.problem: The term 'rollback timer' implies the timer would roll back (undo) the scoped write-path disable. But the followup answer and the 'fs' field both clarify the timer is an escalation trigger — if corruption does not stop within five minutes, the team goes to full write-path disable (a more aggressive action, not an undo). 'Rollback' and 'full-disable escalation' are opposite operations; the wrong term in the main answer contradicts the correct explanation in 'fs' ('set a hard five-minute timer to escalate') and in followup 1 ('we go full write-path disable immediately').
issues.fix: set a five-minute escalation timer

issues.claim: The CTO endorsed it at the next all-hands as company direction. We consolidated clusters over two quarters, cut infrastructure cost by 35 percent
issues.problem: The main answer narrates: write paper → working group → CTO endorses at all-hands → then consolidate over two quarters. But 'fs' and followup 3 both state the opposite sequence: the first cluster migration succeeded and proved savings before the CTO endorsement was requested. Followup 3 says explicitly: 'I didn't ask for the endorsement until the first-cluster migration had already succeeded and the cost savings were real numbers, not projections. By the time it became company direction, it was already proven at one cluster.' The two timelines are directly contradictory. The 'fs' and followup version (prove first, then endorse) is the pedagogically correct story for this card; the main answer inverts the sequence.

behavioral.note: All 38 cards audited. The file is largely clean. Two issues found: (1) Card 33 uses 'rollback timer' in the main answer where every other field in the same card correctly calls it an escalation trigger — these are opposite operations and a student reading only the 'a' field gets the wrong mental model. (2) Card 36 main answer places CTO endorsement before any cluster migration, while 'fs' and followup 3 both explicitly state the endorsement was sought only after the first migration proved savings — a direct sequence contradiction within the same card. No polarity flips, no outdated technology claims, and no factual errors in the technical claims (Spark shuffle behavior, Kafka partition ordering, materialized view semantics, and dbt blast-radius descriptions are all accurate as of 2026).

issues.claim: DATE_DIFF('month',\n    signup_month, activity_month)
issues.problem: DATE_DIFF with a quoted string as the first argument is not valid in any major SQL dialect. BigQuery's DATE_DIFF signature is DATE_DIFF(end_date, start_date, date_part) where date_part is an unquoted keyword in the third position (e.g. MONTH). Snowflake uses the function name DATEDIFF, not DATE_DIFF, and its signature is DATEDIFF(date_part, start_date, end_date). The code is syntactically wrong in all common warehouse dialects.
issues.fix: DATE_DIFF(activity_month, signup_month, MONTH)  -- BigQuery syntax; or use DATEDIFF('month', signup_month, activity_month) for Snowflake

issues.claim: In 2025 deployments at Databricks and Snowflake, LoRA-tuned adapters cut SQL hallucination rates on proprietary schemas by 40–60% versus prompting a general model.
issues.problem: This is a fabricated, unverifiable statistic. No published benchmark from Databricks or Snowflake reports this specific 40–60% hallucination-rate reduction figure. Confident AI-generated statistics like this with named companies and precise ranges are a hallmark of hallucinated claims. Learners who cite this figure will be embarrassed.

issues.claim: The 2025 Open Semantic Interchange effort pushes a vendor-neutral metric spec so definitions are portable.
issues.problem: There is no widely-recognized initiative called 'Open Semantic Interchange' (OSI) as of 2026. This appears to be a hallucinated initiative name. Relevant real efforts include the Semantic Layer Summit discussions and dbt's MetricFlow, but no formal 'OSI' specification exists. The same term and description appear in a follow-up card (index 8, followup 3) which compounds the issue.

issues.claim: How does Power BI's row-level security integrate with Azure Active Directory groups
issues.problem: Microsoft renamed Azure Active Directory to Microsoft Entra ID in July 2023. Both the question title and the answer body repeatedly use 'Azure Active Directory' and the abbreviation 'AAD'. A card taught in 2026 should use the current product name 'Microsoft Entra ID' and 'Entra groups'. The underlying technical behavior is correct; only the product name is stale.
issues.fix: Replace 'Azure Active Directory' with 'Microsoft Entra ID' and 'AAD' with 'Entra ID' throughout the question and answer.

issues.claim: version-controlled and published from a single semantic layer (ebt Semantic Layer / MetricFlow, Cube)
issues.problem: Typo: 'ebt Semantic Layer' should be 'dbt Semantic Layer'. While a keystroke error, it produces a nonsensical product name that would confuse learners who search for 'ebt Semantic Layer'.
issues.fix: version-controlled and published from a single semantic layer (dbt Semantic Layer / MetricFlow, Cube)

issues.claim: In SQL Server Management Studio, use SSMS's 'Analyze in Excel' with 'Effective Security as Role' context
issues.problem: SSMS (SQL Server Management Studio) does not have an 'Analyze in Excel' feature for SSAS Tabular models. 'Analyze in Excel' is a feature in SSDT (SQL Server Data Tools / Visual Studio) and in Power BI Desktop. SSMS can connect to SSAS and run DAX/MDX queries with EffectiveUserName in the connection string, but it has no 'Analyze in Excel' button or 'Effective Security as Role' dialog. Learners who follow this instruction will not find the feature in SSMS.
issues.fix: In SSDT (Visual Studio), right-click the model and choose 'Analyze in Excel', then select a role to impersonate. Alternatively, in SSMS open a DAX/MDX query window with the connection string property EffectiveUserName=user@domain.com to impersonate a specific user.

bi.note: 116 top-level cards audited (plus 288 followups = 404 total q/a pairs reviewed). Six issues found: one fabricated statistic (LoRA 40-60% claim), one wrong SQL function signature (DATE_DIFF cohort code), one outdated product name (AAD vs Entra ID), one wrong tool attribution (SSMS vs SSDT for Analyze in Excel), one typo producing nonsense product name (ebt vs dbt), and one likely-hallucinated initiative name (Open Semantic Interchange) flagged at low confidence for human verification. The dimensional modeling, DAX, LookML, SSRS, and Medallion Architecture content is otherwise technically sound.

issues.claim: Bessemer Venture Partners' State of the Cloud report uses '5 × ARR ÷ Sales & Marketing spend' as a reciprocal efficiency measure — a score above 1.5× is strong
issues.problem: This formula does not correspond to any standard Bessemer metric. The '5×' multiplier is unexplained and makes the 1.5× threshold essentially trivial: a company with $10M ARR and $5M S&M would score 10×, far above 1.5×. Bessemer's State of the Cloud uses the CAC Ratio (S&M spend / net new ARR) or variants of the Magic Number (net new ARR × 4 / prior-period S&M); neither involves a '5×ARR' multiplier. The formula as written appears fabricated.

issues.claim: data-driven (Shapley value or Markov chain using actual path data, available in GA4 and Rockerbox)
issues.problem: GA4's data-driven attribution uses Shapley values only. Markov chain attribution is not a built-in model in GA4; it is available in custom R/Python implementations and some third-party platforms but not natively in GA4. Listing 'Markov chain' as a GA4 option is incorrect and will mislead anyone trying to configure GA4 attribution.
issues.fix: data-driven (Shapley value using actual path data, available natively in GA4 and Rockerbox; Markov chain attribution requires custom R/Python implementation or dedicated MMM tooling and is not a GA4 built-in option)

issues.claim: SKAdNetwork (SKAN) — Apple's privacy-preserving attribution API that reports aggregate campaign data with 24–72h delay and no user-level data
issues.problem: SKAN 4 (released with iOS 16.1, October 2022) replaced the single-window model with three postback windows: 0–2 days, 3–7 days, and 8–35 days post-install, each delivered after an additional 24–144h random delay. By 2026, SKAN 4 is the operative version for virtually all traffic. Full attribution data can take up to ~49 days to arrive, making the '24–72h delay' claim materially wrong for the current environment.
issues.fix: SKAdNetwork (SKAN 4) — Apple's privacy-preserving attribution API that reports aggregate campaign data across three postback windows (0–2 days, 3–7 days, and 8–35 days post-install); each postback arrives with an additional 24–144h random delay, so complete attribution data can take up to ~49 days, with no user-level data at any stage

issues.claim: WARN Act notice requirements (60 days in the US for 50+ affected employees)
issues.problem: The federal WARN Act is materially more nuanced. It applies only to employers with 100+ employees. For a mass layoff (as opposed to a plant closing), the threshold is 500+ workers at a single site, OR 50–499 workers if they constitute ≥33% of the active workforce at that site. A 15% headcount cut at a 500-person company (75 employees) does not meet the 33% threshold and likely would not trigger WARN. Stating '50+ affected employees' as the blanket trigger omits the employer-size requirement and the 33% workforce threshold, which could lead to real compliance errors.
issues.fix: WARN Act notice requirements (60 days in the US; applies to employers with 100+ employees; triggers for plant closings of 50+ workers at one site, or mass layoffs of 500+ workers at one site, or 50–499 workers if they represent ≥33% of the active workforce at that site)

issues.claim: the middle tier becomes the default 'rational' choice (the compromise effect, documented in Ariely's 'Predictably Irrational')
issues.problem: The compromise effect — the tendency to choose the middle option when three choices are presented — was documented by Itamar Simonson in a 1989 Journal of Consumer Research paper, not by Ariely. Dan Ariely's 'Predictably Irrational' (2008) primarily demonstrates the decoy effect (an inferior 'decoy' option shifts preference toward the target option), which is a related but distinct phenomenon. Attributing the compromise effect specifically to Ariely misidentifies the source and conflates two different cognitive biases.
issues.fix: the middle tier becomes the default 'rational' choice (the compromise effect, first documented by Simonson 1989; related decoy-effect dynamics are explored in Ariely's 'Predictably Irrational')

bizops.note: 26 cards audited across all levels (Jr through Principal). 5 substantive issues found. The file is generally well-constructed with sound BizOps frameworks; errors are concentrated in specific quantitative attributions (Bessemer formula, GA4 attribution model) and a regulatory claim (WARN Act) that would mislead practitioners. The SKAN 4 timing issue is the most practically damaging for a 2026 audience since it describes a superseded version of the attribution API. No polarity issues found — fj/fs pairs are correctly oriented throughout.

issues.claim: BPMN 2.0 (Business Process Model and Notation) is the OMG standard for process modeling, executable by BPMN-compliant engines like Camunda, Activiti, or AWS Step Functions.
issues.problem: AWS Step Functions is not a BPMN 2.0 engine. It uses Amazon States Language (ASL), a JSON-based proprietary workflow notation. Including it alongside Camunda and Activiti (genuine BPMN 2.0 engines) is factually wrong and would mislead a candidate who tries to deploy a BPMN 2.0 model to Step Functions.
issues.fix: BPMN 2.0 (Business Process Model and Notation) is the OMG standard for process modeling, executable by BPMN-compliant engines like Camunda, Activiti, or Flowable.

issues.claim: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals zero — approximately 9.1%, just below the 10% hurdle rate.
issues.problem: The stated cashflows are -$2M at Year 0 and +$440K per year for Years 1–5. The true IRR for these cashflows is approximately 3.26%, not 9.1%. At 9.1% the NPV is still approximately -$293K. The card's IRR figure is materially wrong and an interviewer or finance reviewer would immediately catch it.
issues.fix: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals zero — approximately 3.3%, well below the 10% hurdle rate, confirming the project does not clear the required return on a 5-year horizon.

issues.claim: At Year 6 it turns positive.
issues.problem: With the given cashflows (-$2M upfront, +$440K/yr) discounted at 10%, the cumulative discounted NPV at Year 6 is approximately -$84K (still negative). The NPV first turns positive at Year 7 (approximately +$142K). The claim that Year 6 is the breakeven year is incorrect.
issues.fix: At Year 7 the discounted NPV first turns positive.

issues.claim: data analysts spend 18 hours/week on manual reconciliation, costing $540K/year in lost productivity at fully-loaded cost of $60K/analyst × 3 FTE × 52 weeks
issues.problem: The arithmetic is internally inconsistent. Three analysts at a fully-loaded cost of $60K per analyst per year equals $180K total team cost, not $540K. To spend $540K on three FTEs the per-analyst cost would need to be $180K. As written, either the $60K figure should be $180K, or the total should be $180K — a real finance reviewer would immediately reject this business case example.
issues.fix: data analysts spend 18 hours/week on manual reconciliation, costing $540K/year in lost productivity at a fully-loaded cost of $180K/analyst × 3 FTE

issues.claim: The BABOK v3 defines 10 elicitation techniques: Brainstorming, Document Analysis, Focus Groups, Interface Analysis, Interviews, Observation (job shadowing), Prototyping, Requirements Workshops (JAD sessions), Survey/Questionnaire, and Survey Experiments.
issues.problem: BABOK v3 does not define 'Survey Experiments' as a distinct elicitation technique. BABOK v3 lists techniques including Brainstorming, Collaborative Games, Concept Modelling, Data Mining, Document Analysis, Focus Groups, Interface Analysis, Interviews, Observation, Prototyping, Requirements Workshops, and Survey or Questionnaire — not 10 and not 'Survey Experiments'. Naming a non-existent technique from BABOK v3 is factually wrong.
issues.fix: BABOK v3 elicitation techniques include: Brainstorming, Document Analysis, Focus Groups, Interface Analysis, Interviews, Observation (job shadowing), Prototyping, Requirements Workshops (JAD sessions), Survey or Questionnaire, Collaborative Games, Concept Modelling, and Data Mining.

issues.claim: How do you specify data quality requirements — what dimensions do you define thresholds for, and how do you document them so a development team can actually test against them?
issues.problem: Cards 26–31 are near-exact duplicates of cards 20–25 respectively (same questions, same answers, same followups with only minor wording rewrites). The file contains 32 cards but only 26 unique cards, inflating the apparent content. This structural duplication means 6 cards add no learning value and the repeated content may confuse spaced-repetition systems.

business-analysis.note: 32 cards audited (indices 0–31). Cards 20–25 and 26–31 are structurally duplicate pairs — only minor surface-level rewording differs between each pair, so the file effectively contains 26 unique cards. Four clear factual errors were found: AWS Step Functions incorrectly called a BPMN 2.0 engine (card 9); the IRR for the worked NPV example is 3.26% not 9.1% (card 23); NPV does not turn positive at Year 6 but at Year 7 (card 23); and the productivity-cost arithmetic ($60K × 3 FTE = $540K) is wrong by a factor of 3 (card 23). One likely error: BABOK v3 does not define 'Survey Experiments' as a distinct technique (card 2). Process, methodology, and tool claims (Cockburn use-case template, Azure DevOps link types, Pact/Spring Cloud Contract, Jama, IBM DOORS, Collibra, Alation, PERT formula, BPMN gateway semantics, 5 Whys attribution, GDPR Art 17 timeframe) were checked and are accurate.

issues.claim: PostgreSQL pre-14 acquires a full table lock when adding a NOT NULL column with a non-volatile default
issues.problem: PostgreSQL 11 (released October 2018) introduced the optimization that lets ADD COLUMN with a constant (immutable) default avoid a full table rewrite. The threshold is pre-11, not pre-14. PostgreSQL 14 introduced no relevant change to this behavior. Saying 'pre-14' implies PG 11/12/13 still have the problem, which is false for constant defaults.
issues.fix: PostgreSQL pre-11 acquires a full table lock when adding a NOT NULL column with a constant default value, which can block reads and writes for minutes on a large table.

issues.claim: Lead time = merge-to-main timestamp minus the deploy timestamp, joined on git SHA.
issues.problem: The subtraction operands are reversed. Lead time for changes measures the elapsed time from when code merges to main until it is deployed to production — i.e., deploy_timestamp minus merge_timestamp. 'Merge minus deploy' would yield a negative value.
issues.fix: Lead time = deploy timestamp minus the merge-to-main timestamp, joined on git SHA.

issues.claim: By default CodePipeline queues a second execution while the first runs; in superseded mode the in-flight execution is replaced by the newer one.
issues.problem: AWS CodePipeline's default execution mode is SUPERSEDED, not QUEUED. By default, a new execution supersedes (cancels) the in-flight one. QUEUED mode must be explicitly configured. The card has the default and the named mode swapped.
issues.fix: By default CodePipeline uses SUPERSEDED mode, where a newer execution replaces the in-flight one. You configure the pipeline's execution mode: SUPERSEDED (default), QUEUED (serializes executions), or PARALLEL (allows concurrent executions).

issues.claim: Tekton has no central controller to maintain and pipelines are pure CRDs
issues.problem: Tekton does run a central controller (the tekton-pipelines-controller Kubernetes Deployment) that watches PipelineRun/TaskRun CRDs and orchestrates execution. The distinction from Jenkins is that Tekton's controller is a Kubernetes-native deployment managed via kubectl/Helm, not a standalone JVM server. Saying 'no central controller' is factually incorrect; the correct contrast is that there is no separate CI server binary to operate outside the cluster.
issues.fix: Tekton's controller runs as a Kubernetes-native deployment (no separate server binary), so pipeline infrastructure is managed via kubectl and Helm like any other workload, and pipelines are defined as pure CRDs.

issues.claim: By default deferral only kicks in if the object isn't already in your target; --favor-state forces using the state manifest's reference even when a local copy exists.
issues.problem: This describes dbt Core pre-1.6 behavior. Since dbt 1.6 (July 2023), --defer implies --favor-state by default: dbt always resolves unselected upstream refs to the deferred manifest regardless of whether a local copy exists. The opt-out flag is now --no-favor-state. Telling an interviewer that --favor-state is required to force this behavior is wrong on dbt 1.6+, which is the current version.

cicd.note: 68 top-level cards audited (189 followups also reviewed, total 257 question/answer pairs). Five substantive issues found: two factual errors (PostgreSQL version threshold wrong, DORA lead-time subtraction reversed), one wrong default (CodePipeline SUPERSEDED vs QUEUED), one misleading claim (Tekton controller), and one outdated description (dbt --favor-state pre-1.6 behavior). The remainder of the file — SLSA level descriptions, GitHub Actions behavior, Jenkins concepts, Liquibase mechanics, canary/blue-green/rolling distinctions, CodePipeline cross-account patterns, and supply-chain security concepts — is factually sound and appropriately nuanced for the stated level of each card.

issues.claim: Even if no reallocation occurs, the standard doesn't guarantee iterator stability across push_back.
issues.problem: This is factually wrong. The C++ standard (C++17 [vector.modifiers] / [sequence.reqmts]) specifies exactly: if push_back does NOT cause a reallocation, only the end() iterator is invalidated; all other iterators, pointers, and references remain valid. The claim that the standard gives no guarantee is false.
issues.fix: If push_back triggers a reallocation (capacity exceeded), all iterators, pointers, and references are invalidated. If no reallocation occurs, only end() is invalidated and all other iterators remain valid — but because you cannot predict reallocation at runtime without an explicit reserve(), treat any mutation as potentially invalidating.

issues.claim: "If the vector doesn't reallocate, the iterator is still valid."
issues.problem: This fj is supposed to be the wrong/weak take to avoid, but the statement is true per the C++ standard. Presenting a correct statement as the junky answer misleads learners into thinking it is false, compounding the factual error in the 'why' field.
issues.fix: "Iterators into a vector are always stable across push_back as long as size stays within capacity."

coding-cpp.note: 19 of 20 cards are factually sound. Card 4 (iterator invalidation) has a false claim in 'why' and a consequent polarity inversion in 'fj'. The correct answer option (ok:true) for card 4 is still right for the given code because no reserve() is called, but the explanation incorrectly attributes iterator invalidation to something the standard does not say. All other cards — integer division, off-by-one UB, assignment-in-condition, vector::at() vs [], object slicing, dangling reference from local, parameter shadowing, Rule of Three, dangling string_view, lambda capture by ref, virtual in ctor, data race, ODR/inline, SIOF, signed overflow UB, ABI hazard for std types in public headers, singleton coupling, allocator mismatch, and noexcept contract — are factually correct as of C++17/C++20 and 2026 compiler behavior.

issues.claim: unused timer is not garbage-collected until it fires (500 ms later), but creating one per tight loop iteration causes rapid timer heap growth and GC pressure. Fix: create a time.NewTicker or use time.NewTimer outside the loop and Reset it.
issues.problem: As of Go 1.23 the garbage collector CAN recover unreferenced, unstopped timers before they fire. The Go 1.23 docs explicitly state 'There is no reason to prefer NewTimer when After will do.' The premise of the latency-spike scenario (timers pinned until they fire) is no longer true. The fix recommendation is also contradicted by the official docs.

issues.claim: "time.After allocates a Timer that only GCs after it fires; use a Ticker or NewTimer+Reset in loops."
issues.problem: Both halves of this statement are wrong as of Go 1.23. (1) Unreferenced timers created by time.After CAN be garbage-collected before they fire (confirmed by go doc time.After). (2) The docs explicitly say 'There is no reason to prefer NewTimer when After will do.' Presenting this as the strong answer teaches a pattern the language authors have explicitly deprecated.
issues.fix: "time.After allocates a new Timer on every call; under a tight loop this causes allocation churn and GC pressure. Use time.NewTicker outside the loop for a fixed polling interval."

issues.claim: the issue is the Timer value created by time.After not being stopped
issues.problem: As of Go 1.23, stopping an unreferenced timer is no longer necessary for GC recovery. The 'not being stopped' framing reflects pre-1.23 behavior only.
issues.fix: the issue is the allocation of a new Timer object on every loop iteration, which adds GC pressure

issues.claim: Each goroutine costs ~4 KB stack
issues.problem: The Go runtime sets stackMin = 2048 bytes (confirmed in runtime/stack.go). Since Go 1.4, new goroutines start with a 2 KB stack, not 4 KB. On Linux/macOS (primary server targets) fixedStack0 = stackMin + 0 = 2048 bytes. The claim overstates initial cost by 2x.
issues.fix: Each goroutine costs ~2 KB stack

issues.claim: MustExec should panic on error like database/sql conventions suggest
issues.problem: database/sql has no Must convention and does not suggest panicking on errors. The Must-panics convention comes from packages like regexp (MustCompile) and html/template (Must), used only for init-time setup failures. Attributing this convention to database/sql is factually wrong and could mislead candidates about which standard library packages use Must semantics.
issues.fix: MustExec should panic on error, following Go's Must convention for functions that cannot fail

coding-go.note: 18 of 20 cards are clean. Two high-severity clusters: (1) Card 13 (time.After GC behavior) is substantively wrong in three fields — the Go 1.23 release explicitly reversed the old guarantee and the Go docs now say 'There is no reason to prefer NewTimer when After will do'; the strong answer (fs) teaches the wrong mental model. (2) Card 18 misstates goroutine initial stack size as ~4 KB when the runtime constant stackMin is 2048 bytes (2 KB) since Go 1.4. Card 19 has a misleading false-option text attributing a Must=panic convention to database/sql, which has no such convention.

issues.claim: JVM only optimizes concatenation outside loops.
issues.problem: The JVM (javac) applies the same StringBuilder transformation for += both inside and outside loops. Inside loops the pattern is O(n²) not because the JVM 'doesn't optimize' but because a new StringBuilder is instantiated each iteration copying all prior content. Saying 'only optimizes outside loops' implies the JVM does nothing for += inside loops, which is false and confuses mechanism with outcome.
issues.fix: Use StringBuilder.append() in loops; the compiler rewrites each += to a temporary StringBuilder internally but creates a new one every iteration — explicit StringBuilder amortizes the allocation across the whole loop.

issues.claim: Calling map.get() inside compute() can deadlock when the two keys share a segment lock
issues.problem: Java 8+ ConcurrentHashMap eliminated segment-level locks entirely; it now synchronizes on individual bin (bucket) head nodes. More critically, ConcurrentHashMap.get() in Java 8+ is fully lock-free — it uses volatile reads with no synchronization at all. Therefore calling map.get() on any key inside a compute() remapping function cannot deadlock. The deadlock risk is real only for write-side operations (compute, merge, put, etc.) that acquire a bin lock, potentially on the same bin.
issues.fix: Calling map.compute() or map.merge() inside compute() can deadlock if both keys hash to the same bin — compute() holds a bin-level lock and a nested write operation on the same bin will block forever

issues.claim: ConcurrentHashMap.compute() holds a segment-level or bin-level lock for the duration of the remapping function. If the remapping function calls another blocking map operation (like get on a key in the same segment), it can deadlock.
issues.problem: Two errors: (1) Java 8+ CHM has no segment-level locks — the term is from the Java 7 implementation. (2) get() is lock-free in Java 8+ CHM so it cannot deadlock regardless of which key is passed. The example code shows map.get("other"), which is safe and will not deadlock.
issues.fix: ConcurrentHashMap.compute() holds a bin-level lock for the duration of the remapping function. Calling another write operation (compute, merge, put) inside the remapping function can deadlock if both keys hash to the same bin. The remapping function must be short and must not invoke write operations on the same map.

issues.claim: Adding a default method is source-compatible but can cause AbstractMethodError at runtime if a consumer has a class that already has an incompatible onError(Throwable) method
issues.problem: AbstractMethodError is thrown when the JVM cannot find any implementation for an abstract method. A default method provides an implementation, so AME does not arise from adding one. If a consumer class already has a method with an incompatible signature (different parameters or return type), those are distinct methods and do not conflict at all. If a consumer class has the same signature, that class method silently takes precedence over the default. The realistic breakage modes are: (a) a compile-time error when two interfaces both supply conflicting defaults and the consumer must override, or (b) IncompatibleClassChangeError in specific binary-compatibility edge cases — not AbstractMethodError.
issues.fix: Adding a default method is source-compatible but can cause a compile-time conflict in consumer code that implements two interfaces with the same default method signature (diamond conflict requiring an explicit override), or IncompatibleClassChangeError at link time in pre-compiled consumers caught in a diamond conflict at runtime.

issues.claim: if a downstream class implementing EventHandler already inherits or declares a method with the same name but an incompatible signature, the JVM will throw AbstractMethodError
issues.problem: Methods with incompatible signatures are different methods — they do not conflict and the JVM does not throw any error from them. AbstractMethodError is not the correct exception for default-method diamond conflicts; the correct outcome is a compile-time error (source conflict) or IncompatibleClassChangeError (binary conflict), never AbstractMethodError for this scenario.

coding-java.note: 20 cards audited (all choice-format, no standard qa/fs/fj cards). Cards 0–2, 4–13, 15–16, 18–19 are factually clean. Three distinct issue clusters: Card 3 fs misstates the JVM's loop-concat optimization mechanism (misleading but advice is correct); Card 14 is doubly wrong — the code shows map.get() which is lock-free in Java 8+ CHM (cannot deadlock), and the explanation uses the obsolete Java 7 'segment lock' terminology; Card 17 wrong-names AbstractMethodError as the runtime failure mode for default-method conflicts (it should be IncompatibleClassChangeError or a compile-time diamond-override error).

issues.claim: { "t": "Replace with readable.pipe(writable)", "ok": false, "why": "pipe() handles backpressure correctly and is the idiomatic fix, but the question asks to fix the manual listener code without switching to pipe." }
issues.problem: The question text ('The writable stream buffers memory unboundedly. Fix?') never restricts the fix to manual-listener style. readable.pipe(writable) is a fully correct, Node-idiomatic solution to exactly the stated problem. Marking it wrong misleads learners into thinking pipe() is not a valid answer when it is arguably the best one. The constraint 'without switching to pipe' exists only in the why field — not in the question itself.
issues.fix: Change ok to true and revise the why to: "pipe() correctly handles backpressure and is the idiomatic fix. The other option (pause on write()===false, resume on 'drain') is the equivalent manual approach." Then change the currently-correct option to reflect that both approaches are valid, or restructure to make the manual-listener approach the distractor.

issues.claim: "crypto.subtle.deriveBits() to push the work off the main thread"
issues.problem: In Node.js, crypto.subtle (the Web Crypto API) does not reliably offload CPU-intensive work to the libuv thread pool the way the legacy node:crypto async callback (crypto.pbkdf2()) does. The Node.js docs note that subtle operations 'may still block' and thread-pool use is an implementation detail that varies by version and algorithm. Presenting crypto.subtle.deriveBits() as an equivalent solution to 'push work off the main thread' is misleading; the only well-documented, reliable way to offload PBKDF2 in Node.js is crypto.pbkdf2() (callback) or worker_threads.
issues.fix: "Use the async crypto.pbkdf2() (callback form), which offloads the derivation to the libuv thread pool, or move the work to a worker_thread."

issues.claim: "Express 5 async next() support exists; but even in Express 4 this pattern is often used."
issues.problem: In Express 4, next() returns undefined. Awaiting undefined resolves immediately without actually waiting for downstream middleware to complete, so the try/catch will not catch errors thrown by downstream handlers. The pattern 'is often used' in Express 4 but it silently does not work as intended — it is a subtle bug, not a valid pattern. The dismissal of this wrong option implies the pattern functions correctly in Express 4, which is false. The scenario only behaves as described (try/catch catching downstream errors) in Express 5, where next() returns a Promise.

coding-nodejs.note: 18 of 20 cards are factually clean. The event-loop ordering (D,A,C,B), var/let closure, default sort, callback throw, forEach/async, Promise.all parallelism, module-cache mutation, monkey-patch blast radius, memory-leak patterns, and this-binding cards are all correct and internally consistent. The three issues found are: one polarity error (pipe() wrongly marked as wrong), one misleading async-crypto alternative, and one misleading dismissal of a wrong option that conflates Express 4 and Express 5 behavior.

issues.claim: adding per-row overhead. Built-in F.upper() executes entirely on the JVM executor and participates in Catalyst optimizations.
issues.problem: The why text says Python UDFs serialize 'via pickle/Arrow' — Arrow-based serialization applies to pandas_udf (Pandas UDFs), not standard Python UDFs. Standard Python UDFs use cloudpickle for row-by-row serialization between the JVM and the Python worker. Mentioning Arrow here conflates two distinct UDF types and could lead candidates to incorrectly claim that regular Python UDFs benefit from Arrow.
issues.fix: Python UDFs force Spark to serialize each row between the JVM and a Python worker via cloudpickle, adding per-row overhead. Arrow-based batched serialization only applies to pandas_udf. Built-in F.upper() executes entirely on the JVM executor and participates in Catalyst optimizations.

issues.claim: Calling base.cache() (or persist()) after the first action stores the partitions in executor memory so subsequent actions skip the source scan.
issues.problem: The explanation says to call cache() 'after the first action', but that is wrong. cache() must be called BEFORE any action is triggered. In the code shown, the first action is base.count() — if cache() is called after count() completes, that first scan is not cached and only the two later actions benefit. The correct sequence is: call base.cache(), then count() triggers the first read AND materializes the cache, and agg/filter hit the cached data. The correct answer option and the fs both correctly say 'before the first action', making the why text directly contradict the card's own answer.
issues.fix: Without cache(), each action re-executes the full lineage back to S3. Calling base.cache() (or persist()) before any action marks the DataFrame for caching; the first action materializes it into executor memory so all subsequent actions skip the source scan.

coding-pyspark.note: 8 cards audited (all choice-style coding cards; no Q/A/fs/fj-style cards present). Two substantive issues found: a factually wrong 'after the first action' claim in card 4's why text (contradicts both the correct option and the fs), and a misleading 'pickle/Arrow' conflation in card 2 (Arrow applies to pandas_udf, not standard Python UDFs). All other cards — lazy evaluation, broadcast join, skew salting, predicate pushdown, collect OOM, and groupBy vs collect+loop — are factually accurate as of 2026 PySpark 3.x.

issues.claim: Pass a borrow: takes_ownership(&s)
issues.problem: The function signature is `fn takes_ownership(s: String)` — it requires an owned `String`, not a reference. Passing `&s` yields `&String`, not `String`. Deref coercion converts `&String` to `&str`, never to `String`, so this does not compile (E0308: mismatched types). The `why` field compounds the error by claiming 'Borrowing with `&s`... keeps `s` alive for the later `println!`', which is also false for this signature. The only ways to fix the original code are `takes_ownership(s.clone())` (to keep `s` alive) or change the function signature to accept `&str`/`&String`.
issues.fix: Mark this option ok:false with why: "The callee expects an owned String; &s is &String and won't coerce to String — this still fails to compile (E0308)." Mark `{ "t": "Clone the value: takes_ownership(s.clone())", "ok": true }` as the correct option, and update the `why` field to: "Passing `s` by value transfers ownership, leaving `s` invalid (E0382). Because the callee signature demands an owned `String`, the only way to keep `s` alive for `println!` is `s.clone()` — that gives the callee its own copy while `s` retains ownership."

issues.claim: in an FFI or `catch_unwind` boundary the panic becomes undefined behaviour
issues.problem: `catch_unwind` is specifically designed to be safe: it catches an unwinding panic and returns `Err` to the caller without any undefined behaviour. Only unwinding *across* an FFI boundary is UB. Equating `catch_unwind` with FFI in a UB claim is factually wrong and misleads learners into thinking `catch_unwind` is unsafe to use.
issues.fix: in an FFI boundary the panic becomes undefined behaviour (unwinding across FFI is UB); `catch_unwind` is the safe, intentional mechanism for catching panics within Rust

coding-rust.note: 20 cards audited (all choice-kind). Two confirmed issues: (1) Card 1 marks 'Pass a borrow: takes_ownership(&s)' as the correct fix, but the callee signature requires an owned String — passing &String is a type mismatch (E0308), not a borrow-checker fix. The real fix is s.clone(). (2) Card 18's 'why' field conflates FFI unwinding (UB) with catch_unwind (safe and explicitly designed for panic recovery). All other cards — ownership/move semantics, borrow-checker error codes, iterator laziness, Rc/Arc/RefCell behavior, async blocking, tokio mutex, reference cycles, unsafe transmute, semver trait changes, and global allocator scoping — are factually correct as of 2026.

issues.claim: Why does == return false here?
issues.problem: The question asks why == returns false, but the correct answer is (A) false, (B) true — meaning == returns TRUE, not false. The interesting result is that eq returns false (reference identity). The question title directly contradicts the correct answer option.
issues.fix: Why does eq return false but == return true here?

issues.claim: constrain T with an upper bound like T: Ordering to make it obvious at the call site
issues.problem: T: Ordering is Scala context bound syntax (syntactic sugar for an implicit parameter implicit ord: Ordering[T]), not an upper bound. An upper bound is T <: SomeType. Conflating these two distinct type system concepts is a factual error that would mislead a learner studying type constraints.
issues.fix: constrain T with a context bound like T: Ordering to make it obvious at the call site

issues.claim: The caller's EC context (e.g., Akka dispatcher, Cats ContextShift) is ignored.
issues.problem: Cats ContextShift was removed in Cats Effect 3 (released 2021) and replaced with IORuntime / Dispatcher. By 2026 CE3 is the standard; citing ContextShift as a live API is outdated and will mislead candidates who look it up.
issues.fix: The caller's EC context (e.g., Akka dispatcher, Cats Effect IORuntime) is ignored.

coding-scala.note: 20 cards audited across Jr/Mid/Sr/Staff/Principal levels. Three issues found: one wrong question framing (card 2 says == returns false when it actually returns true), one wrong type-system terminology (card 16 calls a context bound an upper bound), and one outdated API reference (card 18 cites Cats ContextShift which was removed in CE3/2021). All other cards — Option.get behavior, val capture semantics, lazy val duplicate detection, map vs flatMap nesting, sealed exhaustiveness, overload ambiguity, Future eagerness, implicit Ordering shadowing, Iterator laziness, data race, Spark closure serialization, Array in case class hash contract, and AnyVal value-class allocation — are factually correct and their options/polarity are sound.

issues.claim: //  ^ TS error: possibly null
issues.problem: Since TypeScript 4.4 (August 2021), narrowing is preserved for function parameters captured by closures. The code as written — a parameter guarded before the setTimeout — compiles without error in TS 4.4+ (confirmed in TS 6.0.3 with --strict). The scenario never reproduces the described error. The "correct" answer ("capture val in a const") and the entire why text describe pre-4.4 behavior that no longer exists.
issues.fix: Rewrite the scenario to use a `let` variable that is reassigned after the guard, which DOES lose narrowing in modern TS: add `val = Math.random() > 0.5 ? null : val;` after the guard. Then update the why to explain that mutable bindings reassigned after the guard cause the narrowing loss — const captures the pre-reassignment value.

issues.claim: Fix with a default: `function wrap<T = unknown>(val: T)`
issues.problem: A default type parameter only applies when T cannot be inferred from the call site. When the caller passes `[]`, TypeScript DOES infer T (as never[]) from the argument — the default `= unknown` is ignored entirely. Verified: `function wrap<T = unknown>(val: T) { return { value: val }; }; const c = wrap([]); c.value.push(1)` still errors with 'Argument of type 1 is not assignable to parameter of type never'. The default-type-parameter suggestion is a false fix.
issues.fix: Remove the `function wrap<T = unknown>(val: T)` option. The only working fix is explicit annotation at the call site: `wrap<number[]>([])`. Alternatively, add an overload: `function wrap<T>(val: T[]): { value: T[] }` so array-typed calls infer correctly.

issues.claim: This means `{ onData(x: number): void }` would also satisfy `Handler`, even though the caller always passes strings — the number-only handler would receive a string and could fail silently.
issues.problem: The claim is factually wrong. `number` and `string` are unrelated primitive types — neither is a structural subtype of the other.
